topics.title: Antenatal Care And Booking
topics.html:
Definition

Antenatal care is the structured care pathway from confirmation of pregnancy to delivery. The booking visit establishes risk, performs initial screening, and initiates a care plan. Continuity of care and timely screening improve outcomes.

Core Clinical Content

First contact

GPs/booking visit ideally by 10 weeks; comprehensive history and examination

Confirm pregnancy

Urine β-hCG; dating by last menstrual period (LMP); first-trimester USS for crown-rump length (CRL)

History

Past obstetric (mode of delivery, complications, GDM/PE); past medical; family (thrombosis, diabetes); social; mental health

Examination

BMI, BP, urine, breast; abdominal examination later

First-trimester screening

Combined test (nuchal translucency + β-hCG + PAPP-A) at 11-13+6 weeks; cfDNA for aneuploidy if high risk

Booking bloods

FBC, blood group + antibody screen, haemoglobinopathy, HIV, syphilis, hepatitis B, rubella, MSU

Anomaly scan

18-20+6 weeks; structural survey; soft markers

Fetal growth

Serial symphyseal fundal height (SFH); third-trimester growth scans if risk factors

Visits

Nulliparous — 10 visits; parous — 7 visits; schedule depends on risk

Lifestyle

Smoking cessation; alcohol/illicit drug avoidance; diet (listeria); exercise; work adjustments

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
First contactGPs/booking visit ideally by 10 weeks; comprehensive history and examination

LifestyleSmoking cessation; alcohol/illicit drug avoidance; diet (listeria); exercise; work adjustments

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Antenatal care and booking?

Antenatal care is the structured care pathway from confirmation of pregnancy to delivery. The booking visit establishes risk, performs initial screening, and initiates a care plan. Continuity of care and timely screening improve outcomes.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Antenatal care and booking?

Key features include: First contact, Confirm pregnancy, History, Examination, First-trimester screening. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Antenatal care and booking diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Antenatal care and booking?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Antenatal care and booking?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Antenatal care and booking — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Antenatal Screening For Aneuploidy And Structural
topics.html:
Definition

Antenatal screening aims to identify pregnancies at risk of fetal aneuploidy (trisomy 21, 18, 13) and structural anomalies. Options include first-trimester combined test, second-trimester serum screening, cell-free DNA testing, and detailed ultrasound.

Core Clinical Content

First-trimester combined test

NT + β-hCG + PAPP-A at 11-13+6 weeks; detection ~85% trisomy 21 at 5% false positive

Cell-free DNA (NIPT/NIPS)

Detects fetal DNA in maternal plasma; detection >99% trisomy 21; <0.1% false positive; not diagnostic

Quadruple test

Second-trimester if booking late; AFP, hCG, uE3, inhibin A; 80% detection

Integrated test

Combined first + second trimester markers; high detection ~95%

Soft markers on USS

Nuchal fold, echogenic bowel, echogenic focus, mild pyelectasis, single umbilical artery

Anomaly scan timing

18-20+6 weeks; structural survey; cardiac outflow tracts; CNS; spine; limbs; face

Diagnostic testing

CVS (11-14 weeks) or amniocentesis (15+ weeks) for karyotype/microarray; 0.5-1% miscarriage risk

Non-invasive options

NIPT for sex chromosome and microdeletion screening — patient-led decision

Counselling

Pre-test: limitations, options; post-test: confirmatory testing, decision-making, choices

Pre-eclampsia screening

First-trimester combined model (maternal factors + MAP + PAPP-A + PlGF) — aspirin prophylaxis

Q1. What is the definition of Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?

Antenatal screening aims to identify pregnancies at risk of fetal aneuploidy (trisomy 21, 18, 13) and structural anomalies. Options include first-trimester combined test, second-trimester serum screening, cell-free DNA testing, and detailed ultrasound.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?

Key features include: First-trimester combined test, Cell-free DNA (NIPT/NIPS), Quadruple test, Integrated test, Soft markers on USS. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Antenatal screening for aneuploidy and structural anomalies?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

SBAs

  1. NT + β-hCG + PAPP-A at 11-13+6 weeks; detection ~85% trisomy 21 at 5% false positive
  2. Detects fetal DNA in maternal plasma; detection >99% trisomy 21; <0.1% false positive; not diagnostic
  3. Second-trimester if booking late; AFP, hCG, uE3, inhibin A; 80% detection
  4. Combined first + second trimester markers; high detection ~95%
  5. Nuchal fold, echogenic bowel, echogenic focus, mild pyelectasis, single umbilical artery
  6. 18-20+6 weeks; structural survey; cardiac outflow tracts; CNS; spine; limbs; face
  7. CVS (11-14 weeks) or amniocentesis (15+ weeks) for karyotype/microarray; 0.5-1% miscarriage risk
  8. NIPT for sex chromosome and microdeletion screening — patient-led decision
  9. Pre-test: limitations, options; post-test: confirmatory testing, decision-making, choices
  10. First-trimester combined model (maternal factors + MAP + PAPP-A + PlGF) — aspirin prophylaxis

topics.title: Chronic Hypertension In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Chronic hypertension in pregnancy is defined as blood pressure ≥140/90 mmHg confirmed on two occasions before 20 weeks' gestation, or pre-existing antihypertensive use, or hypertension persisting beyond 12 weeks postpartum. It affects 1-5% of pregnancies and increases maternal and fetal risk.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

BP ≥140/90 mmHg before 20 weeks OR persisting >12 weeks postpartum

Prevalence

1-5% of pregnancies; rising with maternal age and obesity

Causes

Essential (90%); secondary — renal, endocrine (Conn, phaeo), coarctation

Risks to mother

Superimposed pre-eclampsia (25%); placental abruption; stroke; CKD progression

Risks to fetus

IUGR (2x); preterm birth; stillbirth; placental dysfunction

Preconception

Switch ACEI/ARB to labetalol/nifedipine/methyldopa; optimise BP <130/80

BP target

Treat if >140/90 (CHAP trial) or >150/100; target diastolic 80-90 mmHg

First-line drugs

Labetalol, nifedipine MR, methyldopa — all pregnancy-safe

Avoid

ACE inhibitors (2nd/3rd trimester — renal dysgenesis, oligohydramnios); ARBs; thiazides

Aspirin 75-150 mg

From 12 weeks for pre-eclampsia prevention if ≥1 high-risk factor

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Chronic hypertension in pregnancy?

Chronic hypertension in pregnancy is defined as blood pressure ≥140/90 mmHg confirmed on two occasions before 20 weeks' gestation, or pre-existing antihypertensive use, or hypertension persisting beyond 12 weeks postpartum. It affects 1-5% of pregnancies and increases maternal and fetal risk.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Chronic hypertension in pregnancy?

Key features include: Definition, Prevalence, Causes, Risks to mother, Risks to fetus. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Chronic hypertension in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Chronic hypertension in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Chronic hypertension in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Chronic hypertension in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Eclampsia
topics.html:
Definition

Eclampsia is the occurrence of new-onset generalised tonic-clonic seizures in a woman with pre-eclampsia. It is a life-threatening obstetric emergency requiring immediate stabilisation, magnesium sulphate, control of severe hypertension, and delivery.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Generalised seizures in PE/pregnancy; excludes other causes

Timing

Antepartum (50%); intrapartum (25%); postpartum (25%, often within 48 h)

Risk factors

Severe PE; nulliparity; multiple pregnancy; pre-existing HTN; renal disease

Differential

Epilepsy; cerebral venous sinus thrombosis; stroke; SAH; encephalitis; metabolic

Immediate management

ABC; left lateral tilt; oxygen; IV access; stop fitting

Magnesium sulphate

4-6 g IV loading over 15-20 min; 1-2 g/h maintenance; 24 h post-delivery or post last fit

Recurrent fits

Further 2 g IV bolus; if persists, consider diazepam or phenytoin

BP control

Labetalol IV or hydralazine IV; target <160/110

Definitive treatment

Delivery once stabilised; mode per obstetric indications

Complications

Maternal: stroke, ARDS, DIC, HELLP, abruptio. Fetal: prematurity, asphyxia, death

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Gestational Hypertension
topics.html:
Definition

Gestational hypertension is new-onset BP ≥140/90 mmHg after 20 weeks' gestation in a previously normotensive woman, without proteinuria or other features of pre-eclampsia. It usually resolves within 6 weeks postpartum but ~25% progress to pre-eclampsia.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

New hypertension after 20 weeks; no proteinuria; no end-organ involvement

Onset

After 20 weeks' gestation; usually third trimester

Prevalence

6-17% of pregnancies; rising

Progress to pre-eclampsia

~25% develop proteinuria / end-organ dysfunction

Risk factors

Nulliparity, age >35, BMI >30, multiple pregnancy, family history, prior GH/PE

Differential

Chronic HTN, pre-eclampsia, white-coat HTN (ABPM)

Investigations

Urine PCR; bloods (FBC, U&E, LFT, urate); growth scans; umbilical artery Doppler

Management

Watchful waiting for mild (<150/100); antihypertensives for moderate/severe

Delivery planning

Deliver by 37 weeks if BP controlled; severe features warrant earlier delivery

Postpartum

BP usually normalises by 6 weeks; ongoing surveillance; CV risk in later life

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Gestational hypertension?

Gestational hypertension is new-onset BP ≥140/90 mmHg after 20 weeks' gestation in a previously normotensive woman, without proteinuria or other features of pre-eclampsia. It usually resolves within 6 weeks postpartum but ~25% progress to pre-eclampsia.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Gestational hypertension?

Key features include: Definition, Onset, Prevalence, Progress to pre-eclampsia, Risk factors. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Gestational hypertension diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Gestational hypertension?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Gestational hypertension?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Gestational hypertension — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Hellp Syndrome
topics.html:
Definition

HELLP syndrome (Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) is a severe variant of pre-eclampsia characterised by microangiopathic haemolysis, hepatic dysfunction, and thrombocytopenia. It carries high maternal and fetal mortality and is a medical emergency.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Haemolysis + Elevated Liver enzymes (AST/ALT >70) + Low Platelets (<100 × 10^9/L)

Epidemiology

Affects 0.5-0.9% of pregnancies; 10-20% of severe PE

Onset

Usually 27-37 weeks; 30% postpartum

Symptoms

RUQ/epigastric pain, nausea, vomiting, headache, visual disturbance, oedema

Diagnosis

Blood film (schistocytes, helmet cells); ↑LDH; ↑AST/ALT; ↓platelets; ↑bilirubin

Differential

TMA — TTP (ADAMTS13 <10%), HUS, AFLP, ITP, acute fatty liver

Management

Stabilise; magnesium sulphate; control BP; transfer to tertiary unit; corticosteroids if <34 weeks

Delivery

Definitive treatment; mode based on cervical favourability and severity

Postpartum

May worsen 24-48 h post-delivery; monitor platelets, LFT, LDH, creatinine

Long-term

Increased CV risk; recurrence risk 5-20%; avoid oestrogen contraception

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Pre Eclampsia
topics.html:
Definition

Pre-eclampsia is a multi-system disorder of pregnancy characterised by new-onset hypertension after 20 weeks with proteinuria and/or end-organ dysfunction (renal, hepatic, haematological, neurological, pulmonary, or fetal). It is a leading cause of maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality worldwide.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

BP ≥140/90 + proteinuria (≥300 mg/24h or PCR ≥30) OR end-organ involvement after 20 weeks

Severe features

BP ≥160/110; proteinuria >5 g/24h; PLT <100; ↑LFT 2x; creatinine >110; pulmonary oedema; neurological symptoms

Epidemiology

Affects 2-8% of pregnancies; leading cause of maternal death in LMIC

Pathophysiology

Abnormal placentation → defective spiral artery remodelling → endothelial dysfunction, anti-angiogenic state (↑sFlt-1, ↓PlGF)

Risk factors

Prior PE (strongest); chronic HTN; renal disease; APS; SLE; diabetes; obesity; multiple pregnancy; advanced maternal age; nulliparity

Prevention

Aspirin 75-150 mg nocte from 12 weeks for high-risk; calcium in low-intake populations

Diagnosis

New HTN after 20 weeks + proteinuria OR severe features; sFlt-1/PlGF ratio aids rule-out

Management

Definitive = delivery; magnesium sulphate for severe features; antihypertensives (labetalol IV, hydralazine, nifedipine)

HELLP syndrome

Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets — variant of severe PE

Eclampsia

Seizures in PE/gestational HTN — give MgSO4; supportive care

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DefinitionBP ≥140/90 + proteinuria (≥300 mg/24h or PCR ≥30) OR end-organ involvement after 20 weeks
Severe featuresBP ≥160/110; proteinuria >5 g/24h; PLT <100; ↑LFT 2x; creatinine >110; pulmonary oedema; neurological symptoms
EpidemiologyAffects 2-8% of pregnancies; leading cause of maternal death in LMIC
PathophysiologyAbnormal placentation → defective spiral artery remodelling → endothelial dysfunction, anti-angiogenic state (↑sFlt-1, ↓PlGF)
Risk factorsPrior PE (strongest); chronic HTN; renal disease; APS; SLE; diabetes; obesity; multiple pregnancy; advanced maternal age; nulliparity
PreventionAspirin 75-150 mg nocte from 12 weeks for high-risk; calcium in low-intake populations
DiagnosisNew HTN after 20 weeks + proteinuria OR severe features; sFlt-1/PlGF ratio aids rule-out
ManagementDefinitive = delivery; magnesium sulphate for severe features; antihypertensives (labetalol IV, hydralazine, nifedipine)
HELLP syndromeHaemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets — variant of severe PE
EclampsiaSeizures in PE/gestational HTN — give MgSO4; supportive care

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Pre-eclampsia?

Pre-eclampsia is a multi-system disorder of pregnancy characterised by new-onset hypertension after 20 weeks with proteinuria and/or end-organ dysfunction (renal, hepatic, haematological, neurological, pulmonary, or fetal). It is a leading cause of maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality worldwide.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Pre-eclampsia?

Key features include: Definition, Severe features, Epidemiology, Pathophysiology, Risk factors. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Pre-eclampsia diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Pre-eclampsia?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Pre-eclampsia?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Pre-eclampsia — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Covid 19 In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

COVID-19 in pregnancy generally causes mild disease, but pregnancy is a risk factor for severe disease, hospitalisation, ICU admission, and preterm birth. Vaccination in pregnancy is safe and effective; monoclonal antibodies and antivirals reduce progression in high-risk women.

Core Clinical Content

Severity in pregnancy

Increased risk severe disease, ICU admission, ventilation, death vs non-pregnant — especially 3rd trimester

Maternal complications

Pre-eclampsia; thromboembolism; preterm birth; caesarean; maternal death

Fetal risks

Miscarriage (1st trimester severe disease); stillbirth; IUGR; prematurity; vertical transmission possible

Vaccination

mRNA vaccines (Pfizer/Moderna) safe and effective in any trimester; recommended

Treatment

Antivirals (nirmatrelvir/ritonavir, remdesivir, molnupiravir); monoclonal antibodies (sotrovimab, tixagevimab) for high-risk

Antenatal care

Continue routine care; consider remote consultations; postpone if mild; safety-netting

Thromboprophylaxis

VTE risk high; LMWH for hospitalised; consider 10-14 days postnatal

Delivery

Mode per obstetric indication; vaginal acceptable if stable; preterm delivery only if deteriorating

Neonatal

Vertical transmission 1-2%; routine swabbing; breastfeeding encouraged with hygiene

Postnatal

Vaccination; mental health; post-COVID/long-COVID clinics

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Group B Streptococcus Gbs In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Group B Streptococcus (GBS, Streptococcus agalactiae) is a leading cause of early-onset neonatal sepsis (EONS), pneumonia, and meningitis. Vertical transmission occurs during labour from colonised mothers. Universal screening at 35-37 weeks with intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (IAP) for at-risk women reduces early-onset disease by 80%.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

Maternal carriage 20-30% (rectovaginal); vertical transmission 50% without prophylaxis; EONS 1 per 1000 in colonised

Risk factors for EONS

Previous infant with invasive GBS; GBS bacteriuria in current pregnancy; positive GBS screen at 35-37 weeks; intrapartum fever; prolonged ROM ≥18 h; preterm labour <37 weeks

Screening

Vaginal-rectal swab at 35-37 weeks; culture in selective broth; rapid intrapartum testing if needed (less sensitive)

UK approach

Risk-based: IAP if previous infant GBS disease, GBS bacteriuria, or preterm labour/prolonged ROM

US approach

Universal screening at 36-37 weeks; IAP for all positive

Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (IAP)

Benzylpenicillin 3 g IV loading then 1.5 g 4-hourly until delivery; alternative ampicillin, cefazolin, or clindamycin (if allergic)

Timing

At least 4 h of antibiotics before delivery; reduces EONS by 80%

Preterm labour

IAP if unknown GBS status or positive; intrapartum antibiotics for all

Complications

Neonatal sepsis (early-onset <7 days, late-onset 7-90 days); pneumonia; meningitis; 5-10% mortality

Postpartum

Maternal sepsis (rare); secondary prevention for future pregnancy; no vaccine available

Universal screening at 35-37 weeks with intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis (IAP) for at-risk women reduces early-onset disease by 80%.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Group B Streptococcus (GBS) in pregnancy?

Key features include: Epidemiology, Risk factors for EONS, Screening, UK approach, US approach. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Group B Streptococcus (GBS) in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Group B Streptococcus (GBS) in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Hiv In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

HIV in pregnancy requires combined obstetric, HIV, and paediatric care to prevent vertical transmission. With effective combination antiretroviral therapy (cART), undetectable viral load, and appropriate delivery/feeding, the transmission rate falls below 0.5%.

Core Clinical Content

Vertical transmission rate

Without intervention 15-45%; with cART <0.5% (undetectable VL at delivery)

Antenatal cART

All women regardless of CD4; tenofovir + emtricitabine + dolutegravir (1st trimester acceptable)

Monitoring

CD4; viral load at booking, 28, 36 weeks, 4-6 weeks post-cART change; resistance if detectable VL

Viral load at delivery

If <50 copies/mL — vaginal delivery acceptable; if >400 — planned caesarean at 38 weeks

Intrapartum

Avoid invasive procedures; IV zidovudine if VL >1000; no artificial rupture of membranes; avoid instrumental

Neonatal prophylaxis

Zidovudine for 4 weeks; if maternal VL >1000 — add nevirapine or raltegravir 3-drug regimen for 4-6 weeks

Breastfeeding

Avoid in high-income settings; safe with cART in low-income (WHO)

Maternal health

Continue cART; opportunistic infection prophylaxis if CD4 <200

Preconception

Optimise cART; consider pre-exposure prophylaxis for partner; partner testing

Postpartum

Continue cART; contraception; viral load 4-6 weeks; review resistance if failing

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

HIV in pregnancy requires combined obstetric, HIV, and paediatric care to prevent vertical transmission. With effective combination antiretroviral therapy (cART), undetectable viral load, and appropriate delivery/feeding, the transmission rate falls below 0.5%.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of HIV in pregnancy?

Key features include: Vertical transmission rate, Antenatal cART, Monitoring, Viral load at delivery, Intrapartum. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is HIV in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for HIV in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of HIV in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

SBAs

  1. Without intervention 15-45%; with cART <0.5% (undetectable VL at delivery)
  2. All women regardless of CD4; tenofovir + emtricitabine + dolutegravir (1st trimester acceptable)
  3. CD4; viral load at booking, 28, 36 weeks, 4-6 weeks post-cART change; resistance if detectable VL
  4. If <50 copies/mL — vaginal delivery acceptable; if >400 — planned caesarean at 38 weeks
  5. Avoid invasive procedures; IV zidovudine if VL >1000; no artificial rupture of membranes; avoid instrumental
  6. Zidovudine for 4 weeks; if maternal VL >1000 — add nevirapine or raltegravir 3-drug regimen for 4-6 weeks
  7. Avoid in high-income settings; safe with cART in low-income (WHO)
  8. Continue cART; opportunistic infection prophylaxis if CD4 <200
  9. Optimise cART; consider pre-exposure prophylaxis for partner; partner testing
  10. Continue cART; contraception; viral load 4-6 weeks; review resistance if failing

topics.title: Hepatitis B In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Hepatitis B in pregnancy can cause vertical transmission (90% in HBeAg-positive mothers without intervention), leading to chronic infection in 90% of infected infants. Antenatal screening, maternal viral load monitoring, and neonatal immunoprophylaxis are highly effective.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

0.5-2% in UK; up to 10-20% in endemic regions (Asia, Africa)

Vertical transmission risk

90% HBeAg+; 10-20% HBeAg- without prophylaxis

Screening

Universal at booking; HBsAg, anti-HBs, anti-HBc; if HBsAg+ — HBeAg, anti-HBe, HBV DNA

Maternal management

Tenofovir from 28 weeks if HBV DNA >200,000 IU/mL or HBeAg+; monitor LFT

Mode of delivery

Vaginal acceptable with prophylaxis; caesarean does not prevent transmission if immunoprophylaxis given

Neonatal prophylaxis

Hepatitis B vaccine + hepatitis B immunoglobulin (HBIG) within 12 hours of birth — 90% effective

Follow-up

Anti-HBs at 12 months; HBsAg and anti-HBc at 12-18 months to confirm status

Breastfeeding

Safe with prophylaxis; not contraindicated

Acute HBV in pregnancy

Severe hepatitis risk; tenofovir; consider for fulminant disease

Hepatitis C

No vertical prevention; avoid invasive procedures; treat with DAAs postpartum

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Hepatitis B in pregnancy?

Hepatitis B in pregnancy can cause vertical transmission (90% in HBeAg-positive mothers without intervention), leading to chronic infection in 90% of infected infants. Antenatal screening, maternal viral load monitoring, and neonatal immunoprophylaxis are highly effective.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Hepatitis B in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Vertical transmission risk, Screening, Maternal management, Mode of delivery. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Hepatitis B in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Hepatitis B in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Hepatitis B in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Hepatitis B in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Influenza In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Influenza in pregnancy causes disproportionately severe disease compared to non-pregnant women, with increased risk of hospitalisation, ICU admission, and death, especially in the 2nd and 3rd trimesters. Inactivated influenza vaccination in any trimester is safe and effective. Antiviral treatment (oseltamivir) is recommended within 48 hours of symptoms.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

Pregnancy increases severity (3rd trimester peak); influenza in pregnancy causes excess mortality historically (1918, 2009 H1N1)

Risk factors

Asthma; obesity; diabetes; immunocompromised; cardiac disease; advanced gestation

Complications maternal

Pneumonia; ARDS; hospitalisation; ICU; death

Complications fetal

Preterm birth; stillbirth; IUGR; congenital infection (rare); neonatal influenza

Presentation

Fever, myalgia, headache, cough, sore throat, fatigue; abrupt onset

Diagnosis

Clinical; rapid antigen testing; PCR (respiratory); CXR if pneumonia suspected

Treatment

Oseltamivir 75 mg BD 5 days within 48 h of symptoms (any trimester); benefits likely if later; supportive care

Chemoprophylaxis

Oseltamivir for close contacts in high-risk; post-exposure; vaccination reduces risk

Vaccination

Inactivated influenza vaccine (any trimester; safe); recommended in pregnancy; protects mother and neonate

Prevention

Annual vaccination; hand hygiene; avoid sick contacts; mask in outbreaks

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

  7. Oseltamivir 75 mg BD 5 days within 48 h of symptoms (any trimester); benefits likely if later; supportive care
  8. Oseltamivir for close contacts in high-risk; post-exposure; vaccination reduces risk
  9. Inactivated influenza vaccine (any trimester; safe); recommended in pregnancy; protects mother and neonate
  10. Annual vaccination; hand hygiene; avoid sick contacts; mask in outbreaks

topics.title: Malaria In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Malaria in pregnancy is a major cause of maternal and fetal morbidity in endemic regions (sub-Saharan Africa, Asia). Placental sequestration of Plasmodium falciparum causes severe anaemia, low birth weight, and increased stillbirth and neonatal mortality. Prevention with intermittent preventive treatment (IPT) and insecticide-treated nets is essential.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

Endemic in 90+ countries; 30 million pregnancies at risk annually; 10,000 maternal deaths/year

Pathophysiology

P. falciparum expresses VAR2CSA on RBCs; binds CSA on placenta; sequestration → placental malaria

Complications maternal

Severe anaemia; hypoglycaemia; ARDS; cerebral malaria; AKI; death; pre-existing immunity reduced in pregnancy

Complications fetal

Low birth weight (most common); IUGR; preterm; stillbirth; neonatal malaria; congenital malaria

Diagnosis

Thick and thin blood films; rapid diagnostic test (RDT); PCR for species

Treatment uncomplicated (P. falciparum)

Quinine + clindamycin (1st trimester); ACT (artemether-lumefantrine) 2nd/3rd trimesters

Treatment severe

IV artesunate (all trimesters); exchange transfusion; ITU; supportive

P. vivax/ovale

Chloroquine; avoid primaquine in pregnancy (G6PD deficiency and foetal haemolysis); radical cure postpartum

Prevention

IPTp with sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine (2-3 doses after 1st trimester; not in HIV on cotrimoxazole); insecticide-treated nets; mefloquine prophylaxis (if resistant)

Postpartum

Treat vivax/ovale; contraception; preconception counselling; HIV/screening

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Malaria in pregnancy?

Malaria in pregnancy is a major cause of maternal and fetal morbidity in endemic regions (sub-Saharan Africa, Asia). Placental sequestration of Plasmodium falciparum causes severe anaemia, low birth weight, and increased stillbirth and neonatal mortality. Prevention with intermittent preventive treatment (IPT) and insecticide-treated nets is essential.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Malaria in pregnancy?

Key features include: Epidemiology, Pathophysiology, Complications maternal, Complications fetal, Diagnosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Malaria in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Malaria in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Malaria in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Torch Infections In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

TORCH infections (Toxoplasma, Other, Rubella, CMV, Herpes/HIV) and others (syphilis, parvovirus B19, varicella, listeria) can cause congenital infection with fetal loss, malformation, or long-term sequelae. Antenatal screening and prevention are key.

Core Clinical Content

Toxoplasmosis

Cat faeces, undercooked meat; hydrocephalus, intracranial calcifications, chorioretinitis; spiramycin ± pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine after 1st trimester

Rubella

Vaccine-preventable; CRS — cataracts, sensorineural deafness, PDA; offer post-partum vaccination

CMV

Most common congenital infection; sensorineural hearing loss, microcephaly, periventricular calcifications; no proven treatment

Herpes simplex

Primary genital herpes near delivery → caesarean; recurrent — discussion; aciclovir safe

Parvovirus B19

Hydrops fetalis; aplastic crisis in mother; serology + MCA Doppler

Varicella

Pneumonitis in adult; fetal varicella syndrome (<20 weeks); shingles immunoglobulin to neonate if maternal rash 5 days before to 2 days after delivery

Syphilis

Routine screen; penicillin treatment; desensitize if allergic; Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction

Listeria

Unpasteurised cheese, deli meats; granulomatosis infantiseptica; ampicillin + gentamicin

Zika

Microcephaly; travel avoidance; semen transmission 6 months; no sexual partners from endemic areas

Screening

Rubella, syphilis, HIV, hepatitis B — all antenatal; varicella, CMV, toxoplasma if symptomatic

Q1. What is the definition of TORCH infections in pregnancy?

TORCH infections (Toxoplasma, Other, Rubella, CMV, Herpes/HIV) and others (syphilis, parvovirus B19, varicella, listeria) can cause congenital infection with fetal loss, malformation, or long-term sequelae. Antenatal screening and prevention are key.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of TORCH infections in pregnancy?

Key features include: Toxoplasmosis, Rubella, CMV, Herpes simplex, Parvovirus B19. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is TORCH infections in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for TORCH infections in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of TORCH infections in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Q: Toxoplasmosis?

A: Cat faeces, undercooked meat; hydrocephalus, intracranial calcifications, chorioretinitis; spiramycin ± pyrimethamine/sulfadiazine after 1st trimester

Q: Rubella?

A: Vaccine-preventable; CRS — cataracts, sensorineural deafness, PDA; offer post-partum vaccination

Q: CMV?

A: Most common congenital infection; sensorineural hearing loss, microcephaly, periventricular calcifications; no proven treatment

Q: Herpes simplex?

A: Primary genital herpes near delivery → caesarean; recurrent — discussion; aciclovir safe

Q: Parvovirus B19?

Q: Varicella?

Q: Syphilis?

A: Routine screen; penicillin treatment; desensitize if allergic; Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction

Q: Listeria?

A: Unpasteurised cheese, deli meats; granulomatosis infantiseptica; ampicillin + gentamicin

topics.title: Varicella Zoster In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Varicella zoster virus (VZV) in pregnancy causes significant maternal and fetal morbidity. Maternal infection in the first 20 weeks can cause fetal varicella syndrome (limb hypoplasia, microcephaly, skin scarring, ocular abnormalities). Perinatal infection (5 days before to 2 days after delivery) risks severe neonatal varicella. VZ immunoglobulin (VZIG) and aciclovir are key interventions.

Core Clinical Content

Maternal varicella

Pneumonia (10-20%) — leading cause of death; hospitalisation; ARDS; treat with aciclovir

Fetal varicella syndrome (FVS)

<2% if <20 weeks; cutaneous scarring; limb hypoplasia; microcephaly; ocular (cataract, chorioretinitis); IUGR

Neonatal varicella

Severe if maternal rash 5 days before to 2 days after delivery (no time for IgG transfer); mortality 30% without treatment

VZIG

For susceptible (VZV IgG negative) exposed pregnant women within 96 h; reduces severity; does not prevent infection

Aciclovir

800 mg 5x daily 7 days if varicella in pregnancy (within 24 h of rash); IV if severe or pneumonitis

Diagnosis

Clinical; PCR vesicular fluid; serology (VZV IgG/IgM); CXR if pneumonitis

Exposure in pregnancy

Check VZV IgG status urgently (most adults immune); if non-immune and significant exposure, VZIG

Vaccination

Live vaccine (Varilrix, Varivax) — contraindicated in pregnancy; advise avoidance; 2 doses 4-8 weeks apart; avoid pregnancy 1 month after

Zoster (shingles)

Less risk to fetus (cell-mediated); aciclovir if severe

Postpartum

VZIG to neonate if maternal rash 5 days before to 2 days after; isolate mother and baby; aciclovir for baby

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Varicella zoster in pregnancy?

Varicella zoster virus (VZV) in pregnancy causes significant maternal and fetal morbidity. Maternal infection in the first 20 weeks can cause fetal varicella syndrome (limb hypoplasia, microcephaly, skin scarring, ocular abnormalities). Perinatal infection (5 days before to 2 days after delivery) risks severe neonatal varicella. VZ immunoglobulin (VZIG) and aciclovir are key interventions.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Varicella zoster in pregnancy?

Key features include: Maternal varicella, Fetal varicella syndrome (FVS), Neonatal varicella, VZIG, Aciclovir. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Varicella zoster in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Varicella zoster in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Varicella zoster in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Amniotic Fluid Embolism Afe
topics.html:
Definition

Amniotic fluid embolism is a rare but catastrophic obstetric emergency in which amniotic fluid, fetal cells, or debris enter the maternal circulation causing anaphylactoid reaction, cardiovascular collapse, respiratory distress, DIC, and often death. Despite resuscitation, mortality is 20-60%.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Anaphylactoid reaction to amniotic fluid/debris in maternal circulation

Epidemiology

1-2 in 40,000 deliveries; mortality 20-60%; leading cause of maternal death in some series

Risk factors

Older age; multiparity; caesarean; induction; multiple pregnancy; placenta praevia; abruption; uterine rupture

Classic triad

Sudden hypoxia and respiratory distress; shock/hypotension; DIC/bleeding — often with seizures

Pathophysiology

Amniotic fluid → pulmonary vasculature → anaphylactoid reaction, ARDS, cardiac failure, DIC

Differential

PE; eclampsia; aortic dissection; anaphylaxis; sepsis; haemorrhage; myocardial infarction

Management

ABC; high-quality CPR; oxygen 100%; immediate delivery; ITU; massive transfusion; supportive

Monitoring

Arterial line; CVP; urinary catheter; ABG; coagulation; echo

Complications

ARDS; AKI; DIC; HIE; death; neurological sequelae in survivors

Survivors

May need hysterectomy; psychological support; bereavement care

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE)?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE)?

topics.title: Caesarean Section
topics.html:
Definition

Caesarean section is a surgical procedure for delivery through an abdominal and uterine incision. Rates are rising globally; Robson classification allows comparison. Indications include fetal distress, malpresentation, placenta praevia, previous classical section, and maternal request.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

20-30% in developed countries; up to 50%+ in some regions; WHO ideal 10-15%

Classification

Elective (planned) vs emergency (category 1-4); Robson classification (10 groups)

Indications

Fetal distress; malpresentation; previous classical section; placenta praevia; multiple pregnancy; failure to progress; maternal request

Pre-op

Anaesthetic review; consent; crossmatch; thromboprophylaxis; antibiotic prophylaxis; bladder catheter; WHO checklist

Anaesthesia

Spinal (most common for elective); epidural top-up for emergency; GA for emergencies or coagulopathy

Surgical technique

Pfannenstiel or low midline; lower segment transverse incision; peritoneal closure debated

Complications

Haemorrhage; infection (5-10%); wound haematoma; urinary tract injury; VTE; Hysterectomy rare

Post-op

Early mobilisation; VTE prophylaxis; regular analgesia; wound care; remove catheter early

Recovery

2-3 days hospital; 6 weeks postnatal review; driving after 4-6 weeks; contraception discussion

Subsequent pregnancy

Vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC) — 60-80% success; risk of uterine rupture 0.5%

Caesarean section is a surgical procedure for delivery through an abdominal and uterine incision. Rates are rising globally; Robson classification allows comparison. Indications include fetal distress, malpresentation, placenta praevia, previous classical section, and maternal request.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Caesarean section?

Key features include: Incidence, Classification, Indications, Pre-op, Anaesthesia. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Caesarean section diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Caesarean section?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Caesarean section?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Fetal Monitoring In Labour
topics.html:
Definition

Fetal monitoring in labour detects fetal hypoxia/acidosis to allow timely intervention (operative delivery) and prevent perinatal death and long-term neurological injury. Intermittent auscultation (low risk) and continuous electronic fetal monitoring (CTGs) are used. CTG interpretation requires pattern recognition and adherence to guidelines (NICE).

Core Clinical Content

Methods

Intermittent auscultation (low-risk labours); continuous electronic fetal monitoring (CTGs) for high-risk; fetal scalp electrode if needed

Indications for continuous CTG

Previous C-section; oxytocin augmentation; epidural; meconium; abnormal admission CTG; maternal medical conditions; multiple pregnancy; IUGR; breech

Fetal heart rate features

Baseline (110-160 bpm); variability (5-25); accelerations; decelerations (early, late, variable, prolonged)

Reassuring

Baseline 110-160; variability >5; accelerations present; no decelerations

Non-reassuring

Reduced variability; tachycardia (>160); bradycardia (<110); variable decelerations; late decelerations; complicated FHR

Pathological

Bradycardia <100; absent variability with decelerations; prolonged deceleration >3 min; sinusoidal pattern

Management suspicious/pathological

Maternal reposition; oxygen; stop oxytocin; IV fluids; FBC; consider FBS (fetal blood sampling — pH <7.2 = deliver)

FBS

Fetal scalp blood pH; pH >7.25 continue; <7.2 deliver; 7.2-7.25 repeat in 30 min

When to deliver

Persistent pathological trace despite resuscitation; prolonged bradycardia; FBS pH <7.2

Limitations

High false positive; inter-observer variation; 50-60% of babies with non-reassuring CTG are not acidotic

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Fetal monitoring in labour?

Key features include: Methods, Indications for continuous CTG, Fetal heart rate features, Reassuring, Non-reassuring. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Fetal monitoring in labour diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Fetal monitoring in labour?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Fetal monitoring in labour?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Induction Of Labour
topics.html:
Definition

Induction of labour (IOL) is the artificial initiation of labour before spontaneous onset. Indications include post-term pregnancy, pre-eclampsia, maternal medical conditions, fetal growth restriction, and prolonged rupture of membranes. Methods include mechanical (Foley catheter) and pharmacological (prostaglandins, oxytocin), with choice based on cervical favourability and indication.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

20-30% of pregnancies in developed countries; rising

Indications

Post-term (≥41 weeks); PE; maternal medical (diabetes, renal, lung); IUGR; cholestasis; PPROM; multiple pregnancy; Rhesus; previous stillbirth; maternal request (≥39 weeks)

Contraindications

Placenta praevia; vasa praevia; transverse lie; cord prolapse; prior classical C-section or T-incision; active genital herpes; invasive cervical cancer

Bishop score

Cervical favourability (0-13): dilation, effacement, station, position, consistency; <6 favours ripening

Cervical ripening

Prostaglandin E2 (dinoprostone) vaginal gel/pessary; misoprostol (off-label in UK); mechanical (Foley catheter); safe in most settings

Amniotomy

Artificial rupture of membranes (ARM); only if cervix favourable; combined with oxytocin for augmentation

Oxytocin

Syntocinon infusion for induction/augmentation; titrate to contractions (4-5 per 10 min); monitor continuous CTG; risk of hyperstimulation

Monitoring

Continuous CTG; partogram; maternal observations; pain control; review progress every 4 h (delay if <1 cm/2-4 h nullip, 1 cm/4 h multip)

Failed induction

Rest; repeat ripening; consider C-section if failed after adequate trial

Complications

Hyperstimulation (treat with tocolysis, terbutaline); uterine rupture (rare); failed induction; PPH; cord prolapse with ARM; uterine atony

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Induction of labour?

Induction of labour (IOL) is the artificial initiation of labour before spontaneous onset. Indications include post-term pregnancy, pre-eclampsia, maternal medical conditions, fetal growth restriction, and prolonged rupture of membranes. Methods include mechanical (Foley catheter) and pharmacological (prostaglandins, oxytocin), with choice based on cervical favourability and indication.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Induction of labour?

Key features include: Incidence, Indications, Contraindications, Bishop score, Cervical ripening. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Induction of labour diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Induction of labour?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Induction of labour?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Induction of labour — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Mastitis And Breast Abscess
topics.html:
Definition

Mastitis (inflammation of breast tissue, often with infection) affects 1 in 5 breastfeeding women, usually in the first 3 months. Staphylococcus aureus is the most common pathogen. Most cases respond to antibiotics and continued breastfeeding; abscesses require drainage.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

Lactational mastitis 10-20%; breast abscess 0.1-3%

Risk factors

Cracked nipples; poor attachment; engorgement; blocked ducts; infrequent feeding; rapid weaning; immunocompromise

Pathogens

S. aureus (most); coagulase-negative staph; Strep; rarely MRSA; community-acquired

Presentation

Unilateral breast pain, swelling, erythema, warmth; systemic (fever, malaise, myalgia); wedge-shaped area

Diagnosis

Clinical; milk culture if recurrent/severe; USS if abscess suspected; aspiration if fluid

Differential

Engorgement; blocked duct; breast abscess; inflammatory breast cancer; Paget's; mastitis from other organisms

Management

Continue breastfeeding (drain affected side); analgesia (paracetamol; ibuprofen); cold compresses; ensure attachment; frequent feeding

Antibiotics

Flucloxacillin 500 mg QDS 10-14 days; erythromycin or clarithromycin if allergic; co-amoxiclav if severe

Breast abscess

Needle aspiration (US-guided) or incision and drainage; antibiotics; continue feeding

Prevention

Correct attachment; frequent feeding; treat cracked nipples; avoid sudden weaning

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Mastitis and breast abscess?

Mastitis (inflammation of breast tissue, often with infection) affects 1 in 5 breastfeeding women, usually in the first 3 months. Staphylococcus aureus is the most common pathogen. Most cases respond to antibiotics and continued breastfeeding; abscesses require drainage.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Mastitis and breast abscess?

Key features include: Incidence, Risk factors, Pathogens, Presentation, Diagnosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Mastitis and breast abscess diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Mastitis and breast abscess?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Mastitis and breast abscess?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

  • Mnemonic for Mastitis and breast abscess — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Operative Vaginal Delivery
topics.html:
Definition

Operative vaginal delivery (OVD) — ventouse (vacuum) or forceps — is used to expedite delivery in the second stage of labour for fetal or maternal indications. Choice of instrument depends on clinical circumstances, operator experience, and station. Successful OVD avoids caesarean section and its associated morbidity.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

10-15% of vaginal deliveries; varies by hospital and population

Indications

Fetal distress (CTG abnormalities); maternal exhaustion; prolonged second stage; medical conditions (cardiac, neurological — avoid Valsalva); after epidural

Prerequisites

Fully dilated; ruptured membranes; engaged head (station +2 or below); cephalic presentation; known fetal position; adequate pelvis; empty bladder; experienced operator; maternal consent

Outlet OVD

Head at perineum; scalp visible; easy delivery; low risk

Low OVD

Leading point at +2 or below; rotation up to 45° acceptable

Mid-cavity OVD

Head engaged but above +2; higher risk; rarely used; senior operator

Ventouse

Vacuum cup; silastic or metal Kiwi; less maternal trauma; higher neonatal cephalhaematoma; less effective for rotation; avoid <34 weeks (cephalic) and face/breech

Forceps

Wrigley's (outlet), Neville-Barnes (low), Kielland (rotation); more maternal trauma but faster delivery; safer for preterm and face presentations

Complications maternal

Perineal trauma (3rd/4th degree); PPH; vaginal/cervical lacerations; urinary retention; fistula (rare); pelvic floor dysfunction

Complications neonatal

Cephalhaematoma; caput; retinal haemorrhage; facial nerve palsy (forceps); shoulder dystocia; subgaleal haemorrhage (ventouse) — emergency

topics.title: Postpartum Haemorrhage Pph
topics.html:
Definition

Postpartum haemorrhage is blood loss ≥500 mL within 24 hours of delivery (or any loss causing haemodynamic compromise). It is the leading cause of maternal mortality worldwide. The 4 Ts (tone, trauma, tissue, thrombin) guide cause and management.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Primary PPH: ≥500 mL within 24 h; major PPH ≥1000 mL; secondary PPH: 24 h-12 weeks

Incidence

5-10% of deliveries; major PPH 1-3%

Causes — 4 Ts

Tone (atonic uterus, 70%); Trauma (15%); Tissue (retained placenta, 10%); Thrombin (coagulopathy, 5%)

Risk factors

Previous PPH; multiple pregnancy; macrosomia; prolonged labour; augmentation; placenta praevia; abruption; coagulopathy

Prevention (active management 3rd stage)

Oxytocin 10 IU IM/IV; controlled cord traction; uterine massage

Initial management

ABC; call for help; 2 large-bore IVs; fluids; bloods incl. FBC, coagulation, crossmatch

Medical treatment

Oxytocin infusion; ergometrine (avoid in HTN); carboprost (avoid in asthma); misoprostol; tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 h

Surgical treatment

Bimanual compression; intrauterine balloon (Bakri); B-Lynch suture; arterial ligation; hysterectomy

Resuscitation

Activate massive transfusion protocol (1:1:1 ratio PRBC:FFP:platelets); cell salvage; warming

Secondary PPH

Endometritis; retained products; treat with antibiotics ± evacuation

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH)?

Postpartum haemorrhage is blood loss ≥500 mL within 24 hours of delivery (or any loss causing haemodynamic compromise). It is the leading cause of maternal mortality worldwide. The 4 Ts (tone, trauma, tissue, thrombin) guide cause and management.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH)?

Key features include: Definition, Incidence, Causes — 4 Ts, Risk factors, Prevention (active management 3rd stage). These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH)?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH)?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Sepsis In Pregnancy And The Puerperium
topics.html:
Definition

Sepsis is a leading direct cause of maternal mortality. Pregnancy-specific sources include chorioamnionitis, endometritis, urinary tract infection, wound infection, and mastitis. Recognition (modified SIRS/qSOFA), early antibiotics, and source control are critical.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

Leading direct cause of maternal death in UK MBRRACE; 5-10% of severe maternal morbidity

Sources

Genital tract (chorioamnionitis, endometritis); UTI; wound infection; mastitis; pneumonia; line infection

Screening tools

SIRS modified for pregnancy (WCC >16, HR >110, T >38 or <36, RR >22); qSOFA — altered mental state, RR >22, BP <100

Red flags

Fever, hypothermia, tachycardia, tachypnoea, hypotension, altered mental state, abdominal pain, foul lochia

Microbiology

Group A Strep (GAS); E. coli; S. aureus; anaerobes; Clostridium

Initial management

Sepsis 6 — oxygen; blood cultures; IV antibiotics within 1 h; IV fluids; lactate; urine output

Empirical antibiotics

Piperacillin-tazobactam + gentamicin; consider metronidazole; add clindamycin if GAS suspected

Source control

Evacuation of retained products; surgical intervention; line removal

Escalation

ITU; inotropes; mechanical ventilation; multidisciplinary team

Prevention

Hand hygiene; GBS prophylaxis; aseptic technique; reduce induction duration

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Sepsis in pregnancy and the puerperium?

Key features include: Epidemiology, Sources, Screening tools, Red flags, Microbiology. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Sepsis in pregnancy and the puerperium diagnosed?

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Sepsis in pregnancy and the puerperium?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Sepsis in pregnancy and the puerperium?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Q: Initial management?

A: Sepsis 6 — oxygen; blood cultures; IV antibiotics within 1 h; IV fluids; lactate; urine output

Q: Empirical antibiotics?

A: Piperacillin-tazobactam + gentamicin; consider metronidazole; add clindamycin if GAS suspected

Q: Source control?

A: Evacuation of retained products; surgical intervention; line removal

topics.title: Vaginal Birth After Caesarean Vbac
topics.html:
Definition

Vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC) is a successful vaginal delivery in a woman with a previous caesarean section. Trial of labour after caesarean (TOLAC) is appropriate in selected women with one prior low-transverse C-section. Success rates are 60-80% with appropriate selection; uterine rupture risk is ~0.5%.

Core Clinical Content

Eligibility

One prior low-transverse C-section; no other uterine scar; adequate pelvis; no recurrent indication; obstetrician + anaesthesia available

Success rate

60-80% overall; 70-90% if prior vaginal delivery; 60% if no prior vaginal

Uterine rupture risk

0.5% for TOLAC vs 0.03% for elective repeat C-section; 1% with prostaglandin induction

Contraindications

Prior classical/vertical/T incision; prior uterine rupture; prior extensive surgery; placenta praevia/accreta; multiple pregnancy (relative); patient refusal

Benefits of success

Avoid repeat C-section; lower maternal morbidity; lower VTE/infection; faster recovery; future pregnancies less complex

Risks of TOLAC

Uterine rupture (emergency hysterectomy, fetal death, maternal haemorrhage); failed TOLAC → C-section (higher morbidity)

Place of birth

Tertiary unit with on-site obstetrician, anaesthetist, theatre, blood bank; continuous CTG; IV access; 1:1 midwifery care

Monitoring

Continuous CTG; review 2nd stage progress; avoid prolonged 2nd stage; consider early ARM; avoid prostaglandin if possible

Analgesia

Epidural not contraindicated; allows faster intervention if needed

Future pregnancies

Each repeat C-section increases placenta praevia/accreta risk; generally 2-3 VBACs accepted; shared decision-making

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC)?

Vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC) is a successful vaginal delivery in a woman with a previous caesarean section. Trial of labour after caesarean (TOLAC) is appropriate in selected women with one prior low-transverse C-section. Success rates are 60-80% with appropriate selection; uterine rupture risk is ~0.5%.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC)?

Key features include: Eligibility, Success rate, Uterine rupture risk, Contraindications, Benefits of success. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC)?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC)?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.hub_name: Liver and Gastrointestinal Disease in Pregnancy
topics.title: Acute Fatty Liver Of Pregnancy Aflp
topics.html:
Definition

Acute fatty liver of pregnancy is a rare but life-threatening mitochondrial disorder presenting in the 3rd trimester with nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, jaundice, hepatic failure, encephalopathy, and DIC. Delivery is curative but supportive care is critical.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Microvesicular fatty infiltration of liver; 3rd trimester; liver failure, encephalopathy, DIC

Epidemiology

1 in 7,000-20,000; associated with LCHAD fetal enzyme deficiency

Symptoms

Nausea, vomiting, anorexia, abdominal pain, jaundice, thirst, polyuria, encephalopathy

Investigations

LFT (AST/ALT ↑); bilirubin ↑; coagulopathy (INR ↑); hypoglycaemia; hyperammonaemia; leucocytosis; renal failure; elevated creatinine

Swansea criteria

≥6 of: vomiting, abdominal pain, polydipsia/polyuria, encephalopathy, bilirubin >14, hypoglycaemia <4, uric acid >340, leucocytosis >11, ascites, hepatorenal syndrome, microvesicular steatosis on biopsy, coagulopathy

Differential

HELLP, viral hepatitis, drug-induced, TTP, autoimmune hepatitis

Management

Stabilise (glucose, FFP, platelets); ITU; delivery once stabilised; supportive care

Delivery

Vaginal preferred if stable; may need LSCS

Postpartum

Recovery over days to weeks; LFT and coagulopathy; monitor for pancreatitis, AKI

Future pregnancy

Recurrence low; screen fetus for LCHAD; avoid in subsequent

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.hub_name: Liver and Gastrointestinal Disease in Pregnancy
topics.title: Acute Pancreatitis In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Acute pancreatitis in pregnancy is uncommon (1 in 1000-3000 pregnancies) but carries significant maternal and fetal morbidity. Most cases are due to gallstones; hypertriglyceridaemia, alcohol, and idiopathic causes account for the remainder. Management is supportive with delivery considered in severe cases.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

1 in 1000-3000 pregnancies; rare but serious

Causes

Gallstones (60-70% — commonest); hypertriglyceridaemia (familial or secondary); alcohol; idiopathic; drugs; hyperparathyroidism; post-ERCP; trauma

Presentation

Severe epigastric pain radiating to back; nausea/vomiting; peritonism; fever; shock; jaundice

Diagnosis

Serum amylase >3x ULN; lipase more specific; USS to detect gallstones; MRCP if needed; CT rarely; differential: PUD, biliary colic, abruption, PE

Severity assessment

Atlanta criteria; BISAP score; CRP >150; organ failure (Atlanta severe)

Complications

Pancreatic necrosis; pseudocyst; ARDS; AKI; DIC; sepsis; death; preterm labour; fetal loss

Hypertriglyceridaemia-induced

Triglycerides >1000 mg/dL (>11 mmol/L); risk at >2000; usually with familial hypertriglyceridaemia

Management

NPO + IV fluids; analgesia (pethidine or morphine); NG tube if vomiting; antibiotics if infected necrosis; supportive care

ERCP/surgical

ERCP with sphincterotomy if biliary obstruction with cholangitis; cholecystectomy in 2nd trimester if stones; avoid in 1st and 3rd

Delivery

Vaginal preferred if stable; consider early delivery in severe disease; mode per obstetric; close fetal monitoring

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Acute pancreatitis in pregnancy?

Acute pancreatitis in pregnancy is uncommon (1 in 1000-3000 pregnancies) but carries significant maternal and fetal morbidity. Most cases are due to gallstones; hypertriglyceridaemia, alcohol, and idiopathic causes account for the remainder. Management is supportive with delivery considered in severe cases.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Acute pancreatitis in pregnancy?

Key features include: Incidence, Causes, Presentation, Diagnosis, Severity assessment. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Acute pancreatitis in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Acute pancreatitis in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Acute pancreatitis in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.hub_name: Liver and Gastrointestinal Disease in Pregnancy
topics.title: Cirrhosis And Portal Hypertension In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Cirrhosis and portal hypertension in pregnancy are uncommon as most cirrhotic women are beyond reproductive age or have amenorrhoea. However, with improved care, more women with compensated cirrhosis are becoming pregnant. Pregnancy carries significant risks of variceal bleeding, hepatic decompensation, and maternal mortality.

Core Clinical Content

Preconception

Optimise; screen for varices; beta-blocker; eradication of varices; counsel on risks; consider MELD score

Maternal risks

Variceal bleeding (5-30%); hepatic decompensation (ascites, encephalopathy, jaundice); postpartum haemorrhage; mortality 1-2% (compensated) up to 50% (decompensated)

Fetal risks

Miscarriage; preterm; low birth weight; stillbirth; congenital anomalies if on teratogens

Preconception screening

Upper GI endoscopy (varices); ultrasound (spleen, ascites, liver nodularity); MELD score; viral load if viral cause

Antenatal care

MDT (hepatologist, obstetrician, anaesthetist); endoscopic surveillance; LMWH if portal vein thrombosis or thrombocytopenia; avoid NSAIDs

Variceal bleeding

Most common in 2nd trimester; band ligation preferred; terlipressin (use cautiously); transfusion; rescue TIPSS if refractory

Beta-blockers

Propranolol or carvedilol for variceal prophylaxis; safe in pregnancy

Drugs to avoid

Statins (teratogenic); ACEI/ARB; methotrexate; some herbal products; high-dose vitamins A and E

Delivery

Vaginal preferred; assisted second stage (avoid Valsalva); avoid ergometrine; C-section for obstetric indication (haemorrhage risk)

Postpartum

Monitor for decompensation; VTE prophylaxis; breastfeeding compatible with most liver medications

Cirrhosis and portal hypertension in pregnancy are uncommon as most cirrhotic women are beyond reproductive age or have amenorrhoea. However, with improved care, more women with compensated cirrhosis are becoming pregnant. Pregnancy carries significant risks of variceal bleeding, hepatic decompensation, and maternal mortality.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Cirrhosis and portal hypertension in pregnancy?

Key features include: Preconception, Maternal risks, Fetal risks, Preconception screening, Antenatal care. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Cirrhosis and portal hypertension in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Cirrhosis and portal hypertension in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Cirrhosis and portal hypertension in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.hub_name: Liver and Gastrointestinal Disease in Pregnancy
topics.title: Gallstones And Biliary Disease In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Gallstone disease is the most common non-obstetric cause of abdominal pain in pregnancy due to elevated oestrogen (increased cholesterol secretion) and progesterone (biliary stasis). Most cases are asymptomatic; symptomatic disease includes biliary colic, acute cholecystitis, choledocholithiasis, and gallstone pancreatitis.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

Asymptomatic gallstones in 3-4% pregnancies; symptomatic 0.1%

Risk factors

Pre-existing gallstones; obesity; high-fat diet; rapid weight loss; multiparity; family history; oral oestrogens

Biliary sludge

Common in pregnancy; usually resolves postpartum

Biliary colic

Episodic RUQ/epigastric pain; resolves within hours; no fever; no Murphy's sign; no LFT derangement

Acute cholecystitis

RUQ pain; fever; Murphy's sign; leucocytosis; LFT may be mildly deranged; USS shows gallstones + wall thickening + pericholecystic fluid

Choledocholithiasis

Biliary colic + jaundice; cholangitis if Charcot's triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain)

Investigations

USS (first-line; safe); LFT; FBC; amylase; MRCP if needed; ERCP if cholangitis

Management asymptomatic

Reassure; no surgery

Management symptomatic

Conservative (NPO, IV fluids, analgesia, antibiotics); ERCP if cholangitis (radiation-minimising technique); cholecystectomy in 2nd trimester if recurrent/refractory

Postpartum

Symptoms often resolve; consider interval cholecystectomy if recurrent; contraception (avoid oestrogen)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
