PostpartumSymptoms often resolve; consider interval cholecystectomy if recurrent; contraception (avoid oestrogen)

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Gallstones and biliary disease in pregnancy?

Gallstone disease is the most common non-obstetric cause of abdominal pain in pregnancy due to elevated oestrogen (increased cholesterol secretion) and progesterone (biliary stasis). Most cases are asymptomatic; symptomatic disease includes biliary colic, acute cholecystitis, choledocholithiasis, and gallstone pancreatitis.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Gallstones and biliary disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Incidence, Risk factors, Biliary sludge, Biliary colic, Acute cholecystitis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Gallstones and biliary disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Gallstones and biliary disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Gallstones and biliary disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Gallstones and biliary disease in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.hub_name: Liver and Gastrointestinal Disease in Pregnancy
topics.title: Hyperemesis Gravidarum
topics.html:
Definition

Hyperemesis gravidarum is severe nausea and vomiting in pregnancy causing dehydration, electrolyte disturbance, and weight loss >5% of pre-pregnancy weight, requiring hospital admission. It typically begins before 12 weeks and resolves by 16-20 weeks.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Severe nausea/vomiting before 20 weeks; dehydration; ketones; >5% weight loss; needs admission

Epidemiology

0.3-2% of pregnancies; commoner in younger, primigravid, non-smokers, multiple pregnancy

Aetiology

hCG, oestrogen, GDF15, thyroid, H. pylori, psychosomatic

Differential

Molar pregnancy; UTI; thyrotoxicosis; Addison's; pancreatitis; hepatitis; GI obstruction

Complications

Wernicke's (thiamine); Mallory-Weiss; oesophageal tear; hypokalaemia; hyponatraemia; renal impairment

Investigations

U&E, LFT, TFT (transient hyperthyroidism in 50%), USS (molar/twins)

First-line treatment

Reassurance; lifestyle; ginger; P6 acupressure; antihistamines (cyclizine, promethazine)

Second-line

Metoclopramide; ondansetron (data reassuring but theoretical cardiac risk); prochlorperazine

Third-line

Corticosteroids (prednisolone/prednisone — avoid 1st trimester if possible); enteral/parenteral nutrition

Prevention

Pre-emptive multivitamin + ginger at conception in high-risk women

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Hyperemesis gravidarum?

Hyperemesis gravidarum is severe nausea and vomiting in pregnancy causing dehydration, electrolyte disturbance, and weight loss >5% of pre-pregnancy weight, requiring hospital admission. It typically begins before 12 weeks and resolves by 16-20 weeks.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Hyperemesis gravidarum?

Key features include: Definition, Epidemiology, Aetiology, Differential, Complications. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Hyperemesis gravidarum diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Hyperemesis gravidarum?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Hyperemesis gravidarum?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.hub_name: Liver and Gastrointestinal Disease in Pregnancy
topics.title: Inflammatory Bowel Disease In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) — Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis — commonly affects women in childbearing years. Active disease at conception is the strongest predictor of flare in pregnancy and adverse outcomes. Most medications (5-ASA, corticosteroids, anti-TNF) are safe; methotrexate is teratogenic and contraindicated.

Core Clinical Content

Fertility

Generally normal in UC and quiescent Crohn; decreased in active disease; pelvic surgery affects fertility

Preconception

Achieve remission ≥3-6 months before conception; continue maintenance therapy; switch teratogens (MTX stop 3 months)

Disease activity

Active disease at conception → 70% active during pregnancy; remission → 70% stay in remission

Maternal risks

Flare; malnutrition; anaemia; thromboembolism; surgical complications; need for surgery

Fetal risks

Active disease → preterm; LBW; stillbirth; miscarriage; congenital abnormalities if on teratogens

Safe medications

Mesalazine (5-ASA); sulfasalazine (with folate); corticosteroids (prednisolone, budesonide); azathioprine/6-MP; infliximab/adalimumab (limited placental transfer 1st trimester, then crosses — check levels)

Avoid

Methotrexate (teratogenic); mycophenolate (teratogenic — switch pre-conception); metronidazole in 1st trimester (some concerns); ciclosporin (limited data but used in severe UC)

Investigations during pregnancy

Faecal calprotectin; CRP; USS; MRI without gadolinium; flexible sigmoidoscopy safe; colonoscopy rarely if essential

Management of flare

Similar to non-pregnant: 5-ASA; steroids; biologic; consider colectomy if life-threatening (UC)

Delivery

Vaginal usual; consider C-section if active perianal Crohn; mode per obstetric indication; continue medications intrapartum

A: Active disease at conception → 70% active during pregnancy; remission → 70% stay in remission

Q: Maternal risks?

A: Flare; malnutrition; anaemia; thromboembolism; surgical complications; need for surgery

Q: Fetal risks?

A: Active disease → preterm; LBW; stillbirth; miscarriage; congenital abnormalities if on teratogens

Q: Safe medications?

A: Mesalazine (5-ASA); sulfasalazine (with folate); corticosteroids (prednisolone, budesonide); azathioprine/6-MP; infliximab/adalimumab (limited placental transfer 1st trimester, then crosses — check levels)

Q: Avoid?

A: Methotrexate (teratogenic); mycophenolate (teratogenic — switch pre-conception); metronidazole in 1st trimester (some concerns); ciclosporin (limited data but used in severe UC)

Q: Investigations during pregnancy?

A: Faecal calprotectin; CRP; USS; MRI without gadolinium; flexible sigmoidoscopy safe; colonoscopy rarely if essential

topics.hub_name: Liver and Gastrointestinal Disease in Pregnancy
topics.title: Intrahepatic Cholestasis Of Pregnancy Icp
topics.html:
Definition

Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy is a pruritic liver disorder of pregnancy, typically presenting in the 2nd or 3rd trimester with intense itching (especially palms and soles), elevated bile acids, and abnormal LFTs. It is associated with increased risk of stillbirth, preterm birth, and fetal distress.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Pruritus in pregnancy + abnormal LFT and/or bile acids >10 μmol/L

Prevalence

0.5-1.5% in Europe; up to 5-15% in South American (Araucanian) and South Asian populations

Pathophysiology

Impaired hepatic bile acid excretion; genetic (ABCB4/MDR3) and hormonal factors

Symptoms

Intense pruritus (palms, soles) without rash; worse at night; steatorrhoea; dark urine

Diagnosis

Bile acids >10 μmol/L (gold standard); raised ALT/AST; ALP poorly specific; exclude viral hepatitis, PBC

Fetal risks

Stillbirth (esp. if bile acids >40); preterm birth; meconium staining; fetal distress; postpartum haemorrhage

Maternal management

Ursodeoxycholic acid 10-15 mg/kg/day — first-line; reduces pruritus and may reduce fetal risk

Monitoring

Weekly bile acids and LFT; weekly CTG from 34 weeks; growth scans; patient education on stillbirth

Delivery

Induce at 36-37 weeks (or earlier if severe; bile acids >40 deliver at 36)

Postpartum

Resolves; LFT normalises within 6-12 weeks; recurrence 60-70%; long-term risk of gallstones, biliary cirrhosis

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP)?

Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy is a pruritic liver disorder of pregnancy, typically presenting in the 2nd or 3rd trimester with intense itching (especially palms and soles), elevated bile acids, and abnormal LFTs. It is associated with increased risk of stillbirth, preterm birth, and fetal distress.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP)?

Key features include: Definition, Prevalence, Pathophysiology, Symptoms, Diagnosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP)?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP)?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg

topics.hub_name: Liver and Gastrointestinal Disease in Pregnancy
topics.title: Viral Hepatitis C And E In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Viral hepatitis C (HCV) and E (HEV) in pregnancy are less common than HBV but carry specific risks. HCV has a low vertical transmission rate (5%) but is increased with co-infection (HIV, high viral load). HEV in pregnancy is associated with severe hepatitis and fulminant liver failure, particularly in the 3rd trimester.

Core Clinical Content

Hepatitis C (HCV)

Most common blood-borne virus; affects 1-2% pregnancies; vertical transmission 5% (higher with HIV co-infection or high VL)

Hepatitis E (HEV)

Faecal-oral; endemic (Asia, Africa, Central America); 3rd-trimester mortality 20-30% (vs <1% in non-pregnant)

HCV diagnosis

Anti-HCV; HCV RNA PCR; LFT; genotype

HEV diagnosis

Anti-HEV IgM/IgG; HEV RNA; LFT; consider in unexplained hepatitis

HCV management

Direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) deferred until postpartum; monitor LFT; avoid invasive procedures in labour (scalp electrodes); avoid prolonged ROM; avoid instrumental if possible

HCV delivery

Mode does not affect transmission; avoid internal fetal monitoring; avoid prolonged ROM >6 h

HCV breastfeeding

Safe (if no cracked nipples)

HCV neonate

Test at 12-18 months (anti-HCV); PCR if early

HEV management

Supportive; ribavirin not used (teratogenic); consider delivery if severe; fulminant failure may need transplant

HEV prevention

Avoid undercooked meat (zoonotic); safe water; HEV vaccine (Hecolin) in endemic areas

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viral hepatitis C (HCV) and E (HEV) in pregnancy are less common than HBV but carry specific risks. HCV has a low vertical transmission rate (5%) but is increased with co-infection (HIV, high viral load). HEV in pregnancy is associated with severe hepatitis and fulminant liver failure, particularly in the 3rd trimester.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Viral hepatitis C and E in pregnancy?

Q3. How is Viral hepatitis C and E in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Viral hepatitis C and E in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Viral hepatitis C and E in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Breast Cancer In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Breast cancer in pregnancy is the most common malignancy in pregnancy (1 in 3,000-10,000 pregnancies), often presenting at advanced stage due to pregnancy-related breast changes. Treatment is individualised according to gestational age, stage, and tumour biology, balancing maternal prognosis with fetal safety.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

1 in 3,000-10,000; most common cancer in pregnancy; median age 33

Risk factors

BRCA1/2; family history; older maternal age; not associated with pregnancy itself

Presentation

Lump (most common); nipple discharge; skin changes; often delayed diagnosis (mimics physiological changes)

Diagnosis

Triple assessment: clinical exam; imaging (USS first-line; mammogram with shielding); core biopsy

Staging

MRI without gadolinium; CXR; liver USS; bone scan deferred; sentinel node biopsy with technetium (limited); blue dye avoid (anaphylaxis)

Treatment surgery

Mastectomy or wide local excision; safe all trimesters; SLNB with technetium

Chemotherapy

Avoid 1st trimester (teratogenic); AC or FAC regimens from 2nd trimester; stop 3 weeks before delivery; not after 34-35 weeks

Avoid in pregnancy

Radiotherapy; trastuzumab (HER2+ — oligohydramnios); endocrine therapy (tamoxifen — teratogenic); methotrexate

Delivery

Timing based on treatment; if possible delay until fetal maturity; vaginal if obstetric; avoid iatrogenic prematurity

Prognosis

Often advanced at diagnosis; stage-matched similar to non-pregnant; prognosis improving with modern treatment

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Breast cancer in pregnancy?

Breast cancer in pregnancy is the most common malignancy in pregnancy (1 in 3,000-10,000 pregnancies), often presenting at advanced stage due to pregnancy-related breast changes. Treatment is individualised according to gestational age, stage, and tumour biology, balancing maternal prognosis with fetal safety.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Breast cancer in pregnancy?

Key features include: Incidence, Risk factors, Presentation, Diagnosis, Staging. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Breast cancer in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Breast cancer in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Breast cancer in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Cancer In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Cancer complicates 1 in 1,000-2,000 pregnancies. The most common malignancies are breast, cervical, melanoma, thyroid, lymphoma, and leukaemia. Management requires MDT balancing maternal prognosis, fetal viability, and gestational age.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

1 in 1,000-2,000 pregnancies; commoner as maternal age rises

Common cancers

Breast (most common), cervical, melanoma, thyroid, lymphoma, leukaemia, colorectal

Diagnosis

Often delayed because symptoms overlap with pregnancy; imaging without ionising radiation; tissue diagnosis essential

Staging

MRI without gadolinium; ultrasound; CXR with shielding; avoid CT/PET where possible

Surgery

Safe all trimesters; optimal 2nd trimester

Chemotherapy

Avoid 1st trimester (teratogenicity); safe in 2nd/3rd trimesters; avoid chemo 3 weeks before delivery (myelosuppression)

Radiotherapy

Generally avoided in pregnancy; scatter risks; only if fetus can be shielded and life-saving

Targeted/immunotherapy

Limited data; trastuzumab causes oligohydramnios; checkpoint inhibitors can cause miscarriage

End-of-pregnancy care

Delivery by 35-37 weeks to allow postpartum treatment; consider corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity

Termination

Considered if aggressive malignancy; patient's choice; MDT input

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease Gtd
topics.html:
Definition

Gestational trophoblastic disease encompasses hydatidiform mole (complete and partial), invasive mole, choriocarcinoma, and placental site trophoblastic tumour. hCG is the key biomarker. Most are cured with appropriate management and surveillance.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Spectrum from molar pregnancy to metastatic GTN

Complete mole

46,XX (or XY) androgenetic; empty egg + sperm duplication; no fetus; hCG very high; 2-3% choriocarcinoma

Partial mole

69,XXX/XXY/XYY; triploid; fetus present but abnormal; hCG mildly raised; 0.5% choriocarcinoma

Presentation

Vaginal bleeding; hyperemesis; hyperthyroidism (hCG); uterine size > dates; theca lutein cysts; pre-eclampsia <20 weeks

Diagnosis

USS — 'snowstorm' (complete); cystic spaces with fetus (partial); hCG >100,000

Management molar

Suction curettage (evacuation); Rh prophylaxis if Rh-; send for histology

Surveillance

Serial hCG weekly until normal, then monthly for 6 months (complete) or 1 month (partial); reliable contraception

GTN

Rising or plateau hCG; choriocarcinoma; metastasis to lung (80%), vagina, liver, brain

Treatment GTN

Low-risk: methotrexate or actinomycin-D; high-risk: EMA-CO (etoposide, methotrexate, actinomycin, cyclophosphamide, vincristine)

Future pregnancy

Normal fertility; 1% recurrence; hCG 6-8 weeks postpartum; send histology

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Gestational trophoblastic disease (GTD)?

Gestational trophoblastic disease encompasses hydatidiform mole (complete and partial), invasive mole, choriocarcinoma, and placental site trophoblastic tumour. hCG is the key biomarker. Most are cured with appropriate management and surveillance.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Gestational trophoblastic disease (GTD)?

Key features include: Definition, Complete mole, Partial mole, Presentation, Diagnosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Gestational trophoblastic disease (GTD) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Gestational trophoblastic disease (GTD)?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Gestational trophoblastic disease (GTD)?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Gestational trophoblastic disease (GTD) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Antenatal And Postnatal Depression
topics.html:
Definition

Antenatal (prenatal) and postnatal (postpartum) depression are common and treatable mood disorders affecting 10-15% of women in pregnancy and the year after birth. Distinguishing from normal postpartum adjustment ('baby blues'), anxiety, and postpartum psychosis is critical. Untreated illness has significant impact on mother, baby, and family.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

Antenatal depression 10-15%; postnatal depression 10-15%; underdiagnosed

Risk factors

Prior depression (strongest); family history; lack of support; domestic violence; recent stress; teenage; substance use; social isolation

Screening

Whooley + GAD-2 at booking and 6 weeks; EPDS ≥13 or positive Q10 (suicidal ideation) prompts further assessment

Symptoms

Persistent low mood; anhedonia; sleep disturbance (despite baby sleeping); appetite change; fatigue; guilt; poor concentration; suicidal thoughts; poor bonding

Differential

Baby blues (3-5 days, self-limiting); anxiety; OCD; PTSD; thyroid dysfunction; anaemia; postnatal psychosis (psychotic features)

Treatment

Stepped care: self-help, peer support; psychological therapies (CBT, IPT — first-line); SSRIs (sertraline, citalopram — safe in pregnancy/lactation)

Sertraline

First-line SSRI in pregnancy/lactation; lowest transfer to breast milk; data reassuring

Avoid

Paroxetine (1st-trimester cardiac risk); benzodiazepines (neonatal withdrawal, floppy baby); lithium (cardiac — monitor); valproate (developmental delay)

ECT

For severe, psychotic, or treatment-resistant depression; safe in pregnancy

Multidisciplinary care

GP; HV; obstetrician; psychiatrist; perinatal mental health team; safeguarding; crisis plan

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Distinguishing from normal postpartum adjustment ('baby blues'), anxiety, and postpartum psychosis is critical. Untreated illness has significant impact on mother, baby, and family.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Antenatal and postnatal depression?

Key features include: Prevalence, Risk factors, Screening, Symptoms, Differential. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Antenatal and postnatal depression diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Antenatal and postnatal depression?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Antenatal and postnatal depression?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Perinatal Mental Health Disorders
topics.html:
Definition

Perinatal mental health disorders — including antenatal and postnatal depression, anxiety, OCD, PTSD, postpartum psychosis, and tokophobia — are common and treatable but under-recognised. Untreated illness has significant impact on mother, baby, and family.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

Antenatal depression 10-15%; postnatal depression 10-15%; anxiety 15%; postpartum psychosis 0.1-0.2%

Risk factors

Prior mental illness (strongest); recent stressful events; lack of support; domestic abuse; teenage; substance use

Screening

Whooley questions + GAD-2 at booking and 6 weeks postnatal; Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS)

Postpartum depression

Onset within 4-6 weeks; persistent low mood, anhedonia, anxiety, guilt; suicidal ideation; impact on bonding

Postpartum psychosis

Onset within 2-4 weeks; mania, delusions, hallucinations, disorganised behaviour — psychiatric emergency

Anxiety disorders

Common; generalised anxiety, OCD (intrusive thoughts of harm to baby — ego-dystonic), PTSD (especially after traumatic birth)

Tokophobia

Severe fear of childbirth; consider delivery by caesarean if persistent

Treatment

Psychological therapies first-line (CBT, IPT); SSRIs (sertraline, citalopram — safe in pregnancy/lactation); ECT for severe/psychotic

Drug safety

Paroxetine (cardiac risk — 1st trimester); benzodiazepines (neonatal withdrawal); lithium (cardiac — monitor levels); valproate contraindicated

Multidisciplinary care

Perinatal mental health team; GP; HV; obstetrician; psychiatrist; crisis plan; safeguarding

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Multidisciplinary carePerinatal mental health team; GP; HV; obstetrician; psychiatrist; crisis plan; safeguarding

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Perinatal mental health disorders?

Perinatal mental health disorders — including antenatal and postnatal depression, anxiety, OCD, PTSD, postpartum psychosis, and tokophobia — are common and treatable but under-recognised. Untreated illness has significant impact on mother, baby, and family.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Perinatal mental health disorders?

Key features include: Prevalence, Risk factors, Screening, Postpartum depression, Postpartum psychosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Perinatal mental health disorders diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Perinatal mental health disorders?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Perinatal mental health disorders?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Perinatal mental health disorders — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Postpartum Psychosis
topics.html:
Definition

Postpartum (puerperal) psychosis is a rare psychiatric emergency occurring within 2-4 weeks of delivery, affecting 0.1-0.2% of women. It presents with mania, delusions, hallucinations, and disorganised behaviour, and carries a high risk of suicide and infanticide. Rapid recognition, hospitalisation, and treatment are essential.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

1-2 per 1000 deliveries; psychiatric emergency

Onset

Within 2-4 weeks postpartum (most within 2 weeks); rapid onset over days

Risk factors

Prior postpartum psychosis (25-50% recurrence); bipolar disorder; prior psychosis; family history of bipolar/psychosis; first pregnancy; sleep deprivation

Presentation

Mania (elated, irritable, agitated); delusions (often related to baby — intruder, contamination, harm); hallucinations; disorganised behaviour; insomnia; poor insight

Differential

Severe postnatal depression with psychotic features (later onset, no elation); delirium (eclampsia, sepsis, drug-induced); bipolar relapse

Dangers

Suicide (5%); infanticide (4%); neglect of baby

Treatment

Urgent hospitalisation (often Mother and Baby Unit if available); antipsychotics (olanzapine, haloperidol); lithium (monitor levels); benzodiazepines; ECT if severe

Lithium

Effective but teratogenic (cardiac — Ebstein anomaly) and toxic in neonate; monitor levels; breastfeeding caution; restart in 2nd trimester if needed

Prognosis

Majority recover with treatment; 50% relapse in subsequent postpartum period; risk of bipolar diagnosis lifelong

Prevention (recurrence)

Early identification; lithium prophylaxis from delivery; supervised treatment; preconception planning

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Incidence1-2 per 1000 deliveries; psychiatric emergency
OnsetWithin 2-4 weeks postpartum (most within 2 weeks); rapid onset over days
Risk factorsPrior postpartum psychosis (25-50% recurrence); bipolar disorder; prior psychosis; family history of bipolar/psychosis; first pregnancy; sleep deprivation
PresentationMania (elated, irritable, agitated); delusions (often related to baby — intruder, contamination, harm); hallucinations; disorganised behaviour; insomnia; poor insight
DifferentialSevere postnatal depression with psychotic features (later onset, no elation); delirium (eclampsia, sepsis, drug-induced); bipolar relapse
DangersSuicide (5%); infanticide (4%); neglect of baby
TreatmentUrgent hospitalisation (often Mother and Baby Unit if available); antipsychotics (olanzapine, haloperidol); lithium (monitor levels); benzodiazepines; ECT if severe
LithiumEffective but teratogenic (cardiac — Ebstein anomaly) and toxic in neonate; monitor levels; breastfeeding caution; restart in 2nd trimester if needed
PrognosisMajority recover with treatment; 50% relapse in subsequent postpartum period; risk of bipolar diagnosis lifelong
Prevention (recurrence)Early identification; lithium prophylaxis from delivery; supervised treatment; preconception planning

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Postpartum psychosis?

Postpartum (puerperal) psychosis is a rare psychiatric emergency occurring within 2-4 weeks of delivery, affecting 0.1-0.2% of women. It presents with mania, delusions, hallucinations, and disorganised behaviour, and carries a high risk of suicide and infanticide. Rapid recognition, hospitalisation, and treatment are essential.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Postpartum psychosis?

Key features include: Incidence, Onset, Risk factors, Presentation, Differential. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Postpartum psychosis diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Postpartum psychosis?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Postpartum psychosis?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Substance Misuse In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Substance misuse in pregnancy — alcohol, tobacco, opioids, stimulants, cannabis, and others — is associated with significant maternal, fetal, and neonatal harm. Identification (universal screening), brief intervention, harm reduction, and specialist multidisciplinary support improve outcomes for mother and baby.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

Alcohol 10-15%; smoking 10-15% (declining); cannabis 3-5%; opioids 0.5-1%; stimulants variable

Universal screening

Brief validated tools (T-ACE, AUDIT-C for alcohol; brief tobacco intervention); non-judgmental

Alcohol effects

Fetal alcohol spectrum disorder (FASD); IUGR; preterm; facial dysmorphism; CNS abnormalities; no safe threshold

Smoking effects

Placental abruption (2x); placenta praevia; IUGR; preterm; stillbirth; SIDS (2-3x); childhood respiratory disease; OME

Opioid effects

Neonatal abstinence syndrome (NAS); IUGR; preterm; stillbirth; maternal overdose; HIV/HCV transmission (IV use)

Stimulants (cocaine, amphetamines)

Placental abruption; preterm; IUGR; congenital anomalies; neonatal withdrawal; SIDS

Cannabis

IUGR; preterm; childhood cognitive/behavioural issues; emerging data on neurodevelopment

Management

Brief intervention; motivational interviewing; referral to addiction services; harm reduction; supervised OST (methadone or buprenorphine) for opioids; nicotine replacement safe

Labour and delivery

Avoid pethidine in opioid users (precipitates withdrawal); pain control often higher; monitor for withdrawal; NAS monitoring of neonate

Postpartum

Safeguarding; social services; contraception; LARC; relapse prevention; peer support

topics.title: Multiple Pregnancy Twins And Higher Order
topics.html:
Definition

Multiple pregnancy — most commonly dichorionic diamniotic (DCDA) twins but also monochorionic diamniotic (MCDA), monochorionic monoamniotic (MCMA), and higher-order — carries significantly higher maternal and fetal risks. Specialist care is essential.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

1 in 80 pregnancies; rising with assisted conception and maternal age

Zygosity

DCDA — 2 placentas, 2 sacs; MCDA — 1 placenta, 2 sacs; MCMA — 1 placenta, 1 sac (rare, high risk)

Diagnosis

First-trimester USS — chorionicity determined by lambda/twin-peak sign (DCDA) vs T-sign (MCMA/MCDA)

Maternal risks

Hyperemesis; anaemia; pre-eclampsia (2-3x); GDM; postpartum haemorrhage; VTE

Fetal risks (DCDA)

Preterm 50%; IUGR; discordant growth; twin-twin transfusion; stillbirth

Fetal risks (MCMA/MCDA)

Twin-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS) 10-15%; cord entanglement; selective growth restriction; TRAP sequence

Antenatal care

DCDA — every 4 weeks to 36; MCDA — every 2 weeks from 16; USS growth and Doppler; screen for TTTS

TTTS management

Laser ablation of placental anastomoses; amnioreduction; selective feticide in severe cases

Timing of delivery

DCDA 37 weeks; MCDA 36-37; MCMA 32-34 (inpatient monitoring from 26-28 weeks)

Mode of delivery

DCDA — vaginal if cephalic/cephalic; MCMA — caesarean; MCDA depends on presentation

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Multiple pregnancy (twins and higher-order)?

Multiple pregnancy — most commonly dichorionic diamniotic (DCDA) twins but also monochorionic diamniotic (MCDA), monochorionic monoamniotic (MCMA), and higher-order — carries significantly higher maternal and fetal risks. Specialist care is essential.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Multiple pregnancy (twins and higher-order)?

Key features include: Prevalence, Zygosity, Diagnosis, Maternal risks, Fetal risks (DCDA). These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Multiple pregnancy (twins and higher-order) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Multiple pregnancy (twins and higher-order)?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Multiple pregnancy (twins and higher-order)?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Multiple pregnancy (twins and higher-order) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Preterm Labour And Birth
topics.html:
Definition

Preterm labour is onset of regular uterine contractions with cervical change before 37 weeks' gestation. Preterm birth is the leading cause of neonatal mortality and morbidity. Prevention (progesterone, cerclage) and management (tocolysis, corticosteroids, magnesium) are key.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Delivery before 37 weeks; very preterm <32; extremely preterm <28

Prevalence

7-10% of births; rising; leading cause of neonatal mortality

Risk factors

Prior PTB (strongest); multiple pregnancy; infection (UTI, chorioamnionitis); cervical insufficiency; smoking; substance use; short cervix

Prediction

Cervical length USS 16-24 weeks; short cervix <25 mm at 24 weeks — intervention

Prevention

Progesterone (vaginal 200 mg nocte) for short cervix or prior PTB; cerclage for prior PTB + short cervix; treat infection

Corticosteroids

Betamethasone 12 mg IM 24 h apart or dexamethasone 6 mg IM 12 h × 4 — between 24-34 weeks; reduces RDS, IVH, NEC, death

Tocolysis

Nifedipine (first-line); atosiban; indomethacin (<32 weeks only); beta-agonists; allow 48 h for steroids

Magnesium sulphate

For neuroprotection if delivery <30 weeks; 4 g IV loading

Antibiotics

For group B Strep prophylaxis if previous GBS, GBS in current pregnancy, or unknown status in preterm labour

Mode of delivery

Vaginal if cephalic and reassuring; caesarean for breech, fetal distress, or obstetric indication

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Preterm labour and birth?

Preterm labour is onset of regular uterine contractions with cervical change before 37 weeks' gestation. Preterm birth is the leading cause of neonatal mortality and morbidity. Prevention (progesterone, cerclage) and management (tocolysis, corticosteroids, magnesium) are key.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Preterm labour and birth?

Key features include: Definition, Prevalence, Risk factors, Prediction, Prevention. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Preterm labour and birth diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Preterm labour and birth?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Preterm labour and birth?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Preterm labour and birth — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

A: Prior PTB (strongest); multiple pregnancy; infection (UTI, chorioamnionitis); cervical insufficiency; smoking; substance use; short cervix

Q: Prediction?

A: Cervical length USS 16-24 weeks; short cervix <25 mm at 24 weeks — intervention

Q: Prevention?

A: Progesterone (vaginal 200 mg nocte) for short cervix or prior PTB; cerclage for prior PTB + short cervix; treat infection

Q: Corticosteroids?

A: Betamethasone 12 mg IM 24 h apart or dexamethasone 6 mg IM 12 h × 4 — between 24-34 weeks; reduces RDS, IVH, NEC, death

Q: Tocolysis?

A: Nifedipine (first-line); atosiban; indomethacin (<32 weeks only); beta-agonists; allow 48 h for steroids

Q: Magnesium sulphate?

A: For neuroprotection if delivery <30 weeks; 4 g IV loading

topics.title: Bell S Palsy And Cranial Neuropathies In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Bell's palsy (idiopathic facial nerve palsy) is more common in pregnancy (3x) and the puerperium. It presents with sudden unilateral lower motor neuron facial weakness. Most cases resolve spontaneously; treatment with corticosteroids improves outcomes and is safe in pregnancy.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

1 in 2000-6000 pregnancies; 3x non-pregnant; peak in 3rd trimester and 1st postpartum week

Aetiology

Oedema of facial nerve in fallopian canal; viral (HSV, HZV) association; hypertension; pre-eclampsia risk factor

Presentation

Sudden unilateral facial weakness (forehead involved); hyperacusis; loss of taste anterior 2/3 tongue; decreased lacrimation; post-auricular pain

Differential

Stroke (forehead spared); Ramsay Hunt (VZV); Lyme; otitis media; parotid tumour; Guillain-Barré

Investigations

Clinical diagnosis; consider neuroimaging if atypical (forehead spared, progressive, recurrent); audiometry if indicated

Treatment

Prednisolone 50 mg × 10 days within 72 h of onset; eye protection (artificial tears, tape at night); antivirals (valaciclovir) if severe

Prognosis

85% complete recovery within 3 months; 15% have residual weakness; synkinesis; recurrence 5-10% subsequent pregnancies

Eye care

Critical to prevent exposure keratopathy; lubricate; taping at night; ophthalmology review if corneal exposure

Other cranial neuropathies

Trigeminal neuralgia (carbamazepine caution); optic neuritis (MS-related); vestibular schwannoma (monitor)

Postpartum

Monitor infant if on prednisolone; breastfeeding compatible; review at 3 months

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q1. What is the definition of Bell's palsy and cranial neuropathies in pregnancy?

Bell's palsy (idiopathic facial nerve palsy) is more common in pregnancy (3x) and the puerperium. It presents with sudden unilateral lower motor neuron facial weakness. Most cases resolve spontaneously; treatment with corticosteroids improves outcomes and is safe in pregnancy.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Bell's palsy and cranial neuropathies in pregnancy?

Key features include: Epidemiology, Aetiology, Presentation, Differential, Investigations. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Bell's palsy and cranial neuropathies in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Bell's palsy and cranial neuropathies in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Bell's palsy and cranial neuropathies in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Epilepsy In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Epilepsy affects 0.5-1% of pregnant women. Most anti-epileptic drugs (AEDs) are teratogenic to varying degrees. Preconception counselling, monotherapy at the lowest effective dose with high-dose folic acid, and specialist antenatal care optimise outcomes.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

0.5-1% of pregnancies; AEDs in 0.3-0.5%

Preconception

Switch to lowest-risk AED (lamotrigine/levetiracetam); monotherapy; lowest effective dose; folic acid 5 mg

Teratogenicity

Sodium valproate highest (10% major malformations, neurodevelopmental delay); carbimazole; phenytoin; phenobarbital

Safer AEDs

Lamotrigine and levetiracetam — lowest risk; monitor levels in 2nd/3rd trimesters (lamotrigine clearance rises)

Fetal risks

Major malformations (4-10% with valproate); NTDs (valproate, carbamazepine); fetal AED syndrome

Maternal risks

Increased seizure frequency in 30% (hormones, sleep deprivation, drug levels); SUDEP

Antenatal care

AED levels each trimester; anomaly scan 18-20 weeks; alpha-fetoprotein; targeted USS

Labour and delivery

Continue AEDs; IM vitamin K for neonate; avoid pethidine (metabolite lowers threshold); avoid dehydration/sleep deprivation

Postpartum

AED levels may rise — reduce dose if toxicity; breastfeeding usually safe; contraception (avoid COCP with enzyme inducers)

Counselling

Avoid valproate in women of childbearing age if possible; use LARC; emergency contraception unaffected

Epilepsy affects 0.5-1% of pregnant women. Most anti-epileptic drugs (AEDs) are teratogenic to varying degrees. Preconception counselling, monotherapy at the lowest effective dose with high-dose folic acid, and specialist antenatal care optimise outcomes.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Epilepsy in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Preconception, Teratogenicity, Safer AEDs, Fetal risks. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Epilepsy in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Epilepsy in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Epilepsy in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Headache In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Headache is the most common neurological symptom in pregnancy. Most are benign (tension, migraine), but pregnancy-specific and serious causes include pre-eclampsia, subarachnoid haemorrhage, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, and pituitary apoplexy. New severe headache needs urgent evaluation.

Core Clinical Content

Common causes

Tension (most common); migraine (improves in 60%); sinusitis; neck pain

Red flags

Thunderclap onset; worst-ever; new after 20 weeks; fever; focal deficits; seizures; post-partum onset; papilloedema

Migraine

Often improves in 2nd/3rd trimesters (rising oestrogen); aura unchanged; severe — exclude CVST

Pre-eclampsia headache

Bifrontal, throbbing, worse in morning; new after 20 weeks; check BP, urate, urine PCR

Subarachnoid haemorrhage

Thunderclap; vomiting; photophobia; neck stiffness; CT then LP if negative

CVST

Headache progressive over days; seizures; focal deficits; postpartum; risk factors — dehydration, infection, prothrombotic

Pituitary apoplexy

Sudden severe headache; visual loss; ophthalmoplegia; cortisol deficiency — emergency

Imaging

MRI without contrast preferred; CT if MRI unavailable (low fetal dose)

Treatment migraine

Paracetamol 1st; ibuprofen 2nd trimester; triptans limited data; metoclopramide; avoid ergot

Prevention

Avoid triggers; propranolol safe; topiramate teratogenic

Headache is the most common neurological symptom in pregnancy. Most are benign (tension, migraine), but pregnancy-specific and serious causes include pre-eclampsia, subarachnoid haemorrhage, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, and pituitary apoplexy. New severe headache needs urgent evaluation.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Headache in pregnancy?

Key features include: Common causes, Red flags, Migraine, Pre-eclampsia headache, Subarachnoid haemorrhage. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Headache in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Headache in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Headache in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH, pseudotumour cerebri) in pregnancy typically presents with headache, visual disturbances, and papilloedema. It is more common in obese women of childbearing age. Management is conservative in mild cases; acetazolamide and surgical intervention may be needed for progressive visual loss.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

Commoner in obese women of childbearing years; incidence 0.9-2.2 per 100,000; rising with obesity

Pathophysiology

Impaired CSF absorption; raised intracranial pressure without mass lesion or hydrocephalus

Presentation

Headache (worse lying down/Valsalva); pulsatile tinnitus; transient visual obscurations; diplopia (CN VI palsy); visual loss; papilloedema

Investigations

Fundoscopy; visual fields; CT/MRI (rule out mass/venous sinus thrombosis); LP with opening pressure >25 cmH2O; CSF normal composition

Differential

Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (pregnancy risk); pre-eclampsia; hypertensive encephalopathy; intracranial mass

Treatment

Weight management; acetazolamide (250-500 mg BD; limited data in pregnancy — case-by-case); topiramate (avoid — teratogenic); FBP monitoring

Surgical

Optic nerve sheath fenestration (vision loss); VP shunt; LP shunt; venous sinus stenting (selected)

Repeated LP

Therapeutic LPs to reduce pressure; not definitive

Pregnancy considerations

Avoid dehydration; consider delivery route (assisted second stage to avoid Valsalva); mode of delivery per obstetric indication

Postpartum

Symptoms often improve; continue monitoring; preconception weight loss; consider bariatric surgery; recurrence common

topics.title: Multiple Sclerosis In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Multiple sclerosis affects women 2-3x more than men, often in childbearing years. Pregnancy is associated with reduced relapse rate (especially 3rd trimester) and increased relapse postpartum. Disease-modifying drugs require careful planning around conception.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

1 in 1,000 women; often diagnosed in 20s-30s

Effect of pregnancy

Relapse rate falls (esp. 3rd trimester); rises 3-6 months postpartum (transient)

Effect of MS on pregnancy

No increase in fetal malformations; possible ↑caesarean; small ↑low birth weight

Disease-modifying drugs

Stop before conception: fingolimod (2 months), natalizumab (3 months); interferon-β and glatiramer safe until pregnancy confirmed

Teratogenic

Cladribine; teriflunomide; contraindicated; washout with cholestyramine for teriflunomide

Relapse treatment

Methylprednisolone IV 3-5 days; consider IVIG if severe; MRI without gadolinium if needed

Labour and delivery

Vaginal preferred; epidural does not worsen MS; forceps/ventouse per obstetric indication

Postpartum

Restart DMD if breastfeeding not chosen; exclusive breastfeeding may reduce relapses

Fatigue

Common in pregnancy; manage with rest, support, vitamin D

Long-term

No long-term effect of pregnancy on MS progression

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Multiple sclerosis in pregnancy?

Multiple sclerosis affects women 2-3x more than men, often in childbearing years. Pregnancy is associated with reduced relapse rate (especially 3rd trimester) and increased relapse postpartum. Disease-modifying drugs require careful planning around conception.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Multiple sclerosis in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Effect of pregnancy, Effect of MS on pregnancy, Disease-modifying drugs, Teratogenic. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Multiple sclerosis in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Multiple sclerosis in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Multiple sclerosis in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Myasthenia Gravis In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Myasthenia gravis (MG) in pregnancy has a variable course — exacerbations are unpredictable, with risk highest in the first trimester and postpartum. Treatment with acetylcholinesterase inhibitors and corticosteroids is safe; immunosuppression and plasma exchange may be needed for myasthenic crisis.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

Bimodal: early (<40, female predominance) and late (male predominance); 1 in 10,000-50,000

Effect of pregnancy

Unpredictable; 30-40% worsen (1st trimester, postpartum); 30% improve; 30% stable

Maternal risks

Myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure); exacerbation; aspiration; fatigue

Fetal risks

Neonatal myasthenia (10-20% — transient); preterm; arthrogryposis (maternal anti-AChR antibodies)

Diagnosis

Anti-AChR antibodies (85%); anti-MuSK; EMG (decremental response); CT chest (thymoma)

Treatment

Pyridostigmine (first-line; safe); corticosteroids (safe); azathioprine (safe); IVIG and plasma exchange (safe; for crisis)

Drugs to avoid

Magnesium sulphate (worsens MG — use with caution in PE); aminoglycosides; fluoroquinolones; beta-blockers; some anaesthetic agents

Myasthenic crisis

Respiratory failure; bulbar weakness; treat with IVIG or plasma exchange + steroids; consider ITU/ventilation

Delivery

Vaginal preferred; epidural (avoid opioid respiratory depression); forceps/ventouse if fatigue in 2nd stage; avoid suxamethonium

Postpartum

Neonatal monitoring (respiratory, feeding) for 48-72 h; breastfeeding compatible with most drugs; high-dose steroids may need coverage

topics.title: Stroke In Pregnancy And The Puerperium
topics.html:
Definition

Stroke in pregnancy and the puerperium — ischaemic and haemorrhagic — is a leading cause of maternal mortality. Risk is highest in the postpartum period. Causes include pre-eclampsia, hypertension, paradoxical emboli, arterial dissection, and SAH from aneurysm rupture.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

30 per 100,000 pregnancies; 3x non-pregnant

Timing

Highest postpartum; 50% postpartum; 30% antenatal; 20% intrapartum

Ischaemic stroke risk factors

Pre-eclampsia/HELLP; hypertension; migraine; cardiac disease; APS; prothrombotic states; paradoxical emboli

Haemorrhagic stroke

Subarachnoid (aneurysm/AVM rupture); intracerebral (hypertensive, eclampsia); cortical vein thrombosis

Presentation

Sudden focal deficit; seizures; severe headache; altered consciousness; postpartum thunderclap headache

Investigation

Urgent CT/MRI; CT/MR venogram for CVST; CT/MR angiography; thrombophilia screen postpartum

Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis

Headache, seizures, focal deficits; treat with LMWH even if haemorrhagic conversion

Management ischaemic

Thrombolysis considered (limited evidence in pregnancy); MDT decision; aspirin 75-300 mg after haemorrhage excluded

SAH

Urgent neurosurgical input; securing aneurysm (coiling preferred)

Prevention

BP control in PE; MDT; preconception counselling; avoid dehydration; consider LMWH in APS

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Stroke in pregnancy and the puerperium?

Stroke in pregnancy and the puerperium — ischaemic and haemorrhagic — is a leading cause of maternal mortality. Risk is highest in the postpartum period. Causes include pre-eclampsia, hypertension, paradoxical emboli, arterial dissection, and SAH from aneurysm rupture.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Stroke in pregnancy and the puerperium?

Key features include: Incidence, Timing, Ischaemic stroke risk factors, Haemorrhagic stroke, Presentation. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Stroke in pregnancy and the puerperium diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Stroke in pregnancy and the puerperium?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Stroke in pregnancy and the puerperium?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Stroke in pregnancy and the puerperium — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Nutrition And Vitamins In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Adequate nutrition and supplementation in pregnancy support maternal health and fetal development. Routine supplementation with folic acid and vitamin D is recommended; iron is added for anaemia. Avoid excess vitamin A (teratogenic) and foodborne pathogens.

Core Clinical Content

Energy needs

+200-300 kcal/day in 2nd/3rd trimesters; obese women do not need additional calories

Folic acid

400 μg/day preconception to 12 weeks (reduce NTDs); 5 mg if high risk (prior NTD, antiepileptics, diabetes, obesity)

Vitamin D

10 μg/day (400 IU) for all; monitor 25-OH-D if at risk; deficiency → neonatal rickets

Iron

Routine from 16 weeks; 200 mg ferrous sulphate; IV if intolerance or severe

Vitamin A

Avoid excess (>700 μg retinol); teratogenic; avoid liver, high-dose supplements; beta-carotene safe

Calcium

1 g/day; supplementation in low-intake populations reduces pre-eclampsia

Iodine

140 μg/day; iodised salt; avoid kelp supplements

Foods to avoid

Soft cheeses, pâté, undercooked meat (listeria, toxoplasma); raw fish, high-mercury fish; alcohol

Caffeine

<200 mg/day (2 cups coffee) — high intake may ↑miscarriage, IUGR

Vegetarian/vegan

B12 supplementation; monitor iron, zinc, protein, omega-3

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Energy needs+200-300 kcal/day in 2nd/3rd trimesters; obese women do not need additional calories
Folic acid400 μg/day preconception to 12 weeks (reduce NTDs); 5 mg if high risk (prior NTD, antiepileptics, diabetes, obesity)
Vitamin D10 μg/day (400 IU) for all; monitor 25-OH-D if at risk; deficiency → neonatal rickets
IronRoutine from 16 weeks; 200 mg ferrous sulphate; IV if intolerance or severe
Vitamin AAvoid excess (>700 μg retinol); teratogenic; avoid liver, high-dose supplements; beta-carotene safe

Iodine140 μg/day; iodised salt; avoid kelp supplements

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Nutrition and vitamins in pregnancy?

Adequate nutrition and supplementation in pregnancy support maternal health and fetal development. Routine supplementation with folic acid and vitamin D is recommended; iron is added for anaemia. Avoid excess vitamin A (teratogenic) and foodborne pathogens.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Nutrition and vitamins in pregnancy?

Key features include: Energy needs, Folic acid, Vitamin D, Iron, Vitamin A. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Nutrition and vitamins in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Nutrition and vitamins in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Nutrition and vitamins in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Obesity In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Maternal obesity (BMI ≥30) is associated with increased risk of miscarriage, congenital anomalies, GDM, pre-eclampsia, macrosomia, caesarean section, VTE, and fetal death. Preconception weight loss and lifestyle modification improve outcomes.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

20-30% of pregnant women in developed countries; rising

Risks to mother

Miscarriage; GDM (3-4x); pre-eclampsia (2-3x); caesarean; PPH; VTE; anaesthetic difficulty; wound infection

Risks to fetus

NTD; cardiac defects; stillbirth (2x); macrosomia; shoulder dystocia; childhood obesity

Preconception

Weight loss (5-10%); folic acid 5 mg; screen for diabetes; counsel on risks

Antenatal care

Anomaly scan quality may be limited; consider repeat or alternative imaging; serial growth scans

Thromboprophylaxis

Risk assess; LMWH if BMI >40 or additional risk factors; consider for 10 days postpartum

GDM screening

Earlier OGTT at 24-28 weeks (or earlier if risk factors)

Weight gain

Institute of Medicine guidelines — underweight 12.5-18 kg; normal 11.5-16; overweight 7-11.5; obese 5-9

Bariatric surgery

Conceive 12-18 months post-surgery; monitor iron, B12, folate, calcium, vitamin D; risk of internal hernia

Labour

Higher caesarean; anaesthetic review; consider early epidural; PPH risk

topics.title: Breech Presentation And External Cephalic Version
topics.html:
Definition

Breech presentation is when the fetus presents with buttocks or feet first, occurring in 3-4% of term pregnancies. It is associated with increased perinatal morbidity. External cephalic version (ECV) can turn the breech to cephalic presentation antenatally, reducing the need for caesarean section.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Fetal buttocks or feet presenting at pelvic inlet; frank, complete, footling

Incidence

20% at 28 weeks; 3-4% at term

Risk factors

Prematurity; multiparity; multiple pregnancy; polyhydramnios; placenta praevia; uterine anomalies; fetal anomalies (anencephaly, hydrocephalus); contracted pelvis; previous breech

Diagnosis

Palpation (Leopold); USS confirms; 36-week check for all

External cephalic version (ECV)

Attempted at 36-37 weeks; success 50-60%; tocolysis (terbutaline); Rh status; USS-guided; complications: cord prolapse, abruption, fetal distress (0.5%)

Contraindications ECV

APH, placenta praevia, abnormal CTG, multiple pregnancy (except first twin), ruptured membranes, previous C-section (relative), uterine anomaly

Failed ECV

Offer trial of vaginal breech or planned C-section; mode based on clinician skill + patient preference

Vaginal breech delivery

Careful selection (Frank breech, flexed head, adequate pelvis, no footling); experienced clinician; continuous CTG; second stage assistance

Caesarean breech

Frank breech in primigravida often; footling/compete; failed ECV; maternal preference; previous C-section

Prognosis

Vaginal breech in selected cases similar outcomes to cephalic; morbidity higher in unselected

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Breech presentation and external cephalic version?

Breech presentation is when the fetus presents with buttocks or feet first, occurring in 3-4% of term pregnancies. It is associated with increased perinatal morbidity. External cephalic version (ECV) can turn the breech to cephalic presentation antenatally, reducing the need for caesarean section.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Breech presentation and external cephalic version?

Key features include: Definition, Incidence, Risk factors, Diagnosis, External cephalic version (ECV). These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Breech presentation and external cephalic version diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Breech presentation and external cephalic version?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Breech presentation and external cephalic version?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Fetal Growth Restriction Fgr Iugr
topics.html:
Definition

Fetal growth restriction (FGR), also called intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR), describes a fetus that has not reached its genetically predetermined growth potential. It is associated with increased risk of stillbirth, neonatal morbidity, and long-term metabolic and cardiovascular disease. Antenatal detection with timely delivery reduces perinatal mortality.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Estimated fetal weight (EFW) <10th centile (population) or abdominal circumference (AC) <10th centile

Classification

Symmetric (20%): early onset, genetic/infectious; asymmetric (80%): late onset, uteroplacental insufficiency

Causes

Fetal: chromosomal, structural anomalies, infection (TORCH); Maternal: HTN, autoimmune, smoking, malnutrition; Placental: pre-eclampsia, abruption, mosaicism

Risk factors

Prior FGR (strongest); pre-eclampsia; chronic HTN; APS; smoking; multiple pregnancy; maternal disease

Screening

First-trimester combined screen (PAPP-A low); uterine artery Doppler 20-24 weeks; serial fundal height

Diagnosis

USS: AC <10th centile; estimated fetal weight; umbilical artery Doppler; middle cerebral artery Doppler; cerebroplacental ratio

Monitoring

Serial growth scans (2-3 weeks); umbilical artery Doppler; MCA Doppler; cardiotocography; biophysical profile; liquor volume

Severe FGR

Abnormal ductus venosus; absent/reversed EDF in umbilical artery; delivery recommended based on gestation and findings

Timing of delivery

Depends on severity and Doppler findings; <30 weeks with abnormal DV may need delivery

Long-term

Neurodevelopmental delay; cerebral palsy; metabolic syndrome; HTN; cardiovascular disease (Barker hypothesis)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

A: Symmetric (20%): early onset, genetic/infectious; asymmetric (80%): late onset, uteroplacental insufficiency

Q: Causes?

A: Fetal: chromosomal, structural anomalies, infection (TORCH); Maternal: HTN, autoimmune, smoking, malnutrition; Placental: pre-eclampsia, abruption, mosaicism

Q: Risk factors?

A: Prior FGR (strongest); pre-eclampsia; chronic HTN; APS; smoking; multiple pregnancy; maternal disease

Q: Screening?

A: First-trimester combined screen (PAPP-A low); uterine artery Doppler 20-24 weeks; serial fundal height

Q: Diagnosis?

A: USS: AC <10th centile; estimated fetal weight; umbilical artery Doppler; middle cerebral artery Doppler; cerebroplacental ratio

Q: Monitoring?

A: Serial growth scans (2-3 weeks); umbilical artery Doppler; MCA Doppler; cardiotocography; biophysical profile; liquor volume

Q: Severe FGR?

A: Abnormal ductus venosus; absent/reversed EDF in umbilical artery; delivery recommended based on gestation and findings

Answer Key with Explanations

topics.title: Oligohydramnios
topics.html:
Definition

Oligohydramnios is reduced amniotic fluid volume (AFI <5 cm or DVP <2 cm). It is associated with fetal renal anomalies, IUGR, pre-labour rupture of membranes, and post-term pregnancy. Severe early-onset oligohydramnios causes pulmonary hypoplasia, skeletal deformities (Potter sequence), and high perinatal mortality.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

AFI <5 cm (or <5th centile) or DVP <2 cm

Incidence

0.5-5% at term; higher post-term (12%)

Causes

PPROM (most common at term); fetal renal anomalies (Potter, ARPKD, posterior urethral valves); IUGR/placental insufficiency; post-term; maternal drugs (NSAIDs, ACEI); twin-twin transfusion (donor); idiopathic

Presentation

Fundal height < dates; fetal parts easily palpable; reduced fetal movements

Investigations

Detailed anomaly scan (renal); UA Doppler (placental insufficiency); Kleihauer; review for PPROM; review drug history

Severe early (<24 weeks)

Pulmonary hypoplasia (high mortality); Potter facies; limb deformities; renal agenesis lethal

Monitoring

Serial USS; AFI twice weekly; CTG; consider admission if <34 weeks or severe

Management

Treat cause if possible; amnioinfusion in labour (equivocal); hydration (maternal oral/IV may increase AFI transiently)

Delivery

Induce at 36-37 weeks for isolated oligohydramnios; earlier if IUGR or non-reassuring; C-section for malpresentation; cord compression risk

Antenatal corticosteroids

If preterm delivery planned and <34 weeks

Prognosis

Depends on cause and gestation; isolated at term generally good

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Severe early-onset oligohydramnios causes pulmonary hypoplasia, skeletal deformities (Potter sequence), and high perinatal mortality.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Oligohydramnios?

Key features include: Definition, Incidence, Causes, Presentation, Investigations. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

topics.title: Placenta Accreta Spectrum
topics.html:
Definition

Placenta accreta spectrum (PAS) covers placenta accreta, increta, and percreta — abnormal placental invasion into or through the myometrium. It is associated with significant peripartum haemorrhage, hysterectomy, and maternal mortality. Risk is greatest in women with prior caesarean section and placenta praevia.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Placenta abnormally adherent: accreta (myometrium); increta (through myometrium); percreta (to serosa/adjacent organs)

Incidence

0.2 per 1000; rising with C-section rates

Risk factors

Prior C-section (5% if 1; 25% if 2; 50%+ if ≥3 with praevia); placenta praevia; prior uterine surgery; Asherman; multiparity; advanced maternal age

Presentation

Often asymptomatic antenatally; diagnosed on USS; failed placental separation at delivery; PPH

USS features

Loss of clear retroplacental hypoechoic zone; placental lacunae ('Swiss cheese'); bladder line interruption; hypervascularity

MRI

Confirms depth; useful for posterior placenta and surgical planning

Management

MDT (tertiary centre) — obstetrics, gynae-oncology, vascular surgery, haematology, anaesthetics, neonatology, IR

Delivery

Planned C-section hysterectomy at 34-36 weeks; leave placenta in situ if extensive; avoid attempts at removal

Conservative

Leave placenta in situ + methotrexate (controversial) ± UAE; successful in 60-80% but risk of sepsis/hemorrhage

Future pregnancy

Fertility preserved if uterus retained; recurrence risk; need careful counselling

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Placenta Praevia
topics.html:
Definition

Placenta praevia is implantation of the placenta in the lower uterine segment, covering or near the internal cervical os. It presents with painless, recurrent, bright-red vaginal bleeding in the second half of pregnancy. Diagnosis is by transabdominal and transvaginal ultrasound; delivery is by planned caesarean section.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Placenta covering or within 2 cm of internal os (major if covers, minor if low-lying)

Incidence

0.5-1% of pregnancies; rising with caesarean and advanced maternal age

Risk factors

Prior caesarean (major risk); prior placenta praevia; multiparity; multiple pregnancy; advanced maternal age; smoking; prior uterine surgery/curettage

Presentation

Painless bright-red bleeding (often recurrent); may be asymptomatic; malpresentation (breech/transverse)

Diagnosis

Transabdominal + transvaginal USS (definitive); MRI if accreta suspected; do NOT do digital vaginal examination

Management

Expectant if preterm (transfuse, steroids <34 weeks); iron supplementation; thromboprophylaxis; delivery by caesarean

Timing of delivery

Elective caesarean 36-37 weeks; emergency if heavy bleeding or fetal/maternal compromise

Placenta accreta

Placenta abnormally adherent (accreta/increta/percreta); risk with prior C-section + praevia; may need hysterectomy

Complications

Haemorrhage; shock; hysterectomy; preterm birth; fetal growth restriction; vasa praevia; recurrence 4-8%

Antenatal care

MDT (obstetrician, anaesthetist, haematologist); blood available; cell salvage; consider interventional radiology

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Placenta praevia?

Placenta praevia is implantation of the placenta in the lower uterine segment, covering or near the internal cervical os. It presents with painless, recurrent, bright-red vaginal bleeding in the second half of pregnancy. Diagnosis is by transabdominal and transvaginal ultrasound; delivery is by planned caesarean section.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Placenta praevia?

Key features include: Definition, Incidence, Risk factors, Presentation, Diagnosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Placenta praevia diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Placenta praevia?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Placenta praevia?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Placental Abruption
topics.html:
Definition

Placental abruption (abruptio placentae) is the premature separation of a normally implanted placenta from the uterine wall after 20 weeks' gestation and before delivery. It presents with painful vaginal bleeding, uterine tenderness, and hypertonic ('woody') uterus, and is a major cause of maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Premature separation of normally sited placenta after 20 weeks

Incidence

0.5-1% of pregnancies; 10-15% of perinatal mortality

Risk factors

Prior abruption (10x); hypertension (strongest); smoking; cocaine; trauma; PPROM; multiple pregnancy; polyhydramnios; thrombophilia

Presentation

Painful vaginal bleeding (80%); uterine tenderness/rigidity ('woody'); hypertonic uterus; absent/reduced fetal movements; shock; DIC; fetal distress/death

Concealed abruption

Retroplacental clot; no visible bleeding; 20%

Diagnosis

Clinical; USS (sensitivity <50%); CTG abnormal; exclude praevia

Investigations

FBC, coagulation (DIC screen), crossmatch, U&E, Kleihauer (if Rh-)

Management

Resuscitate (ABC, IV access, fluids); correct coagulopathy; delivery if unstable (vaginal if possible; C-section if fetal salvage required); steroids if preterm

Complications

Maternal: shock, DIC, AKI, PPH, Couvelaire uterus, Sheehan; Fetal: 15-20% mortality; preterm; asphyxia

Future pregnancy

Recurrence 5-15% (10% if one, 25% if two); aspirin prophylaxis; close surveillance

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary
