Placental abruption (abruptio placentae) is the premature separation of a normally implanted placenta from the uterine wall after 20 weeks' gestation and before delivery. It presents with painful vaginal bleeding, uterine tenderness, and hypertonic ('woody') uterus, and is a major cause of maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Placental abruption?

Key features include: Definition, Incidence, Risk factors, Presentation, Concealed abruption. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Placental abruption diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Placental abruption?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Placental abruption?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Polyhydramnios
topics.html:
Definition

Polyhydramnios is excessive amniotic fluid volume (AFI >25 cm or deepest vertical pocket >8 cm). It affects 1-2% of pregnancies and is associated with fetal anomalies (especially GI obstruction and CNS), maternal diabetes, and multiple pregnancy. Severe cases present with abdominal discomfort, preterm labour, and cord prolapse.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Amniotic fluid index (AFI) >25 cm or deepest vertical pocket (DVP) >8 cm

Incidence

0.5-2% of pregnancies; mild common, severe rare

Causes

Idiopathic (60%); fetal anomalies (15-20% — GI obstruction, oesophageal atresia, duodenal atresia, anencephaly, NTDs); maternal diabetes (10-20%); multiple pregnancy (TTTS); fetal infection; anaemia (parvovirus, haemolysis)

Mild polyhydramnios

Often idiopathic; AFI 25-30; close surveillance

Severe polyhydramnios

AFI >35; preterm labour risk; cord prolapse; abruption; PPH; respiratory compromise

Presentation

Fundal height > dates; tense abdomen; fluid thrill; dyspnoea; preterm contractions

Investigations

Detailed anomaly scan; OGTT for diabetes; Kleihauer; maternal serology (parvovirus, CMV); fetal karyotype if indicated

Monitoring

Regular scans; growth; wellbeing; symphysis-fundal height

Management mild

Expectant; treat underlying cause if possible

Management severe

Amnioreduction (therapeutic amniocentesis); indomethacin (reduces fetal urine output — <32 weeks); close monitoring

Delivery

Risk of cord prolapse (ARM with controlled drain); PPH risk; breech presentation

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Polyhydramnios?

Polyhydramnios is excessive amniotic fluid volume (AFI >25 cm or deepest vertical pocket >8 cm). It affects 1-2% of pregnancies and is associated with fetal anomalies (especially GI obstruction and CNS), maternal diabetes, and multiple pregnancy. Severe cases present with abdominal discomfort, preterm labour, and cord prolapse.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Polyhydramnios?

Key features include: Definition, Incidence, Causes, Mild polyhydramnios, Severe polyhydramnios. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Polyhydramnios diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Polyhydramnios?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Polyhydramnios?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Polyhydramnios — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Pre Labour Rupture Of Membranes Prom Pprom
topics.html:
Definition

Pre-labour rupture of membranes (PROM) is rupture of membranes before onset of labour. When it occurs before 37 weeks it is termed preterm pre-labour rupture of membranes (PPROM) and is associated with infection, preterm delivery, and neonatal morbidity. Management balances prolonging pregnancy for fetal maturity against infection risk.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

PROM: ROM before labour at term; PPROM: ROM before labour <37 weeks

Diagnosis

History of gush; speculum showing pooling of fluid; nitrazine paper (amniotic fluid alkaline); ferning test; actim PROM (PAMG-1); USS oligohydramnios

PPROM incidence

2-4% of pregnancies; 30-40% of preterm births

Risk factors

Prior PPROM; infection (chorioamnionitis, GBS); multiple pregnancy; polyhydramnios; smoking; cervical insufficiency; low BMI

Complications

Chorioamnionitis (10-30%); preterm delivery; cord prolapse; placental abruption; neonatal sepsis; pulmonary hypoplasia (<24 weeks); deformity

Investigations

Maternal: FBC, CRP, temperature, pulse; swabs (GBS, chlamydia); high vaginal swab; USS (AFI, growth, presentation)

Management PPROM

Expectant if no infection: steroids (24-34 weeks); magnesium <32 weeks; latency antibiotics (erythromycin + amoxicillin) 7-10 days; GBS prophylaxis; LMWH; monitor for chorioamnionitis

Chorioamnionitis

Fever, tachycardia, uterine tenderness, foul discharge, fetal tachycardia — immediate delivery + IV antibiotics

PROM at term

Induce labour at 24 h (reduce infection; expectant is acceptable if wishes); GBS prophylaxis if prolonged

Prognosis

Latency inversely related to gestation; 50% deliver within 1 week PPROM at <34 weeks

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

PPROM incidence2-4% of pregnancies; 30-40% of preterm births
Risk factorsPrior PPROM; infection (chorioamnionitis, GBS); multiple pregnancy; polyhydramnios; smoking; cervical insufficiency; low BMI

Management balances prolonging pregnancy for fetal maturity against infection risk.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Pre-labour rupture of membranes (PROM-PPROM)?

Key features include: Definition, Diagnosis, PPROM incidence, Risk factors, Complications. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Pre-labour rupture of membranes (PROM-PPROM) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Pre-labour rupture of membranes (PROM-PPROM)?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Pre-labour rupture of membranes (PROM-PPROM)?

topics.title: Rhesus Isoimmunisation And Anti D
topics.html:
Definition

Rhesus (Rh) isoimmunisation occurs when a Rh-negative mother is exposed to Rh-positive fetal red cells (often at delivery), producing anti-D IgG antibodies that can cross the placenta and cause haemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn (HDFN) in subsequent pregnancies. Anti-D prophylaxis at 28-30 weeks and post-delivery prevents sensitisation.

Core Clinical Content

Pathophysiology

Rh- mother sensitised to Rh+ fetal red cells → IgG anti-D → crosses placenta → HDFN in subsequent pregnancies

At-risk pregnancies

Any Rh- mother with Rh+ (or unknown) fetus; sensitising events: delivery, miscarriage, ectopic, CVS, amniocentesis, APH, trauma, ECV

Sensitising events

Anti-D 250-500 IU IM within 72 h of event; Kleihauer test to quantify fetomaternal haemorrhage and adjust dose

Routine prophylaxis

Anti-D 500 IU IM at 28 weeks (UK); 1500 IU IM at 28 and 34 weeks (US)

Post-delivery

Anti-D 500 IU IM within 72 h of delivery of Rh+ baby; Kleihauer to confirm adequate dose

HDFN spectrum

Mild anaemia → hydrops fetalis (severe); kernicterus in neonate; intrauterine transfusion

Monitoring affected pregnancy

Maternal anti-D titres; middle cerebral artery Doppler (peak systolic velocity); serial USS; amniocentesis if needed

Treatment severe HDFN

Intrauterine transfusion; early delivery; exchange transfusion; IVIG neonatal

Already sensitised

No prophylaxis helps; monitor titres; consider plasmapheresis; MDT; consider IVIG; delivery timing critical

Future

Prophylaxis effective; rare failures due to silent sensitisation or inadequate dose

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D?

Rhesus (Rh) isoimmunisation occurs when a Rh-negative mother is exposed to Rh-positive fetal red cells (often at delivery), producing anti-D IgG antibodies that can cross the placenta and cause haemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn (HDFN) in subsequent pregnancies. Anti-D prophylaxis at 28-30 weeks and post-delivery prevents sensitisation.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D?

Key features include: Pathophysiology, At-risk pregnancies, Sensitising events, Routine prophylaxis, Post-delivery. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Rhesus isoimmunisation and anti-D?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg

  4. Anti-D 500 IU IM at 28 weeks (UK); 1500 IU IM at 28 and 34 weeks (US)
  5. Anti-D 500 IU IM within 72 h of delivery of Rh+ baby; Kleihauer to confirm adequate dose
  6. Mild anaemia → hydrops fetalis (severe); kernicterus in neonate; intrauterine transfusion
  7. Maternal anti-D titres; middle cerebral artery Doppler (peak systolic velocity); serial USS; amniocentesis if needed
  8. Intrauterine transfusion; early delivery; exchange transfusion; IVIG neonatal
  9. No prophylaxis helps; monitor titres; consider plasmapheresis; MDT; consider IVIG; delivery timing critical
  10. Prophylaxis effective; rare failures due to silent sensitisation or inadequate dose

topics.hub_name: Bone and Joint Disease in Pregnancy
topics.title: Pelvic Girdle Pain And Low Back Pain In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Pelvic girdle pain (PGP) and low back pain (LBP) affect 50-70% of pregnant women. PGP arises from the sacroiliac joints and/or pubic symphysis; LBP from the lumbar spine. Most resolve postpartum; physiotherapy and analgesia are mainstays of treatment.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

PGP 20%; LBP 50-70%; 5-10% severe/disabling

Risk factors

Prior PGP/LBP; multiparity; BMI; high physical workload; stress; pelvic trauma

Symptoms PGP

Pain over SI joints or pubic symphysis; difficulty walking, climbing stairs, turning in bed; positive Patrick-Faber and active SLR tests

Symptoms LBP

Lumbar pain; worse with activity; tender over paraspinal muscles

Differential

Disc prolapse (radiculopathy); cauda equina (saddle anaesthesia, bladder/bowel — emergency); vertebral fracture (osteoporosis); infection; malignancy

Management

Paracetamol; physiotherapy; pelvic support belt; acupuncture; gentle exercise; avoid heavy lifting

Drugs

Paracetamol first-line; codeine in short courses; avoid NSAIDs (especially 3rd trimester)

Labour

Consider intra-partum physiotherapy; avoid lithotomy; consider water birth; trial of labour if pain manageable

Postpartum

Most resolve within 6 months; physiotherapy; manual therapy; persistent pain in 10%

Prevention

Preconception core stability; avoid excessive weight gain

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Pelvic girdle pain and low back pain in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Risk factors, Symptoms PGP, Symptoms LBP, Differential. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Pelvic girdle pain and low back pain in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Pelvic girdle pain and low back pain in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Pelvic girdle pain and low back pain in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Cardiovascular Adaptations In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Cardiovascular adaptations to pregnancy include increased blood volume, cardiac output and heart rate, reduced systemic vascular resistance, and a hyperdynamic, vasodilated circulation. Recognising these changes is essential to avoid misinterpreting normal pregnancy findings as pathology.

Core Clinical Content

Cardiac output

Rises 30-50%; peaks 32-34 weeks; HR + stroke volume contributions

Stroke volume

Rises by ~30% in first two trimesters; falls in third trimester as IVC compression occurs

Auscultatory findings

Ejection systolic murmur (90%); S3; wide split S2 are normal

ECG changes

Sinus tachycardia; left axis shift; small Q in III/aVF; ST/T changes acceptable

Peripheral oedema

Common in 80%; due to venous stasis and reduced oncotic pressure

Supine hypotension

IVC compression from ~20 weeks — avoid supine position in late pregnancy

Labour haemodynamics

Cardiac output rises further (300-500 mL per contraction)

Postpartum autotransfusion

Placental return + IVC release; CO peaks immediately postpartum

Preload and afterload

Afterload reduced (low SVR); preload increased (plasma volume)

Clinical implications

Chest pain/dyspnoea at rest, syncope, AF, new MR — all abnormal and need workup

SBAs

  1. Rises 30-50%; peaks 32-34 weeks; HR + stroke volume contributions
  2. Rises by ~30% in first two trimesters; falls in third trimester as IVC compression occurs
  3. Ejection systolic murmur (90%); S3; wide split S2 are normal
  4. Sinus tachycardia; left axis shift; small Q in III/aVF; ST/T changes acceptable
  5. Common in 80%; due to venous stasis and reduced oncotic pressure
  6. IVC compression from ~20 weeks — avoid supine position in late pregnancy
  7. Cardiac output rises further (300-500 mL per contraction)
  8. Placental return + IVC release; CO peaks immediately postpartum
  9. Afterload reduced (low SVR); preload increased (plasma volume)
  10. Chest pain/dyspnoea at rest, syncope, AF, new MR — all abnormal and need workup

topics.title: Haematological Adaptations In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Haematological changes in pregnancy include plasma volume expansion outpacing red cell mass (dilutional anaemia), leucocytosis, thrombocytopenia (gestational), and a prothrombotic shift in coagulation factors. These influence normal reference ranges and threshold for investigation.

Core Clinical Content

Plasma volume

+40-50%; peaks 32 weeks

Red cell mass

+20-30% (greater with iron supplementation)

Physiological anaemia

Hb ~110 g/L (trimester 1) and 105 g/L (trimester 3) is normal

MCV

Rises; mean ~85-95 fL

White cell count

Rises to 9-15 × 10^9/L; neutrophils predominate

Platelets

Mild fall (gestational thrombocytopenia); <100 × 10^9/L needs workup

Coagulation factors

I, II, VII, VIII, IX, X, XII, XIII rise; protein S falls; APC resistance rises

Fibrinolysis

Reduced (PAI-1, PAI-2 rise) — net prothrombotic state

ESR

Markedly elevated; not useful in pregnancy

Clinical implications

VTE risk 4-5x; consider LMWH prophylaxis at delivery

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Plasma volume+40-50%; peaks 32 weeks
Red cell mass+20-30% (greater with iron supplementation)
Physiological anaemiaHb ~110 g/L (trimester 1) and 105 g/L (trimester 3) is normal
MCVRises; mean ~85-95 fL
White cell countRises to 9-15 × 10^9/L; neutrophils predominate
PlateletsMild fall (gestational thrombocytopenia); <100 × 10^9/L needs workup
Coagulation factorsI, II, VII, VIII, IX, X, XII, XIII rise; protein S falls; APC resistance rises
FibrinolysisReduced (PAI-1, PAI-2 rise) — net prothrombotic state
ESRMarkedly elevated; not useful in pregnancy
Clinical implicationsVTE risk 4-5x; consider LMWH prophylaxis at delivery

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Haematological adaptations in pregnancy?

Haematological changes in pregnancy include plasma volume expansion outpacing red cell mass (dilutional anaemia), leucocytosis, thrombocytopenia (gestational), and a prothrombotic shift in coagulation factors. These influence normal reference ranges and threshold for investigation.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Haematological adaptations in pregnancy?

Key features include: Plasma volume, Red cell mass, Physiological anaemia, MCV, White cell count. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Haematological adaptations in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Haematological adaptations in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Haematological adaptations in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Haematological adaptations in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Physiological Changes In Pregnancy Overview
topics.html:
Definition

Comprehensive overview of the multisystem physiological adaptations to pregnancy, including cardiovascular, respiratory, renal, haematological, metabolic, and endocrine changes that distinguish the pregnant state and influence disease presentation, investigation, and management.

Core Clinical Content

Cardiac output

+30-50%; peaks at 32-34 weeks; falls to baseline by 2 weeks postpartum

Plasma volume

+40-50% by term; mediates dilutional 'physiological anaemia'

Heart rate

+10-20 bpm by third trimester

Blood pressure

Falls in 1st/2nd trimesters; nadir at ~20 weeks; returns to pre-pregnancy by term

Systemic vascular resistance

Falls ~30% due to progesterone-mediated vasodilation

Renal blood flow and GFR

+50-60%; creatinine falls (normal pregnancy creatinine <77 μmol/L)

Tidal volume and minute ventilation

+30-50%; respiratory alkalosis with PCO2 ~30 mmHg

Coagulation

Hypercoagulable state; factors I, VII, VIII, IX, X rise; protein S falls

Thyroid-binding globulin

Rises (oestrogen effect); total T4/T3 rise; free T4 unchanged

Drug pharmacokinetics

Increased Vd, GFR, hepatic flow; altered protein binding; clinical dose adjustment

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Renal And Urinary Tract Adaptations In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Renal adaptations in pregnancy include marked increases in renal plasma flow and GFR, leading to lower serum creatinine and urea, glycosuria, and proteinuria. Hydronephrosis (especially right-sided) and ureteric dilatation are common.

Core Clinical Content

Renal plasma flow

+50-80% by mid-pregnancy

GFR

+50%; peaks mid-pregnancy; falls towards term

Serum creatinine

Falls (~35-70 μmol/L); values >77 μmol/L abnormal

Urea

Falls (~1-4 mmol/L); >4.5 mmol/L suggests renal impairment

Uric acid

Falls in 1st/2nd trimesters; rises in pre-eclampsia

Glycosuria

Common (50%) — reduced tubular reabsorption, not necessarily diabetes

Proteinuria

Up to 300 mg/24h normal; >300 mg/24h = significant

Hydronephrosis

Right > left; mechanical obstruction; risk of pyelonephritis

UTI risk

Increased urinary stasis + glycosuria; asymptomatic bacteriuria needs treatment

Drug clearance

Renally cleared drugs (e.g. lithium, digoxin, β-lactams) may need dose adjustment

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Renal and urinary tract adaptations in pregnancy?

Renal adaptations in pregnancy include marked increases in renal plasma flow and GFR, leading to lower serum creatinine and urea, glycosuria, and proteinuria. Hydronephrosis (especially right-sided) and ureteric dilatation are common.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Renal and urinary tract adaptations in pregnancy?

Key features include: Renal plasma flow, GFR, Serum creatinine, Urea, Uric acid. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Renal and urinary tract adaptations in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Renal and urinary tract adaptations in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Renal and urinary tract adaptations in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Chronic Kidney Disease In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Chronic kidney disease in pregnancy poses risks of accelerated maternal renal decline, hypertension, pre-eclampsia, fetal growth restriction, and preterm birth. Preconception counselling and tight BP control are key. Pregnancy is generally avoided if creatinine >180 μmol/L (CKD 3b-5).

Core Clinical Content

Definitions

CKD stages 1-5 by GFR; pregnancy generally avoided if Cr >180 μmol/L or GFR <30

Preconception

Optimise BP; switch ACEI/ARB; review immunosuppression; counsel on outcomes

Maternal risks

Worsening renal function (especially Cr >125 μmol/L); pre-eclampsia (40-80%); hypertension

Fetal risks

IUGR; preterm birth; stillbirth; miscarriage; neonatal morbidity

CKD stage prognosis

Stage 1-2: 90-95% live birth; Stage 3: 70-90%; Stage 4-5: 30-50%

BP control

Labetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa; target <130/80; avoid ACEI/ARB

Monitoring

U&E 4-6 weekly; urate; urine PCR; growth scans; umbilical artery Doppler; anaemia management (EPO)

Immunosuppression

Azathioprine, prednisolone, calcineurin inhibitors safe; MMF teratogenic — switch to azathioprine pre-conception

Delivery

Vaginal preferred; consider induction at 37 weeks; ITU availability

Postpartum

Monitor renal recovery; some never return to baseline; future pregnancy counselling

topics.title: Glomerulonephritis In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Glomerulonephritis (GN) in pregnancy — including IgA nephropathy, membranous nephropathy, focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), and lupus nephritis — requires careful management. Pregnancy is generally safe with preserved renal function but carries risks of disease progression, hypertension, and adverse fetal outcomes.

Core Clinical Content

Preconception

Stable renal function >1-2 years; creatinine <125 μmol/L; proteinuria <1 g/24h; BP controlled; switch teratogens (MMF → azathioprine)

IgA nephropathy

Most common primary GN; often well tolerated if Cr <125; pre-eclampsia risk increased

Membranous nephropathy

Nephrotic syndrome; risk of VTE; ACEI/ARB pre-conception; monitor

FSGS

Risk of disease progression; nephrotic-range proteinuria worsens outcomes

Lupus nephritis (class III/IV/V)

Active disease at conception → 50% flare; quiescent for 6 months → 5-10% flare; careful MDT

Maternal risks

Worsening renal function; hypertension; pre-eclampsia; nephrotic syndrome; VTE; infection; CKD progression

Fetal risks

Preterm; IUGR; miscarriage; stillbirth; neonatal complications of prematurity

Investigations

Monthly U&E; urinalysis; urine PCR; 24-h proteinuria; anti-dsDNA, C3/C4 (lupus); anti-PLA2R (membranous); complement

Management

Treat hypertension (labetalol, nifedipine, methyldopa); avoid ACEI/ARB; steroids; azathioprine; calcineurin inhibitors; LMWH if nephrotic

Postpartum

Resume ACEI/ARB; review immunosuppression; breastfeeding; contraception (avoid oestrogen if high VTE risk)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Glomerulonephritis in pregnancy?

Glomerulonephritis (GN) in pregnancy — including IgA nephropathy, membranous nephropathy, focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), and lupus nephritis — requires careful management. Pregnancy is generally safe with preserved renal function but carries risks of disease progression, hypertension, and adverse fetal outcomes.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Glomerulonephritis in pregnancy?

Key features include: Preconception, IgA nephropathy, Membranous nephropathy, FSGS, Lupus nephritis (class III/IV/V). These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Glomerulonephritis in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Glomerulonephritis in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Glomerulonephritis in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Renal Stones In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Renal stone disease (nephrolithiasis) in pregnancy affects 1 in 1,500 pregnancies. Physiological hydronephrosis and stasis of pregnancy increase risk. Stones may present with flank pain, haematuria, or infection. Most pass spontaneously; management is supportive with intervention reserved for obstruction, infection, or intractable pain.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

1 in 1,500 pregnancies; similar to non-pregnant; right > left

Composition

Calcium oxalate (75%); calcium phosphate; uric acid; struvite; cystine

Risk factors

Personal/family history; dehydration; high oxalate diet; hyperparathyroidism; gout; UTI; IBD; bariatric surgery

Presentation

Severe flank pain radiating to groin; haematuria (90%); nausea/vomiting; dysuria; may mimic abruption, appendicitis, pyelonephritis

Investigations

Urine dipstick + culture; USS (first-line; safe; hydronephrosis common); MRI urogram if needed; low-dose CT rarely

Differential

Pyelonephritis; appendicitis; placental abruption; ovarian torsion; ectopic; AAA (older)

Management conservative

Hydration; analgesia (paracetamol; codeine; morphine; avoid NSAIDs especially in 3rd trimester); tamsulosin (medical expulsive)

Indications for intervention

Obstruction with infection; bilateral obstruction; solitary kidney; intractable pain/vomiting; declining renal function

Interventions

Ureteric stent (PCN if stenting fails); ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy (in experienced hands); ESWL contraindicated

Delivery

Not affected unless obstructed in labour; vaginal preferred; monitor renal function

A: Pyelonephritis; appendicitis; placental abruption; ovarian torsion; ectopic; AAA (older)

Q: Management conservative?

A: Hydration; analgesia (paracetamol; codeine; morphine; avoid NSAIDs especially in 3rd trimester); tamsulosin (medical expulsive)

Q: Indications for intervention?

A: Obstruction with infection; bilateral obstruction; solitary kidney; intractable pain/vomiting; declining renal function

  1. 1 in 1,500 pregnancies; similar to non-pregnant; right > left
  2. Calcium oxalate (75%); calcium phosphate; uric acid; struvite; cystine
  3. Personal/family history; dehydration; high oxalate diet; hyperparathyroidism; gout; UTI; IBD; bariatric surgery
  4. Severe flank pain radiating to groin; haematuria (90%); nausea/vomiting; dysuria; may mimic abruption, appendicitis, pyelonephritis
  5. Urine dipstick + culture; USS (first-line; safe; hydronephrosis common); MRI urogram if needed; low-dose CT rarely
  6. Pyelonephritis; appendicitis; placental abruption; ovarian torsion; ectopic; AAA (older)
  7. Hydration; analgesia (paracetamol; codeine; morphine; avoid NSAIDs especially in 3rd trimester); tamsulosin (medical expulsive)
  8. Obstruction with infection; bilateral obstruction; solitary kidney; intractable pain/vomiting; declining renal function
  9. Ureteric stent (PCN if stenting fails); ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy (in experienced hands); ESWL contraindicated
  10. Not affected unless obstructed in labour; vaginal preferred; monitor renal function

topics.title: Renal Transplant And Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Pregnancy in renal transplant recipients is achievable with stable graft function but requires multidisciplinary care. Optimal outcomes require stable graft function for >1-2 years, no recent rejection, baseline creatinine <125 μmol/L, and stable immunosuppression.

Core Clinical Content

Eligibility

Stable graft >1-2 years; Cr <125 μmol/L; proteinuria <0.5 g/24h; no recent rejection

Maternal risks

Pre-eclampsia (30-50%); infection; graft rejection (10%); deterioration of renal function

Fetal risks

Preterm birth; IUGR; low birth weight; neonatal immunosuppression

Safe immunosuppression

Calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, ciclosporin); azathioprine; prednisolone — all pregnancy-compatible

Avoid in pregnancy

Mycophenolate (teratogenic — switch pre-conception); mTOR inhibitors (sirolimus, everolimus)

Monitoring

Monthly U&E; trough tacrolimus levels; proteinuria; CMV/ BK viral load; growth scans

BP control

Labetalol; nifedipine; target <130/80

Delivery

Vaginal if obstetrically suitable; caesarean if pelvic kidney obstructing birth canal

Postpartum

Continue immunosuppression; breastfeeding generally safe; re-introduce MMF after weaning

Contraception

Avoid oestrogen (VTE/immunosuppression); progesterone-only or IUD preferred

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Pregnancy in renal transplant recipients is achievable with stable graft function but requires multidisciplinary care. Optimal outcomes require stable graft function for >1-2 years, no recent rejection, baseline creatinine <125 μmol/L, and stable immunosuppression.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Renal transplant and pregnancy?

Key features include: Eligibility, Maternal risks, Fetal risks, Safe immunosuppression, Avoid in pregnancy. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Renal transplant and pregnancy diagnosed?

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Renal transplant and pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

topics.title: Urinary Tract Infection In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Urinary tract infection in pregnancy is common due to urinary stasis, glycosuria, and altered immunity. Asymptomatic bacteriuria (2-10%) must be treated because it frequently progresses to pyelonephritis (20-40%), with risk of preterm labour and low birth weight.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

Asymptomatic bacteriuria 2-10%; cystitis 1-4%; pyelonephritis 1-2%

Pathogens

E. coli (75-90%); Klebsiella, Proteus, group B Strep, Enterococcus

Asymptomatic bacteriuria

10^5 CFU/mL on MSU — must be treated even without symptoms

Cystitis

Dysuria, frequency, urgency, suprapubic pain; no fever or systemic features

Pyelonephritis

Fever, flank pain, nausea, vomiting; can cause sepsis, preterm labour, ARDS

Investigations

MSU culture; treat empirically in pyelonephritis; blood cultures if febrile

Treatment ASB/cystitis

Nitrofurantoin (avoid near term), cephalexin, amoxicillin — 7 days

Pyelonephritis

IV ceftriaxone or co-amoxiclav; admit; IV fluids; switch to oral when afebrile 24-48 h

Drug safety

Avoid: trimethoprim (1st trimester — neural tube defects), quinolones, tetracyclines

Recurrence

Monthly MSU until delivery; prophylactic nitrofurantoin or cephalexin if recurrent

A: Asymptomatic bacteriuria 2-10%; cystitis 1-4%; pyelonephritis 1-2%

Q: Pathogens?

A: E. coli (75-90%); Klebsiella, Proteus, group B Strep, Enterococcus

Q: Asymptomatic bacteriuria?

A: >10^5 CFU/mL on MSU — must be treated even without symptoms

Q: Cystitis?

A: Dysuria, frequency, urgency, suprapubic pain; no fever or systemic features

Q: Pyelonephritis?

A: Fever, flank pain, nausea, vomiting; can cause sepsis, preterm labour, ARDS

Q: Investigations?

A: MSU culture; treat empirically in pyelonephritis; blood cultures if febrile

Q: Treatment ASB/cystitis?

A: Nitrofurantoin (avoid near term), cephalexin, amoxicillin — 7 days

Q: Pyelonephritis?

A: IV ceftriaxone or co-amoxiclav; admit; IV fluids; switch to oral when afebrile 24-48 h

topics.title: Asthma In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Asthma is the most common chronic respiratory disease in pregnancy, affecting 4-8% of women. Control may improve, worsen, or stay the same; poorly controlled asthma poses greater risk to mother and fetus than asthma medications.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

4-8% of pregnancies; most common chronic respiratory condition

Course

~30% improve; ~30% worsen; ~40% unchanged — variable, monitor closely

Maternal risks (uncontrolled)

Pre-eclampsia; GDM; preterm birth; caesarean; pneumonia

Fetal risks (uncontrolled)

IUGR; low birth weight; stillbirth; neonatal hypoxia

Safe medications

Inhaled SABA (salbutamol); ICS (budesonide safe); LABA; theophylline; oral steroids if needed

Avoid

Uncontrolled symptoms > medications; leukotriene antagonists data limited but not contraindicated

Acute severe asthma

Same management as non-pregnant — oxygen, SABA, systemic steroids, magnesium

Monitoring

Peak expiratory flow; symptom diary; consider FeNO; review every 4 weeks

Labour and delivery

Continue inhaled therapy; SABA if needed; epidural analgesia preferred

Postpartum

Continue medications; breastfeeding compatible; monitor infant for steroid exposure if high-dose oral

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q1. What is the definition of Asthma in pregnancy?

Asthma is the most common chronic respiratory disease in pregnancy, affecting 4-8% of women. Control may improve, worsen, or stay the same; poorly controlled asthma poses greater risk to mother and fetus than asthma medications.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Asthma in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Course, Maternal risks (uncontrolled), Fetal risks (uncontrolled), Safe medications. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Asthma in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Asthma in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Asthma in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Cystic Fibrosis In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Cystic fibrosis (CF) in pregnancy is increasingly common as life expectancy improves (now >40 years). Pre-conception optimisation and multidisciplinary care are essential. Pregnancy is usually well tolerated in well-nourished women with good lung function (FEV1 >50-60% predicted) but carries significant risk if lung disease is advanced.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

CF carriers 1 in 25 (Caucasians); affected patients now survive to reproductive age

Preconception

Optimise lung function (FEV1 >60%); nutritional status (BMI >20); screen for CFRD; genetic counselling; partner screening; review medications

Fertility

Women with CF — normal fertility (thick cervical mucus may be issue); men — 98% azoospermia (CBAVD)

Maternal risks

Worsening lung function; cor pulmonale; malnutrition; CFRD; CFRD-related complications; infection (Pseudomonas, Staph aureus)

Fetal risks

IUGR; preterm birth; low birth weight; congenital malformations (CF if partner carrier)

Antenatal care

MDT (respiratory, obstetric, CF, dietician, physio); monthly spirometry; weight; sputum culture; CFTR modulators (limited data; case-by-case)

Treatment

Continue airway clearance; nebulisers (safe); dornase alfa; azithromycin; IV antibiotics for exacerbations; insulin for CFRD; pancreatic enzymes; vitamins (ADEK)

Drug safety

Avoid tetracyclines; fluoroquinolones (limited data); aminoglycosides (ototoxic); CFTR modulators — limited data, consider benefit

Delivery

Vaginal preferred; assisted second stage (avoid exhaustion); consider epidural; high-risk anaesthesia review

Prognosis

Pregnancy safe if FEV1 >60%; 10-20% decline in FEV1 may be permanent in advanced disease; 80%+ live birth rate

Q: Epidemiology?

A: CF carriers 1 in 25 (Caucasians); affected patients now survive to reproductive age

Q: Preconception?

A: Optimise lung function (FEV1 >60%); nutritional status (BMI >20); screen for CFRD; genetic counselling; partner screening; review medications

Q: Fertility?

A: Women with CF — normal fertility (thick cervical mucus may be issue); men — 98% azoospermia (CBAVD)

Q: Maternal risks?

A: Worsening lung function; cor pulmonale; malnutrition; CFRD; CFRD-related complications; infection (Pseudomonas, Staph aureus)

Q: Fetal risks?

A: IUGR; preterm birth; low birth weight; congenital malformations (CF if partner carrier)

Q: Antenatal care?

A: MDT (respiratory, obstetric, CF, dietician, physio); monthly spirometry; weight; sputum culture; CFTR modulators (limited data; case-by-case)

Q: Treatment?

A: Continue airway clearance; nebulisers (safe); dornase alfa; azithromycin; IV antibiotics for exacerbations; insulin for CFRD; pancreatic enzymes; vitamins (ADEK)

Q: Drug safety?

A: Avoid tetracyclines; fluoroquinolones (limited data); aminoglycosides (ototoxic); CFTR modulators — limited data, consider benefit

topics.title: Pneumonia In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Pneumonia in pregnancy is a leading non-obstetric cause of maternal mortality. Physiological changes (raised diaphragm, reduced functional residual capacity, altered immunity) increase susceptibility and severity. Prompt empirical antibiotics, oxygen, and supportive care are essential.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

0.5-1.5% of pregnancies; leading non-obstetric infection

Pathogens

Strep pneumoniae; H. influenzae; atypicals (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia); Legionella; viral (influenza, COVID-19)

Risk factors

Asthma; anaemia; GDM; HIV; immunosuppression; smoking; substance misuse

Presentation

Fever, productive cough, dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, tachypnoea, hypoxia

Investigations

CXR (safe, lead shield); blood cultures; sputum culture; urinary antigens; viral PCR

Empirical therapy

Co-amoxiclav + clarithromycin IV; or ceftriaxone + azithromycin

Severe disease

Oxygen to maintain SpO2 ≥94%; consider CPAP; ITU/HDU; early delivery if deteriorating

Influenza

Treat with oseltamivir within 48 h of symptoms

Complications

ARDS; empyema; lung abscess; preterm labour; fetal loss

Prevention

Influenza and pneumococcal vaccination preconception; smoking cessation

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Pneumonia in pregnancy is a leading non-obstetric cause of maternal mortality. Physiological changes (raised diaphragm, reduced functional residual capacity, altered immunity) increase susceptibility and severity. Prompt empirical antibiotics, oxygen, and supportive care are essential.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Pneumonia in pregnancy?

Key features include: Incidence, Pathogens, Risk factors, Presentation, Investigations. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Pneumonia in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Pneumonia in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Pneumonia in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Sarcoidosis In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Sarcoidosis is a multisystem granulomatous disease that commonly affects young women. Pregnancy often improves symptoms (immunomodulation), but advanced disease with pulmonary fibrosis, cardiac, or neurological involvement carries significant risk. Management is usually supportive; most medications are safe.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

Commoner in women; peak incidence 20-40 years; often stable in pregnancy

Effect of pregnancy

Symptoms often improve (rising cortisol, Th2 shift); flares postpartum

Maternal risks

Worsening if advanced fibrosis; cor pulmonale; cardiac sarcoid (arrhythmia, heart block); neurological (cranial nerve palsies, seizures); hypercalcaemia; uveitis

Fetal risks

Minimal if mild disease; preterm and LBW if severe; neonatal hypercalcaemia if mother hypercalcaemic

Investigations

CXR (with shielding); spirometry; CT chest if needed; serum ACE (less reliable in pregnancy); serum calcium; LFTs; ECG; echo if cardiac

Biopsy

Often unnecessary if clear clinical picture; defer to postpartum; or specific tissue if needed (lymph node)

Management

Observation if asymptomatic; corticosteroids for active disease; immunosuppressants if severe; hydroxychloroquine for skin/eye

Drugs safe

Prednisolone; azathioprine; hydroxychloroquine; infliximab (limited data)

Avoid

Methotrexate (teratogenic); cyclophosphamide; avoid vitamin D supplementation in those with hypercalcaemia

Postpartum

Continue treatment; monitor for flare; contraception (avoid oestrogen if hypercoagulable state)

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Sarcoidosis in pregnancy?

topics.title: Tuberculosis In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Tuberculosis in pregnancy, both pulmonary and extrapulmonary, requires prompt diagnosis and treatment. Active TB poses greater risk to the pregnancy than the standard anti-TB regimen, which is largely safe.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

Highest in low/middle-income countries; latent TB common

Maternal risks

Active TB: ↑preterm birth, low birth weight, pre-eclampsia, maternal mortality

Diagnosis

Sputum AFB + culture + NAAT; CXR (with shielding); TST/IGRA; if extrapulmonary, biopsy/aspiration

Treatment of active TB

Standard RIF + INH + PZA + EMB for 2 months, then RIF + INH for 7 months

Drug safety

Rifampicin, isoniazid, ethambutol — all safe; pyrazinamide safe

Avoid

Streptomycin (ototoxic to fetus); second-line drugs limited data

Latent TB

Treat postpartum usually; if high-risk recent exposure, treat in pregnancy with INH + pyridoxine 9 months

BCG

Contraindicated in pregnancy; give to neonate at birth if mother has active TB

Multidrug-resistant TB

Individualised; expert input; bedaquiline, linezolid — limited safety data

Postpartum and breastfeeding

Continue treatment; breastfeeding safe with non-infectious TB; mother-infant separation only if active pulmonary

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Tuberculosis in pregnancy?

Tuberculosis in pregnancy, both pulmonary and extrapulmonary, requires prompt diagnosis and treatment. Active TB poses greater risk to the pregnancy than the standard anti-TB regimen, which is largely safe.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Tuberculosis in pregnancy?

Key features include: Epidemiology, Maternal risks, Diagnosis, Treatment of active TB, Drug safety. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Tuberculosis in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Tuberculosis in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Tuberculosis in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Tuberculosis in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.hub_name: Sexual and Reproductive Health in Pregnancy
topics.title: Contraception Postpartum
topics.html:
Definition

Postpartum contraception should be discussed antenatally and initiated before hospital discharge. Breastfeeding, VTE risk, and future fertility influence choice. Lactational amenorrhoea is effective only if exclusively breastfeeding and amenorrhoeic.

Core Clinical Content

Counselling

Discuss antenatally; document plan; offer before discharge; consider breastfeeding, VTE, future fertility

Lactational amenorrhoea

98% effective if <6 months, exclusive breastfeeding, amenorrhoeic; LARC fails if any of these relax

Postpartum timing

Ovulation can return at 6 weeks (earlier in non-breastfeeding); contraception needed from 21 days

Combined hormonal contraception (CHC)

Avoid <21 days; if VTE risk factors, avoid <42 days; not generally recommended in breastfeeding <6 months

Progestogen-only pill (POP)

Safe from 21 days; breastfeeding compatible; desogestrel POP preferred for efficacy

Injectable (DMPA)

Safe; breastfeeding compatible; delayed return of fertility (6-12 months)

Implant (Nexplanon)

Safe; breastfeeding compatible; quick to insert; lasts 3 years

IUD (copper)

Insertion 4-6 weeks postpartum; safe; non-hormonal; lasts 10 years; increased bleeding

IUS (Mirena)

Insertion 4-6 weeks postpartum (or at caesarean); safe; breastfeeding; reduced bleeding; lasts 8 years (off-licence 5)

Sterilisation

Female sterilisation at caesarean or laparoscopy; male vasectomy ideal; counsel carefully — regret 5-10%

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Pruritus And Specific Dermatoses In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Pruritus in pregnancy has many causes, including specific pregnancy dermatoses (polymorphic eruption of pregnancy, pemphigoid gestationis, intrahepatic cholestasis), eczema, and systemic disease. Recognition of the specific dermatosis guides management and prognosis.

Core Clinical Content

Polymorphic eruption of pregnancy (PEP/PUPPP)

Late pregnancy; abdominal striae; urticarial papules and plaques; no maternal/fetal risk; resolves postpartum

Pemphigoid gestationis (HG)

Autoimmune BP180 antibodies; blistering; periumbilical; risk of preterm, SGA; neonatal blistering

Intrahepatic cholestasis

Severe pruritus; no rash; raised bile acids; see Liver chapter

Atopic eruption of pregnancy (AEP)

Eczema in pregnancy; face, neck, flexures; topical steroids; safe

Prurigo of pregnancy

Excoriated papules; limbs; no maternal/fetal risk; topical steroids

Investigations

LFT, bile acids (rule out ICP); skin biopsy if bullae; direct immunofluorescence for HG

Treatment PEP/AEP

Topical steroids; antihistamines; usually resolves within weeks postpartum

Treatment HG

Topical/oral steroids; prednisolone; cyclosporin if severe; monitor fetal growth

Other skin changes

Melasma; linea nigra; striae; spider naevi; palmar erythema — normal

Postpartum

Most resolve; HG may flare postpartum; recurrence with COC, menses, subsequent pregnancy

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q1. What is the definition of Pruritus and specific dermatoses in pregnancy?

Pruritus in pregnancy has many causes, including specific pregnancy dermatoses (polymorphic eruption of pregnancy, pemphigoid gestationis, intrahepatic cholestasis), eczema, and systemic disease. Recognition of the specific dermatosis guides management and prognosis.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Pruritus and specific dermatoses in pregnancy?

Key features include: Polymorphic eruption of pregnancy (PEP/PUPPP), Pemphigoid gestationis (HG), Intrahepatic cholestasis, Atopic eruption of pregnancy (AEP), Prurigo of pregnancy. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Pruritus and specific dermatoses in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Pruritus and specific dermatoses in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Pruritus and specific dermatoses in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Antiphospholipid Syndrome In Pregnancy Obstetric
topics.html:
Definition

Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) is a leading cause of recurrent pregnancy loss and is associated with late fetal loss, pre-eclampsia, IUGR, and thrombosis. Diagnosis requires persistent aPL antibodies and clinical events; treatment with aspirin ± LMWH improves outcomes.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Persistent aPL antibodies + clinical event (thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity)

Antibodies

Lupus anticoagulant; anticardiolipin; anti-β2 glycoprotein I — confirm 12 weeks apart

Obstetric morbidity criteria

≥3 consecutive <10-week losses; ≥1 ≥10-week loss; preterm <34 weeks with PE/placental insufficiency

Pathophysiology

Placental thrombosis + inflammation; impaired trophoblast invasion; complement activation

Maternal risks

VTE; pre-eclampsia; HELLP; placental abruption; stroke

Fetal risks

Miscarriage (early and late); IUGR; stillbirth; preterm birth

Treatment

Low-dose aspirin 75-150 mg + prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy

High-risk (prior thrombosis/lupus)

Therapeutic LMWH dose; consider warfarin postpartum for life

Monitoring

Uterine artery Doppler 20-24 weeks; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance

Postpartum

Continue LMWH 6 weeks; lifelong if thrombosis; preconception counselling for future

topics.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) in pregnancy often improves due to immunomodulation (Th2 shift), but flares occur postpartum in 50-70%. Active disease at conception increases risk of preterm birth and low birth weight. Most medications (sulfasalazine, hydroxychloroquine, low-dose steroids) are safe; methotrexate and leflunomide are teratogenic.

Core Clinical Content

Effect of pregnancy on RA

50-75% improve (esp. 1st and 2nd trimesters); 50% flare postpartum; hormonal and immunomodulatory factors

Effect of RA on pregnancy

Active disease → preterm, LBW, pre-eclampsia; quiescent → generally good outcomes

Preconception

Achieve remission 3-6 months; switch teratogens (MTX stop 3 months, leflunomide cholestyramine washout); review biologics

Safe drugs

Sulfasalazine (with folate); hydroxychloroquine (continue); azathioprine; low-dose prednisolone; certolizumab (limited placental transfer — safe); other anti-TNF generally safe until 3rd trimester

Avoid

Methotrexate (teratogenic); leflunomide (teratogenic); cyclophosphamide; baricitinib; JAK inhibitors (limited data)

Biologics

Anti-TNF (adalimumab, infliximab, etanercept) — generally safe; discontinue in 3rd trimester if possible to avoid neonatal immunosuppression; live vaccines contraindicated in exposed infants <6 months

Monitoring

Disease activity (DAS28); FBC; U&E; LFT; trough levels if on biologic; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance

Postpartum

Flare common; resume medications; breastfeeding compatible with sulfasalazine, HCQ, azathioprine, prednisolone; avoid MTX; contraception

Anaesthesia

Difficult intubation if cricoarytenoid involvement; careful airway assessment if C-section

Long-term

Joint damage during pregnancy limited; functional outcomes good; preconception counselling important

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Rheumatoid arthritis in pregnancy?

Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) in pregnancy often improves due to immunomodulation (Th2 shift), but flares occur postpartum in 50-70%. Active disease at conception increases risk of preterm birth and low birth weight. Most medications (sulfasalazine, hydroxychloroquine, low-dose steroids) are safe; methotrexate and leflunomide are teratogenic.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Rheumatoid arthritis in pregnancy?

Key features include: Effect of pregnancy on RA, Effect of RA on pregnancy, Preconception, Safe drugs, Avoid. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Rheumatoid arthritis in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Rheumatoid arthritis in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Rheumatoid arthritis in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Rheumatoid arthritis in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Scleroderma In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Scleroderma (systemic sclerosis) in pregnancy is uncommon as disease often affects older women, but it does occur. Limited cutaneous disease has better outcomes; diffuse cutaneous disease with renal crisis risk, pulmonary hypertension, and severe organ involvement carries high maternal mortality. Multidisciplinary care is essential.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

F:M 3-4:1; usually perimenopausal; F childbearing years have worse disease

Types

Limited cutaneous (CREST — old definition; better prognosis); diffuse cutaneous (rapid skin, internal organ); systemic sclerosis overlap

Effect of pregnancy

Disease often stable; renal crisis risk (especially diffuse + abrupt BP rise); cardiopulmonary complications dangerous

Maternal risks

Renal crisis (scleroderma renal crisis — malignant HTN, AKI, microangiopathic haemolysis — 5-10% mortality); PAH crisis; PPH (poor wound healing; uterine vessels affected)

Fetal risks

IUGR; preterm; miscarriage; stillbirth; small for gestational age; placental insufficiency

Preconception

Stable disease ≥3-5 years; echo (PAH, LV); PFTs; renal function; switch MMF to azathioprine; avoid teratogens

Monitoring

Monthly BP, U&E, urine; growth scans; umbilical artery Doppler; echo 2nd/3rd trimester; review symptoms

Treatment

Continue nifedipine; ACE inhibitors for renal crisis (despite pregnancy; life-saving); proton pump inhibitors; iloprost for severe Raynaud's; steroids

Renal crisis management

Captopril or enalapril IV (off-label but life-saving); control BP urgently; ITU; avoid NSAIDs

Delivery and postpartum

Vaginal preferred; assisted second stage; avoid ergometrine; PPH risk; breastfeeding considerations

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q1. What is the definition of Scleroderma in pregnancy?

Scleroderma (systemic sclerosis) in pregnancy is uncommon as disease often affects older women, but it does occur. Limited cutaneous disease has better outcomes; diffuse cutaneous disease with renal crisis risk, pulmonary hypertension, and severe organ involvement carries high maternal mortality. Multidisciplinary care is essential.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Scleroderma in pregnancy?

Key features include: Epidemiology, Types, Effect of pregnancy, Maternal risks, Fetal risks. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Scleroderma in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Scleroderma in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Scleroderma in pregnancy?

topics.title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus Sle In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Systemic lupus erythematosus affects women in childbearing years and is a leading cause of maternal mortality. Pregnancy is safe in quiescent disease but flares (especially renal) and increased rates of pre-eclampsia, miscarriage, preterm birth, and fetal loss are seen.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

F:M = 9:1; common in 20s-40s; affects 1 in 1,000 pregnancies

Preconception

Disease quiescent >6 months; renal function stable; switch MMF to azathioprine; stop ACEI

Maternal risks

Flare (esp. renal); pre-eclampsia (20-30%); thrombosis; infection; hypertension

Fetal risks

Miscarriage; IUGR; preterm birth; stillbirth; neonatal lupus; congenital heart block (anti-Ro/La)

Anti-Ro/La antibodies

Risk of neonatal lupus (1-2%) and congenital heart block (1-2%); serial fetal echocardiography 16-34 weeks

Monitoring

Monthly U&E, urinalysis, anti-dsDNA, complement (C3/C4); growth scans; umbilical artery Doppler

Treatment

Hydroxychloroquine continued (reduces flares); azathioprine; prednisolone; aspirin 75-150 mg; LMWH if APS

Avoid

Cyclophosphamide (1st trimester); MMF; methotrexate; ACEI/ARB

Lupus nephritis flare

Often mimics pre-eclampsia; treat with steroids/azathioprine; check anti-dsDNA/complement

Postpartum

Restart MMF; breastfeeding compatible with HCQ/azathioprine/prednisolone; contraception

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy?

Systemic lupus erythematosus affects women in childbearing years and is a leading cause of maternal mortality. Pregnancy is safe in quiescent disease but flares (especially renal) and increased rates of pre-eclampsia, miscarriage, preterm birth, and fetal loss are seen.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Preconception, Maternal risks, Fetal risks, Anti-Ro/La antibodies. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Gestational Diabetes Mellitus Gdm
topics.html:
Definition

Gestational diabetes mellitus is glucose intolerance first recognised during pregnancy that is not overt pre-existing diabetes. It typically resolves after delivery but confers long-term maternal risk of type 2 diabetes and fetal risk of macrosomia and metabolic disease.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Glucose intolerance first recognised in pregnancy; excludes overt pre-pregnational diabetes

Epidemiology

Affects 5-18% of pregnancies; rising with obesity

Risk factors

BMI >30, age >25, prior GDM, family history, prior macrosomic baby, PCOS, ethnicity (South Asian)

Screening

Universal at 24-28 weeks (NICE); 2-step 50 g GCT → 100 g OGTT or 1-step 75 g OGTT

Diagnostic OGTT (IADPSG)

Fasting ≥5.1; 1-h ≥10.0; 2-h ≥8.5 mmol/L — one abnormal value = GDM

Complications

Macrosomia; shoulder dystocia; pre-eclampsia; caesarean; neonatal hypoglycaemia; stillbirth

Management

Diet and exercise first-line; metformin or insulin if glycaemic targets not met

Targets

Fasting <5.3; 1-h <7.8; 2-h <6.4 mmol/L

Fetal monitoring

Growth scans 28, 32, 36 weeks; daily fetal movement count from 28 weeks

Postpartum

75 g OGTT at 6-12 weeks; 50% progress to T2DM over 5-10 years; lifestyle

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM)?

Gestational diabetes mellitus is glucose intolerance first recognised during pregnancy that is not overt pre-existing diabetes. It typically resolves after delivery but confers long-term maternal risk of type 2 diabetes and fetal risk of macrosomia and metabolic disease.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM)?

Key features include: Definition, Epidemiology, Risk factors, Screening, Diagnostic OGTT (IADPSG). These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM)?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM)?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Pre Existing Diabetes In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Pre-existing (overt) diabetes in pregnancy refers to type 1 or type 2 diabetes present before conception. Pregnancy is associated with increased insulin resistance and risk of complications; preconception optimisation and tight glycaemic control are essential.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

1-2% of pregnancies; rising with obesity and T2DM

Preconception

HbA1c <6.5% (ideally <48 mmol/mol); folic acid 5 mg; switch to insulin; review drugs

Risks to mother

Miscarriage; pre-eclampsia (2x); infection; caesarean; DKA; hypoglycaemia

Risks to fetus

Congenital malformation (4-10%); macrosomia; IUGR (T1DM with vasculopathy); stillbirth; preterm; neonatal hypoglycaemia

Fetal surveillance

Anomaly scan 18-20 weeks; growth scans 28, 32, 36 weeks

Glycaemic targets

Fasting <5.3 mmol/L; 1-h postprandial <7.8 mmol/L; 2-h <6.4 mmol/L

Insulin

First-line; rapid-acting (aspart, lispro) + NPH; basal-bolus; CSII pumps

Metformin

Can continue; crosses placenta; long-term offspring data reassuring

Labour

IV insulin + dextrose sliding scale; target glucose 4-7 mmol/L

Postpartum

Insulin sensitivity rises immediately — reduce insulin by ~50% on day 1

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Preconception, Risks to mother, Risks to fetus, Fetal surveillance. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Pre-existing diabetes in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

SBAs

  1. 1-2% of pregnancies; rising with obesity and T2DM
  2. HbA1c <6.5% (ideally <48 mmol/mol); folic acid 5 mg; switch to insulin; review drugs
  3. Miscarriage; pre-eclampsia (2x); infection; caesarean; DKA; hypoglycaemia
  4. Congenital malformation (4-10%); macrosomia; IUGR (T1DM with vasculopathy); stillbirth; preterm; neonatal hypoglycaemia
  5. Anomaly scan 18-20 weeks; growth scans 28, 32, 36 weeks
  6. Fasting <5.3 mmol/L; 1-h postprandial <7.8 mmol/L; 2-h <6.4 mmol/L
  7. First-line; rapid-acting (aspart, lispro) + NPH; basal-bolus; CSII pumps
  8. Can continue; crosses placenta; long-term offspring data reassuring
  9. IV insulin + dextrose sliding scale; target glucose 4-7 mmol/L
  10. Insulin sensitivity rises immediately — reduce insulin by ~50% on day 1

topics.title: Drug Safety In Breastfeeding
topics.html:
Definition

Most drugs are compatible with breastfeeding; few are absolute contraindications. Assess risk-benefit, use lowest dose, schedule feeds around peak drug levels, and consult LactMed or UKDILAS.

Core Clinical Content

Principles

Most drugs safe; few absolute contraindications; assess risk-benefit; monitor infant

Safe analgesics

Paracetamol; ibuprofen; codeine in short courses (caution CYP2D6 ultra-rapid metabolisers)

Safe antibiotics

Penicillins, cephalosporins, erythromycin, azithromycin, nitrofurantoin

Avoid

Cytotoxic drugs (chemotherapy); radioisotopes (withhold breastfeeding for 5 half-lives); ergot alkaloids; chloramphenicol (neonate grey baby)

Caution

Codeine; tramadol; benzodiazepines (accumulation, sedation); lithium (monitor infant levels); lamotrigine (monitor); SSRIs (sertraline preferred)

Safe antihypertensives

Labetalol, nifedipine, enalapril (small amounts), methyldopa

Safe antiepileptics

Lamotrigine, levetiracetam, valproate (avoid — developmental delay); carbamazepine; monitor infant

Safe anticoagulants

LMWH, warfarin (small amount, monitor INR); DOACs avoid (limited data)

Drug entry into milk

Lipid-soluble, low molecular weight, low protein-bound drugs cross more readily

Resources

LactMed (NIH); UKDILAS; BNF 'Breast feeding' appendix

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Drug safety in breastfeeding?

Most drugs are compatible with breastfeeding; few are absolute contraindications. Assess risk-benefit, use lowest dose, schedule feeds around peak drug levels, and consult LactMed or UKDILAS.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Drug safety in breastfeeding?

Key features include: Principles, Safe analgesics, Safe antibiotics, Avoid, Caution. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Drug safety in breastfeeding diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Drug safety in breastfeeding?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Drug safety in breastfeeding?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Drug safety in breastfeeding — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Drug Safety In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Drug use in pregnancy requires balancing maternal benefit with fetal risk. Most drugs cross the placenta; the first trimester carries the highest teratogenic risk. Use the lowest effective dose, avoid new drugs where possible, and consult specific resources.

Core Clinical Content

Principles

Lowest effective dose; minimal duration; avoid new drugs; consider non-pharmacological; benefit > risk

FDA categories (legacy)

A: safe; B: animal safe; C: risk can't be ruled out; D: evidence of risk; X: contraindicated (B3 UK)

Safe drugs (common)

Paracetamol; penicillins/cephalosporins; labetalol/nifedipine/methyldopa; levothyroxine; insulin; aciclovir; ondansetron

Teratogenic

Valproate (NTD, neurodevelopment); carbimazole (aplasia cutis); ACEI/ARB (renal); warfarin (1st trimester); methotrexate; retinoids; misoprostol; thalidomide; lithium (cardiac)

First-trimester risks

Organogenesis; congenital malformations; avoid all unnecessary drugs

Late-pregnancy risks

NSAIDs (closure of ductus, oligohydramnios); ACEI/ARB; opioids (neonatal abstinence); benzodiazepines (floppy baby)

Antimicrobials safe

Penicillins, cephalosporins, erythromycin, azithromycin, nitrofurantoin (avoid near term)

Antimicrobials avoid

Tetracyclines (teeth, bones); quinolones (cartilage); aminoglycosides (ototoxic); metronidazole (1st trimester caution)

Resource

UKTIS (UK Teratology Information Service); BNF; LactMed for breastfeeding; app-based decision support

Vaccination in pregnancy

Safe — inactivated influenza, COVID-19, pertussis (dTpa, 16-32 weeks). Avoid live: MMR, varicella, BCG, yellow fever (if needed) live OK

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Drug safety in pregnancy?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Drug safety in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Drug safety in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

D) A: safe; B: animal safe; C: risk can't be ruled out; D: evidence of risk; X: contraindicated (B3 UK)

topics.title: Ectopic Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Ectopic pregnancy is implantation of a fertilised ovum outside the uterine cavity, most commonly in the fallopian tube (95%). It is a leading cause of first-trimester maternal mortality due to rupture and haemorrhage. Early diagnosis using β-hCG and transvaginal ultrasound is essential.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Pregnancy implanted outside uterine cavity

Incidence

1-2% of pregnancies; rising with IVF, PID, smoking

Sites

Ampullary (70%); isthmic (12%); fimbrial (11%); interstitial (2-4%); cervical, ovarian, abdominal (rare)

Risk factors

Prior ectopic (10x); tubal damage (PID, surgery, endometriosis); IUD; smoking; IVF; tubal ligation

Presentation

Amenorrhoea 6-8 weeks; abdominal pain; vaginal bleeding; syncope; shoulder tip pain (diaphragmatic irritation); hypovolaemic shock if ruptured

Diagnosis

β-hCG (often <1000 and falling/rising abnormally); TVUS (no IUP + adnexal mass + free fluid); progesterone; diagnostic laparoscopy gold standard

Differential

Miscarriage; molar pregnancy; appendicitis; PID; ovarian torsion; corpus luteum cyst

Management unruptured

Methotrexate (50 mg/m² IM; stable; mass <3.5 cm; no fetal heart; β-hCG <5000); mifepristone + misoprostol

Surgical management

Salpingectomy (ruptured, unstable, large mass, fetal heart); salpingostomy (conservative; risk of persistent trophoblast)

Follow-up

Serial β-hCG until <5; counsel future fertility; recurrence 10%

Q1. What is the definition of Ectopic pregnancy?

Ectopic pregnancy is implantation of a fertilised ovum outside the uterine cavity, most commonly in the fallopian tube (95%). It is a leading cause of first-trimester maternal mortality due to rupture and haemorrhage. Early diagnosis using β-hCG and transvaginal ultrasound is essential.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Ectopic pregnancy?

Key features include: Definition, Incidence, Sites, Risk factors, Presentation. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Ectopic pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Ectopic pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Ectopic pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Recurrent Miscarriage
topics.html:
Definition

Recurrent miscarriage is defined as ≥3 consecutive pregnancy losses before 24 weeks' gestation (or ≥2 in some guidelines). It affects 1-2% of couples and warrants investigation for underlying causes. Identifiable causes include antiphospholipid syndrome, uterine anomalies, parental chromosomal abnormalities, and endocrine disorders.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

≥3 consecutive miscarriages <24 weeks (RCOG); some use ≥2

Incidence

1-2% of couples; 5% with ≥2 losses

Causes

Antiphospholipid syndrome (15%); uterine anomalies (10%); parental karyotype (3-5%); endocrine (PCOS, thyroid, diabetes); thrombophilia; infection; idiopathic (50%)

Investigations

Antiphospholipid antibodies (LA, aCL, anti-β2GP1); parental karyotyping; pelvic USS/MRI; thrombophilia screen; TFT; OGTT; uterine cavity (HSG/SIS)

APS treatment

Low-dose aspirin + LMWH from positive pregnancy test; improves live birth rate

Uterine anomalies

Septum resection (controversial); unicornuate/bicornuate — obstetric surveillance

Parental chromosomal

Robertsonian translocations; PGD/IVF; genetic counselling

Prognosis

70-80% live birth rate with supportive care; better if no identifiable cause

Supportive care

Tender loving care (TLC); early pregnancy unit access; psychological support

Future pregnancy

Folic acid 5 mg; aspirin if indicated; close monitoring; serial scans

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Recurrent miscarriage?
