topics.title: Spontaneous Miscarriage
topics.html:
Definition

Spontaneous miscarriage (spontaneous abortion) is the loss of pregnancy before 24 weeks' gestation. It affects 10-20% of confirmed pregnancies and presents with vaginal bleeding, abdominal pain, and passage of products of conception. Management is supportive, medical (misoprostol), or surgical (ERPC) according to clinical scenario and patient preference.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Pregnancy loss before 24 weeks' gestation (UK); 20 weeks (WHO)

Incidence

10-20% of confirmed pregnancies; 80% occur in 1st trimester

Types

Threatened; inevitable; incomplete; complete; missed; septic; recurrent (≥3)

Causes

Chromosomal (50%, esp. trisomies); uterine anomalies; endocrine; autoimmune; infection; thrombophilia; trauma; idiopathic

Risk factors

Advanced maternal age; prior miscarriage; smoking; alcohol; obesity; uterine anomalies

Presentation

Vaginal bleeding; lower abdominal pain; products of conception passed; cervical dilation

Investigations

β-hCG serial (doubles 48 h normally); TVUS (fetal heart from 5-6 weeks); blood group; FBC; coagulation if severe

Management

Expectant; medical (misoprostol 800 μg PV); surgical (ERPC/VAE) — patient choice; anti-D if Rh-

Complications

Haemorrhage; infection (septic miscarriage); DIC; psychological impact

Follow-up

Bereavement support; recurrent miscarriage clinic if ≥3; pre-conception counselling; folic acid

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Spontaneous miscarriage?

Spontaneous miscarriage (spontaneous abortion) is the loss of pregnancy before 24 weeks' gestation. It affects 10-20% of confirmed pregnancies and presents with vaginal bleeding, abdominal pain, and passage of products of conception. Management is supportive, medical (misoprostol), or surgical (ERPC) according to clinical scenario and patient preference.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Spontaneous miscarriage?

Key features include: Definition, Incidence, Types, Causes, Risk factors. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Spontaneous miscarriage diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Spontaneous miscarriage?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Spontaneous miscarriage?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

  • Mnemonic for Spontaneous miscarriage — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Termination Of Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Termination of pregnancy (induced abortion) is the medical or surgical ending of pregnancy. In the UK it is governed by the Abortion Act 1967 (with subsequent amendments). Most terminations are performed in the first trimester using medical (mifepristone + misoprostol) or surgical (suction curettage) methods. Complications are uncommon but include bleeding, infection, and uterine perforation.

Core Clinical Content

Legal (UK)

Abortion Act 1967: <24 weeks if risk to mother, child, or woman's health; any time if serious risk

First-trimester methods

Medical (mifepristone 200 mg oral then misoprostol 800 μg PV/buccal 24-48 h later) OR surgical (manual vacuum aspiration up to 12 weeks)

Second-trimester methods

Medical (mifepristone + repeated misoprostol); D&E (dilation and evacuation)

Feticide

For terminations >22 weeks; intracardiac potassium chloride or lidocaine

Complications

Haemorrhage (1%); infection (1-5%); retained products (1%); uterine perforation (<0.1%); Asherman syndrome (rare); psychological impact

Pre-procedure

Counselling; consent; USS for gestation; blood group + anti-D; STI screen; contraception plan

Post-procedure

Anti-D if Rh-; contraception initiated; β-hCG follow-up if medical; bleeding up to 2 weeks

Contraindications medical

Severe anaemia; coagulopathy; ectopic; adrenal/liver disease; long-term steroids; IUD in situ

Subsequent pregnancy

Usually no effect on fertility; small ↑preterm birth if multiple surgical; psychological support

Recurrent

Provide contraception; LARC methods; emergency contraception plan

topics.title: Adrenal Disease In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Adrenal disease in pregnancy is rare but includes Cushing's syndrome (often ACTH-independent, e.g. adrenal adenoma), primary hyperaldosteronism, Addison's disease, and congenital adrenal hyperplasia. Physiological cortisol rise makes diagnosis challenging; biochemical workup and imaging are key.

Core Clinical Content

Physiology

CBG rises; total cortisol rises 2-3x; free cortisol rises; ACTH normal to mildly elevated

Cushing's syndrome

Rare; usually adrenal adenoma; features overlap with normal pregnancy (HTN, striae, GDM)

Diagnosis

24-h urinary free cortisol (cut-off 3x upper limit); late-night salivary cortisol; MRI without gadolinium

Management Cushing's

Surgery (adrenalectomy) in 2nd trimester if feasible; medical — metyrapone preferred

Addison's disease

Primary adrenal insufficiency; glucocorticoid + mineralocorticoid replacement; labour dose 100 mg hydrocortisone IV

Addisonian crisis

Vomiting, hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia; IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat

Congenital adrenal hyperplasia

21-hydroxylase deficiency; dexamethasone crosses placenta; consider prenatal treatment for affected female fetus

Primary hyperaldosteronism

Rare; features overlap with normal pregnancy; treat with labetalol; surgical if Conn's adenoma

Phaeochromocytoma

Rare but dangerous; screen if refractory HTN or paroxysmal symptoms; α-blockade then β; deliver with MDT

Postpartum

Review replacement; may need dose reduction if pre-pregnancy dose high

Q1. What is the definition of Adrenal disease in pregnancy?

Adrenal disease in pregnancy is rare but includes Cushing's syndrome (often ACTH-independent, e.g. adrenal adenoma), primary hyperaldosteronism, Addison's disease, and congenital adrenal hyperplasia. Physiological cortisol rise makes diagnosis challenging; biochemical workup and imaging are key.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Adrenal disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Physiology, Cushing's syndrome, Diagnosis, Management Cushing's, Addison's disease. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Adrenal disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Adrenal disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Adrenal disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Diabetes Insipidus In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Diabetes insipidus (DI) in pregnancy may be pre-existing (central or nephrogenic) or develop de novo in pregnancy (gestational/transient DI of pregnancy). Pregnancy unmask subclinical DI due to rising vasopressinase from placenta. Distinguish from physiological polyuria of pregnancy.

Core Clinical Content

Forms

Central (pituitary); nephrogenic (kidney); gestational/transient (placental vasopressinase); pre-existing may worsen

Incidence

Pre-existing DI rare; gestational DI 1-6 per 100,000 pregnancies

Pathophysiology

Placental vasopressinase degrades ADH; unmasked in subclinical central DI; transient DI when placental mass high (twins, pre-eclampsia)

Presentation

Polyuria (>3 L/day); polydipsia; dehydration; hypernatraemia if no access to water

Investigations

Urine osmolality (<300); plasma osmolality (>287); sodium; water deprivation test (cautious in pregnancy); copeptin; serum vasopressin

Differential

Physiological polyuria; gestational diabetes (glycosuria); psychogenic polydipsia; hypercalcaemia; hypokalaemia

Treatment

Desmopressin (DDAVP) — safe, not degraded by vasopressinase; titrate dose; thiazide + low-salt for nephrogenic

Dose adjustment

Often need higher dose in pregnancy (vasopressinase); reassess postpartum

Monitoring

Sodium; fluid balance; weight; urine output; symptomatic review

Postpartum

Gestational DI resolves; pre-existing may revert to pre-pregnancy dose; breastfeeding may worsen (oxytocin)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Parathyroid Disease In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Parathyroid disease in pregnancy includes primary hyperparathyroidism (usually parathyroid adenoma) and hypoparathyroidism. Hyperparathyroidism in pregnancy is associated with miscarriage, pre-eclampsia, neonatal hypocalcaemia, and renal stones. Hypoparathyroidism is usually iatrogenic post-thyroidectomy.

Core Clinical Content

Primary hyperparathyroidism

Rare in pregnancy; most common cause: parathyroid adenoma (85%); associated with MEN1, MEN2A

Presentation

Often asymptomatic; stones, bones, groans, psychiatric overtones; nausea, vomiting, fatigue, depression

Risks to mother

Hyperemesis; renal stones; pancreatitis; pre-eclampsia; hypercalcaemic crisis; fractures

Risks to fetus/neonate

Miscarriage; IUGR; preterm birth; neonatal hypocalcaemia (suppressed parathyroids) — tetany, seizures

Diagnosis

Corrected calcium (albumin-adjusted or ionised); PTH; 24-h urinary calcium; vitamin D; phosphate; consider sestamibi scan/US if needed

Management

Hydration; oral phosphate; cinacalcet (limited data); parathyroidectomy in 2nd trimester if severe/refractory

Severe hypercalcaemia (>3.0)

IV fluids; bisphosphonates avoid; calcitonin; consider surgery; parathyroidectomy preferred

Hypoparathyroidism

Usually iatrogenic post-thyroidectomy or autoimmune; replacement with calcitriol + calcium

Risks of hypoparathyroidism

Hypocalcaemia → tetany, seizures, laryngospasm; long-QT; preterm labour

Postpartum

Monitor calcium; breastfed infants at risk of hypocalcaemia; re-evaluate replacement doses

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Parathyroid disease in pregnancy?

Parathyroid disease in pregnancy includes primary hyperparathyroidism (usually parathyroid adenoma) and hypoparathyroidism. Hyperparathyroidism in pregnancy is associated with miscarriage, pre-eclampsia, neonatal hypocalcaemia, and renal stones. Hypoparathyroidism is usually iatrogenic post-thyroidectomy.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Parathyroid disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Primary hyperparathyroidism, Presentation, Risks to mother, Risks to fetus/neonate, Diagnosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Parathyroid disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Parathyroid disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Parathyroid disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.html:
Definition

Pituitary disease in pregnancy includes prolactinoma (most common), hypopituitarism, lymphocytic hypophysitis, and Sheehan syndrome (postpartum pituitary infarction). Physiological lactotroph hyperplasia enlarges the pituitary, affecting imaging and symptoms.

Core Clinical Content

Physiology

Pituitary enlarges 30-50% in pregnancy; lactotrophs proliferate; other axes relatively suppressed

Prolactinoma

Most common; microadenomas (<10 mm) usually asymptomatic; macroprolactinomas need monitoring

Management prolactinoma

Dopamine agonists (cabergoline preferred) — discontinue in pregnancy if possible; restart if symptoms or growth

Visual fields

Monitor macroprolactinomas with monthly visual fields; MRI if symptoms

Lymphocytic hypophysitis

Peripartum; mimics pituitary mass with headache, visual loss, hypopituitarism; biopsy may be needed

Sheehan syndrome

Postpartum pituitary infarction following severe PPH; failure to lactate, amenorrhoea, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency

Diabetes insipidus

May worsen in pregnancy (vasopressinase from placenta); desmopressin safe

Hypopituitarism

Hydrocortisone + levothyroxine; growth hormone discontinued; close MDT monitoring

Cushing disease

Rare; surgery in 2nd trimester if confirmed

Postpartum

Reassess pituitary function 3-6 months; restart dopamine agonist if breastfeeding; MRI if macroadenoma

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Pituitary disease in pregnancy?

Pituitary disease in pregnancy includes prolactinoma (most common), hypopituitarism, lymphocytic hypophysitis, and Sheehan syndrome (postpartum pituitary infarction). Physiological lactotroph hyperplasia enlarges the pituitary, affecting imaging and symptoms.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Pituitary disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Physiology, Prolactinoma, Management prolactinoma, Visual fields, Lymphocytic hypophysitis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Pituitary disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Pituitary disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Pituitary disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Pituitary disease in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Postpartum Thyroiditis
topics.html:
Definition

Postpartum thyroiditis is an autoimmune thyroiditis occurring within 12 months of delivery, often presenting in a biphasic pattern of hyperthyroidism followed by hypothyroidism. It affects 5-10% of postpartum women and is associated with anti-TPO antibodies. Most women return to euthyroid but a proportion develop permanent hypothyroidism.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Autoimmune thyroiditis within 12 months postpartum

Incidence

5-10% of postpartum women; up to 25% if TPOAb+

Risk factors

Anti-TPO antibodies (strongest); prior postpartum thyroiditis; type 1 DM; other autoimmune disease (SLE); family history

Phases

Biphasic: hyperthyroid (1-4 months postpartum) → euthyroid → hypothyroid (4-8 months) → recovery; 20-30% have isolated hypothyroid

Hyperthyroid phase

Symptoms: fatigue, palpitations, weight loss, anxiety; thyrotoxic but no ophthalmopathy; due to thyroid hormone release from inflammation

Hypothyroid phase

Symptoms: fatigue, weight gain, depression, constipation, dry skin, hair loss; depression may be misattributed to PND

Diagnosis

TSH, free T4, free T3; TPO antibodies; thyroid USS; low uptake on radioisotope scan (avoid in breastfeeding if possible)

Differential

Graves' disease (ophthalmopathy, persistently high uptake); PND; anaemia

Management hyperthyroid

β-blocker (propranolol); no antithyroid drugs (destructive, not synthetic); usually self-limiting

Management hypothyroid

Levothyroxine if symptomatic or TSH >10; weaning trial 6-12 months; monitor

Long-term

20-30% permanent hypothyroidism; recurrence in 70% subsequent pregnancies; screen if symptomatic

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

  9. β-blocker (propranolol); no antithyroid drugs (destructive, not synthetic); usually self-limiting
  10. Levothyroxine if symptomatic or TSH >10; weaning trial 6-12 months; monitor

topics.title: Thyroid Disease In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Thyroid disease in pregnancy is common and includes overt hypothyroidism, subclinical hypothyroidism, isolated hypothyroxinaemia, hyperthyroidism (usually Graves'), and postpartum thyroiditis. Both maternal hypo- and hyperthyroidism adversely affect pregnancy and fetal development.

Core Clinical Content

Physiology

TBG rises (oestrogen); total T4/T3 rise; free T4 unchanged; TSH falls in 1st trimester (hCG)

Trimester-specific ranges

TSH: 1st 0.1-2.5; 2nd 0.2-3.0; 3rd 0.3-3.0 mIU/L (population-specific)

Hypothyroidism

Prevalence 1-2%; levothyroxine dose often needs increase 30-50% in 1st trimester; target TSH <2.5

Hashimoto's

Most common cause; anti-TPO antibodies; monitor after delivery

Hyperthyroidism

Prevalence 0.1-0.4%; 90% Graves' disease; risks: pre-eclampsia, IUGR, preterm, stillbirth, fetal thyrotoxicosis

Treatment hyperthyroidism

Propylthiouracil (1st trimester) then switch to carbimazole; avoid methimazole in 1st trimester; target FT4 in upper normal range

Side effects

PTU: hepatotoxicity; carbimazole: aplasia cutis, choanal atresia (1st trimester)

Fetal monitoring

Fetal heart rate (>160 — thyrotoxicosis); growth; goitre on US

Postpartum thyroiditis

Affects 5-10%; hyper then hypo phase; anti-TPO; screen at 6 weeks if high risk

Postpartum care

Reassess thyroid function 6 weeks postpartum; reduce levothyroxine to pre-pregnancy dose

Q1. What is the definition of Thyroid disease in pregnancy?

Thyroid disease in pregnancy is common and includes overt hypothyroidism, subclinical hypothyroidism, isolated hypothyroxinaemia, hyperthyroidism (usually Graves'), and postpartum thyroiditis. Both maternal hypo- and hyperthyroidism adversely affect pregnancy and fetal development.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Thyroid disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Physiology, Trimester-specific ranges, Hypothyroidism, Hashimoto's, Hyperthyroidism. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Thyroid disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Thyroid disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Thyroid disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Antiphospholipid Syndrome In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) in pregnancy is associated with recurrent miscarriage, fetal loss, pre-eclampsia, IUGR, and thrombosis. Diagnosis requires persistent antiphospholipid antibodies plus clinical features. Treatment with aspirin ± LMWH improves pregnancy outcomes.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Persistent aPL antibodies + clinical event (thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity)

Antibodies

Lupus anticoagulant (strongest); anticardiolipin; anti-β2 glycoprotein I

Obstetric morbidity

≥3 consecutive <10-week losses; ≥1 ≥10-week loss; preterm <34 weeks with PE/placental insufficiency

Thrombotic risk

Arterial (stroke, MI) and venous (DVT, PE); pregnancy adds risk

Catastrophic APS

Rapid multi-organ thrombosis; mortality ~30%; intensive care; triple therapy

Preconception

Confirm antibodies 12 weeks apart; counsel; plan aspirin + LMWH

Treatment

Aspirin 75-150 mg daily + prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy; therapeutic LMWH if prior VTE

Monitoring

Uterine artery Doppler at 20-24 weeks; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance

Postpartum

Continue LMWH 6 weeks; lifelong anticoagulation if thrombosis

Prognosis

Live birth 70-80% with treatment; recurrence 5-10%

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

A: Confirm antibodies 12 weeks apart; counsel; plan aspirin + LMWH

Q: Treatment?

A: Aspirin 75-150 mg daily + prophylactic LMWH throughout pregnancy; therapeutic LMWH if prior VTE

Q: Monitoring?

A: Uterine artery Doppler at 20-24 weeks; growth scans; pre-eclampsia surveillance

  2. Lupus anticoagulant (strongest); anticardiolipin; anti-β2 glycoprotein I
  3. ≥3 consecutive <10-week losses; ≥1 ≥10-week loss; preterm <34 weeks with PE/placental insufficiency
  4. Arterial (stroke, MI) and venous (DVT, PE); pregnancy adds risk
  5. Rapid multi-organ thrombosis; mortality ~30%; intensive care; triple therapy

  9. Continue LMWH 6 weeks; lifelong anticoagulation if thrombosis
  10. Live birth 70-80% with treatment; recurrence 5-10%

topics.title: Iron Deficiency Anaemia In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy is the most common anaemia in pregnancy worldwide and a major cause of maternal morbidity, preterm birth, and low birth weight. Diagnosis uses trimester-specific cut-offs; treatment combines oral iron with parenteral iron when needed.

Core Clinical Content

Definitions

1st trimester Hb <110; 2nd/3rd <105 g/L; postpartum <100 g/L (WHO)

Prevalence

20-40% of pregnancies; 50%+ in LMIC

Causes

Iron deficiency (90%); folate/B12 deficiency; haemoglobinopathies; haemolysis

Investigations

FBC; MCV (microcytic); ferritin (<30 μg/L = depleted); serum iron; TIBC

Maternal impact

Fatigue, breathlessness; pre-eclampsia; postpartum haemorrhage; infection; transfusion need

Fetal impact

IUGR; preterm birth; low birth weight; poor neurodevelopment

Oral iron

Ferrous sulphate 200 mg BD-TDS; alternate-day dosing improves absorption; take with vitamin C

IV iron

Iron sucrose, ferric carboxymaltose, iron isomaltoside — for intolerance/non-response/severe

Blood transfusion

Reserved for severe symptomatic anaemia near term; aim Hb >70-80 g/L

Prevention

Routine oral iron from 16 weeks; high-risk women from 1st trimester

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy is the most common anaemia in pregnancy worldwide and a major cause of maternal morbidity, preterm birth, and low birth weight. Diagnosis uses trimester-specific cut-offs; treatment combines oral iron with parenteral iron when needed.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy?

Q3. How is Iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy diagnosed?

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

topics.title: Myeloproliferative Disorders In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Myeloproliferative neoplasms (MPN) — essential thrombocythaemia (ET), polycythaemia vera (PV), and primary myelofibrosis — are clonal haematopoietic disorders. In pregnancy they are associated with increased risk of thrombosis, haemorrhage, miscarriage, IUGR, stillbirth, and pre-eclampsia. Specialist MDT care is essential.

Core Clinical Content

Epidemiology

ET most common MPN in pregnancy; median age 30-40; affects women in childbearing years

ET pathophysiology

JAK2 V617F (50%); CALR (25%); MPL (5%); triple negative (20%); platelets >450

PV pathophysiology

JAK2 V617F in 95%; raised Hb/Hct; EPO low

Complications pregnancy

Arterial and venous thrombosis (DVT, PE, stroke, MI); haemorrhage; miscarriage; IUGR; stillbirth; pre-eclampsia; placental abruption; preterm

Preconception

MDT (haematologist, obstetrician); optimise counts; switch teratogens (hydroxyurea → IFN-α); aspirin; review

Treatment

Aspirin 75-150 mg; LMWH (prophylactic or therapeutic depending on risk); cytoreduction if counts very high (IFN-α preferred); venesection for PV; avoid hydroxyurea; avoid anagrelide 1st trimester

Monitoring

FBC 2-4 weekly; U&E; LFT; uric acid; growth scans; uterine artery Doppler; MCA Doppler if needed

Delivery

Planned; discontinue LMWH 12-24 h before if regional anaesthesia; consider platelet count if needed

Postpartum

Continue LMWH 6 weeks; aspirin; breastfeeding compatible with IFN-α; resume prior therapy

Counselling

Recurrence risk; consider PGD if heritable component; multidisciplinary follow-up lifelong

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Myeloproliferative disorders in pregnancy?

Key features include: Epidemiology, ET pathophysiology, PV pathophysiology, Complications pregnancy, Preconception. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Myeloproliferative disorders in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Myeloproliferative disorders in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Myeloproliferative disorders in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Q: PV pathophysiology?

A: JAK2 V617F in 95%; raised Hb/Hct; EPO low

Q: Complications pregnancy?

Q: Delivery?

A: Planned; discontinue LMWH 12-24 h before if regional anaesthesia; consider platelet count if needed

topics.title: Sickle Cell Disease In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Sickle cell disease in pregnancy is associated with increased maternal and fetal complications — painful crises, infections, pre-eclampsia, IUGR, and preterm birth. Multidisciplinary care in a specialist centre with prophylactic transfusion, exchange transfusion for crises, and intensive monitoring is essential.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

Highest in sub-Saharan Africa, Caribbean, Middle East; 1 in 2,000-10,000 pregnancies

Maternal risks

Painful crises; infection (UTI, pneumonia, sepsis); pre-eclampsia; anaemia; acute chest syndrome; VTE; death

Fetal risks

Miscarriage; IUGR; preterm birth; stillbirth (HbF/APCR helps)

Preconception

Genetic counselling; partner screening; medication review; hydroxyurea stop 3 months before

Antenatal care

MDT (haematologist, obstetrician); folic acid 5 mg; penicillin V prophylaxis; transfusion programme debated

Transfusion

Prophylactic top-up transfusion if Hb <70-80 or symptoms; exchange transfusion for severe complications

Monitoring

Monthly FBC, U&E, LFT; growth scans; uterine artery Doppler; MCA Doppler

Acute crisis

Analgesia (parenteral opioids); oxygen; hydration; antibiotics; transfusion; exclude other causes

Labour and delivery

Vaginal preferred; avoid dehydration, hypoxia, cold; continuous CTG

Postpartum

VTE prophylaxis; close monitoring for crises; contraception counselling; avoid oestrogen

Sickle cell disease in pregnancy is associated with increased maternal and fetal complications — painful crises, infections, pre-eclampsia, IUGR, and preterm birth. Multidisciplinary care in a specialist centre with prophylactic transfusion, exchange transfusion for crises, and intensive monitoring is essential.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Sickle cell disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Maternal risks, Fetal risks, Preconception, Antenatal care. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Sickle cell disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Sickle cell disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Sickle cell disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Thalassaemia In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Thalassaemia in pregnancy — alpha and beta — are inherited disorders of haemoglobin synthesis common in Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, South Asian, and South-East Asian populations. Maternal complications include anaemia, transfusion requirements, iron overload, and thromboembolism; severe fetal forms (α-thalassaemia major, β-thalassaemia major) cause hydrops fetalis or lifelong transfusion dependence.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Inherited disorders of globin chain synthesis; α (4 genes) and β (2 genes)

α-thalassaemia

Silent carrier (1 gene); trait (2); HbH (3) — moderate anaemia; Hb Barts (4) — hydrops fetalis, lethal

β-thalassaemia

Trait (carrier) — mild anaemia; intermedia — moderate; major — transfusion dependent; chelation

Screening

MCV <80; ferritin normal; Hb electrophoresis/HPLC; partner testing if MCV low; cascade screening in families

Preconception

Genetic counselling; partner testing; PGD if both carriers; optimise transfusion/chelation

Antenatal care

MDT (haematologist, obstetrician); serial Hb; transfuse if <80 g/L or symptomatic; folic acid; iron if deficient (cautious); monitor chelation (deferoxamine safe; deferasirox avoid)

Fetal monitoring

Anomaly scan; MCA Doppler for fetal anaemia; consideration of intrauterine transfusion if hydrops

Risks maternal

Anaemia; transfusion; thromboembolism; alloimmunisation; iron overload; cardiac complications

Risks fetal/neonatal

IUGR; preterm birth; hydrops (α-major); congenital anomalies if transfused in 1st trimester

Delivery

Vaginal preferred; assisted second stage if cardiomyopathy; avoid ergometrine; active management

Postpartum

VTE prophylaxis; contraception (avoid COC if iron overload/high thrombosis risk); transfusion plan

Key features include: Definition, α-thalassaemia, β-thalassaemia, Screening, Preconception. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Thalassaemia in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Thrombocytopenia In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Thrombocytopenia in pregnancy affects 6-10% of pregnancies. Gestational thrombocytopenia is the most common cause, but pre-eclampsia, HELLP, ITP, TTP, and HUS require urgent differentiation. Severe thrombocytopenia (<50 × 10^9/L) increases bleeding risk and affects delivery mode.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

6-10% of pregnancies; 1% have platelets <100 × 10^9/L

Causes (mnemonic)

GESTATIONAL — Gestational, Eclampsia, Sepsis, TTP, HELLP, AFLP, ITP, DIC, Neonatal alloimmune, APLS, Malignant marrow

Gestational

Mild (>70); 2nd/3rd trimester; no bleeding; resolves postpartum

ITP

Autoimmune; platelets <100; 1st/2nd trimester onset; bleeding tendency; steroids/IVIG for <30

TTP

Pentad — MAHA, thrombocytopenia, neuro, renal, fever; ADAMTS13 <10%; plasma exchange; mortality 90% untreated

Pre-eclampsia/HELLP

Severe features; LFT deranged; coagulopathy in HELLP

Neonatal alloimmune

Mother makes antibodies against fetal platelets; risk ICH; treat with IVIG

Investigation

FBC, blood film, LFT, LDH, haptoglobin, coagulation, autoantibodies, viral screen

Management

Treat cause; ITP — steroids/IVIG for <30 or bleeding; platelet transfusion only for surgery/bleeding

Delivery threshold

Vaginal safe >50; C-section >50; epidural/anaesthesia requires >70-80

Postpartum

ITP may flare; monitor; neonatal platelet count in ITP

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q1. What is the definition of Thrombocytopenia in pregnancy?

Thrombocytopenia in pregnancy affects 6-10% of pregnancies. Gestational thrombocytopenia is the most common cause, but pre-eclampsia, HELLP, ITP, TTP, and HUS require urgent differentiation. Severe thrombocytopenia (<50 × 10^9/L) increases bleeding risk and affects delivery mode.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Thrombocytopenia in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Causes (mnemonic), Gestational, ITP, TTP. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Thrombocytopenia in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Thrombocytopenia in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Thrombocytopenia in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Venous Thromboembolism In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Venous thromboembolism (VTE) — deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism — is a leading direct cause of maternal mortality. Pregnancy increases VTE risk 4-5 fold due to venous stasis, hypercoagulability, and vascular damage. Prompt investigation and treatment with LMWH are essential.

Core Clinical Content

Risk

5-6x non-pregnant; 15-35x postpartum (first 6 weeks)

Sites

DVT: 80% left leg; iliofemoral > calf in pregnancy; PE: postpartum > antenatal

Risk factors

Prior VTE (strongest); obesity; age >35; thrombophilia; APS; caesarean; pre-eclampsia; multiple pregnancy; immobility; smoking

Clinical features DVT

Unilateral leg swelling/pain; tenderness along venous tract; pitting oedema; low-grade fever

Clinical features PE

Pleuritic chest pain; dyspnoea; tachycardia; haemoptysis; syncope; hypoxia

Diagnosis DVT

Compression duplex ultrasound (no radiation); iliac vein imaging if negative

Diagnosis PE

CXR; V/Q scan or CTPA (low fetal radiation); bilateral leg Doppler; D-dimer not useful

Treatment

LMWH (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg BD); warfarin teratogenic; DOACs contraindicated; UFH if haemodynamic instability or delivery imminent

Duration

Minimum 3 months and until 6 weeks postpartum

Prevention

Risk assessment at booking and delivery; LMWH prophylaxis for intermediate/high risk; compression stockings; early mobilisation

Q3. How is Venous thromboembolism in pregnancy diagnosed?

topics.title: Vitamin B12 And Folate Deficiency In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy cause megaloblastic anaemia and are associated with neural tube defects (folate), preterm birth, and neurodevelopmental delay (B12). Preconception folate supplementation reduces NTD risk. B12 deficiency is uncommon but important in vegan/vegetarian mothers, after bariatric surgery, or with pernicious anaemia.

Core Clinical Content

Folate requirements

Increased 5-10x in pregnancy; deficiency common if poor diet, malabsorption, anticonvulsants

Folate functions

DNA synthesis; cell division; NTD prevention; placental growth

B12 functions

Myelin synthesis; DNA synthesis; neurological function

Causes folate deficiency

Poor diet; multiple pregnancy; haemolysis; malabsorption; drugs (anticonvulsants, methotrexate, trimethoprim)

Causes B12 deficiency

Vegan/vegetarian diet; pernicious anaemia; post-bariatric; ileal disease (Crohn's); metformin

Folate deficiency effects

Megaloblastic anaemia; NTD (anencephaly, spina bifida); cleft lip/palate; cardiac defects; placental abruption; recurrent miscarriage

B12 deficiency effects

Megaloblastic anaemia; neurological (paresthesia, ataxia, dementia); infertility; preterm; low birth weight

Investigations

FBC (MCV >100); blood film (macroovalocytes, hypersegmented neutrophils); serum folate; B12; MMA; homocysteine; anti-IF antibody

Supplementation

Folic acid 400 μg daily preconception to 12 weeks; 5 mg if high risk (prior NTD, diabetes, antiepileptics, BMI >30, sickle); B12 IM for severe deficiency; oral if dietary

Postpartum

Continue supplementation if breastfeeding; assess mother's B12/folate if breastfed infant symptomatic

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy?

Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy cause megaloblastic anaemia and are associated with neural tube defects (folate), preterm birth, and neurodevelopmental delay (B12). Preconception folate supplementation reduces NTD risk. B12 deficiency is uncommon but important in vegan/vegetarian mothers, after bariatric surgery, or with pernicious anaemia.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy?

Key features include: Folate requirements, Folate functions, B12 functions, Causes folate deficiency, Causes B12 deficiency. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Aortic Disease In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Aortic disease in pregnancy, including aortic aneurysm, aortic dissection, and conditions predisposing to dissection (Marfan, Loeys-Dietz, vascular Ehlers-Danlos, bicuspid aortic valve, coarctation), carries significant mortality. Pregnancy increases aortic wall stress due to hyperdynamic circulation and hormonal effects on connective tissue.

Core Clinical Content

Aortic dissection

1 per 10,000 pregnancies; 50% occur in 3rd trimester or postpartum; type A 2/3

Risk factors

Marfan syndrome (5-10% risk); bicuspid aortic valve; coarctation; HTN; cocaine; trauma; vascular Ehlers-Danlos

Marfan in pregnancy

Aortic root >45 mm — pregnancy contraindicated; 40-45 mm — individualised; <40 mm — generally safe with β-blocker

Presentation dissection

Severe tearing chest/back pain; pulse deficits; BP differential; aortic regurgitation murmur; shock; neurological signs

Diagnosis

CT aortogram (gold standard); TTE/TEE; MRI; ECG (LVH, ischaemia); D-dimer may be raised

Management dissection

Type A: emergency surgery (Caesarean if viable fetus first); Type B: medical (β-blocker, analgesia) ± TEVAR

Medical management

β-blocker (labetalol, metoprolol) to reduce dP/dt; target HR <70; systolic BP 100-120 mmHg; analgesia

Surgical

Aortic root replacement; valve-sparing; Caesarean at same time if needed

Preconception

Aortic root imaging; replace if >45 mm; genetic counselling; switch teratogens; β-blocker preconception

Delivery

MDT; epidural (reduces stress response); assisted second stage; avoid ergometrine; consider C-section in Marfan >40 mm

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point

Risk factorsMarfan syndrome (5-10% risk); bicuspid aortic valve; coarctation; HTN; cocaine; trauma; vascular Ehlers-Danlos

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Aortic disease in pregnancy?

Aortic disease in pregnancy, including aortic aneurysm, aortic dissection, and conditions predisposing to dissection (Marfan, Loeys-Dietz, vascular Ehlers-Danlos, bicuspid aortic valve, coarctation), carries significant mortality. Pregnancy increases aortic wall stress due to hyperdynamic circulation and hormonal effects on connective tissue.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Aortic disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Aortic dissection, Risk factors, Marfan in pregnancy, Presentation dissection, Diagnosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Aortic disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Aortic disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Aortic disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Arrhythmias In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Arrhythmias in pregnancy include atrial and ventricular ectopics, supraventricular tachycardia (SVT), atrial fibrillation/flutter, and ventricular tachycardia. They are usually benign but may indicate underlying heart disease, thyroid dysfunction, or electrolyte disturbance. Treatment is similar to non-pregnant with attention to fetal safety of drugs.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

Palpitations common (50%); AF rare (2/1000) — usually with structural heart disease or hyperthyroidism

Causes

Structural heart disease; hyperthyroidism; electrolyte disturbance; stimulants; caffeine; alcohol; drug use; pre-excitation (WPW); congenital arrhythmia syndromes

SVT

Most common sustained arrhythmia in pregnancy; vagal manoeuvres first; adenosine if fails (safe in pregnancy); β-blocker; verapamil; DC cardioversion if unstable

Atrial fibrillation/flutter

Often with structural heart disease (MS, congenital); anticoagulate (LMWH); rate control (β-blocker, digoxin); cardioversion if unstable

Ventricular tachycardia

Rare; assess for structural disease and channelopathy (Long QT, Brugada, CPVT); DC cardioversion if unstable; ICD if recurrent; avoid QT-prolonging drugs

Bradyarrhythmias

Rare; consider heart block (congenital or SLE); pacing if symptomatic; complete heart block often needs permanent pacing

Drug safety

Adenosine, β-blockers (labetalol, metoprolol), verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin, lidocaine — generally safe; amiodarone avoid (thyroid, fetal); flecainide safe; sotalol safe

Investigation

ECG; 24-h Holter; echo; TFT; electrolytes; consider EP study postpartum

Anticoagulation

Score and consider LMWH if AF >48 h or high risk; cardioversion with TOE if duration unclear

Delivery planning

Avoid Valsalva; consider assisted second stage; consider epidural; continuous CTG; postpartum monitoring

Q: SVT?

A: Most common sustained arrhythmia in pregnancy; vagal manoeuvres first; adenosine if fails (safe in pregnancy); β-blocker; verapamil; DC cardioversion if unstable

Q: Atrial fibrillation/flutter?

A: Often with structural heart disease (MS, congenital); anticoagulate (LMWH); rate control (β-blocker, digoxin); cardioversion if unstable

Q: Ventricular tachycardia?

A: Rare; assess for structural disease and channelopathy (Long QT, Brugada, CPVT); DC cardioversion if unstable; ICD if recurrent; avoid QT-prolonging drugs

Q: Bradyarrhythmias?

A: Rare; consider heart block (congenital or SLE); pacing if symptomatic; complete heart block often needs permanent pacing

Q: Drug safety?

A: Adenosine, β-blockers (labetalol, metoprolol), verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin, lidocaine — generally safe; amiodarone avoid (thyroid, fetal); flecainide safe; sotalol safe

Q: Investigation?

A: ECG; 24-h Holter; echo; TFT; electrolytes; consider EP study postpartum

  3. Most common sustained arrhythmia in pregnancy; vagal manoeuvres first; adenosine if fails (safe in pregnancy); β-blocker; verapamil; DC cardioversion if unstable
  4. Often with structural heart disease (MS, congenital); anticoagulate (LMWH); rate control (β-blocker, digoxin); cardioversion if unstable

  6. Rare; consider heart block (congenital or SLE); pacing if symptomatic; complete heart block often needs permanent pacing
  7. Adenosine, β-blockers (labetalol, metoprolol), verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin, lidocaine — generally safe; amiodarone avoid (thyroid, fetal); flecainide safe; sotalol safe

topics.title: Ischaemic Heart Disease In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Ischaemic heart disease in pregnancy — including stable angina, acute coronary syndrome (ACS), and myocardial infarction — is rare but increasing due to advanced maternal age, obesity, and pre-existing risk factors. Pregnancy is hypercoagulable and hyperdynamic, increasing cardiovascular risk. Presentation may be atypical and is often missed.

Core Clinical Content

Incidence

MI 1-3 per 100,000 deliveries; rising; leading non-obstetric cause of maternal death in some series

Risk factors

Advanced maternal age; obesity; smoking; hypertension; diabetes; hyperlipidaemia; family history; APS; previous MI

Mechanism

Atherosclerosis (50%); coronary dissection (P-SCAD; 30% — especially postpartum); thrombosis (coronary spasm); embolism

Presentation

Chest pain (atypical — epigastric, jaw, back); dyspnoea; diaphoresis; nausea; fatigue; collapse

Diagnosis

ECG (ST changes; may be hard to interpret in pregnancy); troponin (valid); echo (regional wall motion abnormalities); CT coronary angiography (low dose)

Management ACS

Aspirin 300 mg; clopidogrel; LMWH; β-blocker; nitrates; oxygen; PCI if STEMI (preferred over thrombolysis); radial approach preferred

Drugs to avoid

Statins (teratogenic — stop at pregnancy diagnosis); ACEI/ARB; warfarin in 1st trimester; glycoprotein IIb/IIIa — limited data

PCI

Bare metal stent preferred (avoid drug-eluting for 1st trimester); dual antiplatelet; LMWH cover for delivery

Delivery planning

MDT; vaginal preferred (avoid tachycardia, hypotension); epidural; assisted second stage; avoid ergometrine (causes vasospasm)

Prognosis

Maternal mortality 5-10% (higher if peripartum or anterior MI); recurrence 15-30% in subsequent pregnancies

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Ischaemic heart disease in pregnancy?

Ischaemic heart disease in pregnancy — including stable angina, acute coronary syndrome (ACS), and myocardial infarction — is rare but increasing due to advanced maternal age, obesity, and pre-existing risk factors. Pregnancy is hypercoagulable and hyperdynamic, increasing cardiovascular risk. Presentation may be atypical and is often missed.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Ischaemic heart disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Incidence, Risk factors, Mechanism, Presentation, Diagnosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Ischaemic heart disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Ischaemic heart disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Ischaemic heart disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

topics.title: Peripartum Cardiomyopathy
topics.html:
Definition

Peripartum cardiomyopathy is an idiopathic cardiomyopathy presenting with heart failure in the last month of pregnancy or within 5 months postpartum, in the absence of other identifiable causes. Diagnosis requires LV systolic dysfunction (LVEF <45%).

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Idiopathic cardiomyopathy with LVEF <45% presenting last month of pregnancy or within 5 months postpartum

Epidemiology

1 in 1,000-4,000 births; higher in Black women, age >30, multiparity, pre-eclampsia

Pathophysiology

Likely cleaved prolactin, sFlt-1, vasculopathy, viral, autoimmune

Presentation

Dyspnoea, orthopnoea, fatigue, oedema, palpitations, chest pain — often misdiagnosed as 'normal pregnancy'

Diagnosis

Echo (LVEF <45%); ECG; BNP/NT-proBNP; exclude other causes (TTE, CMR)

Differential

Pre-existing dilated cardiomyopathy; pre-eclampsia/HELLP with cardiac involvement; PE; sepsis

Acute management

Oxygen, diuretics (furosemide), vasodilators; ACEI/ARB/ARNI postpartum; beta-blocker; bromocriptine 2.5 mg BD 2 weeks (reduces mortality)

Anticoagulation

LMWH if LVEF <35% or AF

Subsequent pregnancy

Contraindicated if LVEF <50%; if LVEF recovers to >50% — close monitoring

Prognosis

~50-70% recover LV function by 6 months; 10-25% deteriorate; 5-year mortality 5-25%

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Peripartum cardiomyopathy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Peripartum cardiomyopathy?

D) LMWH if LVEF <35% or AF

topics.title: Pre Existing Heart Disease In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Pre-existing heart disease affects 1-4% of pregnancies and is a leading cause of maternal mortality. Risk assessment (modified WHO classification) and multidisciplinary care in a specialist centre are essential. Pregnancy is generally contraindicated in WHO class IV conditions.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence

1-4% of pregnancies; cardiac disease is leading non-obstetric cause of maternal death

Modified WHO classification

I: small risk; II: small-moderate; III: significant (specialist care); IV: pregnancy contraindicated

WHO IV (contraindicated)

Severe systemic ventricular dysfunction (EF <30%); severe MS/AS; previous PPCM with residual EF <50%; cyanotic CHD; Marfan with aortic root >45 mm

Common conditions

Congenital (ASD, VSD, ToF, Marfan, bicuspid aortic valve); acquired (rheumatic, MVP, ischaemic); cardiomyopathy (PPCMP)

Preconception

Echo, ECG, medication review; switch ACEI/warfarin; genetic counselling if inherited

Antenatal care

Multidisciplinary team; review every 4-6 weeks; serial BNP/echo; anaemia prevention

Labour and delivery

Vaginal usually preferred; assisted second stage; avoid supine position; early epidural

Antibiotic prophylaxis

For delivery in women with prosthetic valves or unrepaired cyanotic CHD

VTE prophylaxis

LMWH if class III/IV; immobility; mechanical valves

Postpartum

Haemodynamic monitoring 24-48 h; warfarin can be restarted; breastfeeding compatible with most cardiac drugs

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Pre-existing heart disease in pregnancy?

Pre-existing heart disease affects 1-4% of pregnancies and is a leading cause of maternal mortality. Risk assessment (modified WHO classification) and multidisciplinary care in a specialist centre are essential. Pregnancy is generally contraindicated in WHO class IV conditions.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Pre-existing heart disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Prevalence, Modified WHO classification, WHO IV (contraindicated), Common conditions, Preconception. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Pre-existing heart disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Pre-existing heart disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Pre-existing heart disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Pre-existing heart disease in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content

  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)

topics.title: Pulmonary Hypertension In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Pulmonary hypertension (PH) in pregnancy is a high-risk condition associated with up to 30-50% maternal mortality. Pregnancy is generally contraindicated in severe PH (WHO class IV). Multidisciplinary care in a specialist PH centre is essential if pregnancy continues.

Core Clinical Content

Definition

Mean pulmonary artery pressure >20 mmHg at rest (revised); previously ≥25 mmHg

Classification

Group 1 pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH); Group 2 (left heart); Group 3 (lung); Group 4 (CTEPH); Group 5 (misc)

Maternal mortality

30-50% without therapy; reduces to ~10-30% with modern PH care

Pathophysiology

Fixed PVR + pregnancy-related ↑CO + ↓SVR → RV failure; peripartum particularly dangerous

Symptoms

Progressive dyspnoea, fatigue, syncope, chest pain, RV failure signs

Investigations

Echo (PASP, RV function); RHC for definitive diagnosis; V/Q scan if CTEPH suspected

Preconception

Counsel about mortality; offer termination; switch endothelin receptor antagonists (teratogenic)

Therapy

Prostacyclins (epoprostenol, iloprost); sildenafil; LMWH; planned delivery under MDT

Labour and delivery

Avoid hypotension; assisted second stage; avoid general anaesthesia; central line; pulmonary vasodilators continued

Postpartum

24-48 h ITU monitoring; PH team input; VTE prophylaxis; avoid pregnancy for 1 year minimum

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Valvular Heart Disease In Pregnancy
topics.html:
Definition

Valvular heart disease — most commonly rheumatic mitral stenosis in developing countries and congenital bicuspid aortic valve/stenosis in developed countries — is a leading cause of maternal morbidity. Stenotic lesions are higher risk than regurgitant lesions because the fixed output cannot accommodate pregnancy demands.

Core Clinical Content

Mitral stenosis

Most common rheumatic lesion; pre-conception balloon valvuloplasty if severe (MVA <1.5 cm²)

Symptoms MS

Dyspnoea, orthopnoea, PND, haemoptysis, AF — often worsen in 2nd/3rd trimesters

Aortic stenosis

Bicuspid valve; symptoms (angina, syncope) may develop as CO fails to rise

Mitral/aortic regurgitation

Lower risk — reduced SVR is protective; treat medically

Anticoagulation

AF, mechanical valve, prior thrombus — LMWH throughout pregnancy; switch warfarin at 6-12 weeks

Mechanical valve

High risk: 1-2% maternal mortality; valve thrombosis; LMWH requires anti-Xa monitoring; warfarin 5 mg max preferred for mechanical AV after 12 weeks

Antibiotic prophylaxis

Not routinely for delivery; per current guidelines

Labour and delivery

Vaginal preferred; haemodynamic monitoring; assisted second stage; avoid tachycardia/hypotension

Percutaneous valvotomy

Option for severe MS failing medical therapy; experienced centre

Postpartum

VTE prophylaxis; close follow-up; valve surgery deferred if possible until after delivery

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Valvular heart disease in pregnancy?

Valvular heart disease — most commonly rheumatic mitral stenosis in developing countries and congenital bicuspid aortic valve/stenosis in developed countries — is a leading cause of maternal morbidity. Stenotic lesions are higher risk than regurgitant lesions because the fixed output cannot accommodate pregnancy demands.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Valvular heart disease in pregnancy?

Key features include: Mitral stenosis, Symptoms MS, Aortic stenosis, Mitral/aortic regurgitation, Anticoagulation. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Valvular heart disease in pregnancy diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features and specific investigations. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Valvular heart disease in pregnancy?

First-line management typically includes pregnancy-safe pharmacological and supportive interventions, tailored to severity and trimester. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the maternal and fetal complications of Valvular heart disease in pregnancy?

Complications depend on disease severity, gestational age, and management. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content. Early recognition and specialist MDT care are essential.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Valvular heart disease in pregnancy — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'PRE-ECLAMPSIA' risk factors — Previous PE, Renal disease, Extremes of age, Chronic HTN, Ethnicity, Antiphospholipid, nulliparity, Multiple, Obesity, Smoking
  • 'Mnemonic: H-E-L-L-P' — Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
  • 'CHAP' trial target — Chronic HTN treated to <140/90 mmHg
  • 'M-M-R-V' safe vaccines in pregnancy — Measles-Mumps-Rubella (MMR, avoid), inactivated influenza, COVID-19, Whooping cough (dTpa)
